title: Worthless Toys Deceptively Marketed
body: A couple of years ago, when Amazon announced it’s Prime Family Sharing, I added my father as the additional adult to the Prime Household. I’d like to mention that nowhere in the description of Prime Family Sharing does it indicate in any way, shape or form that the additional member needs to be at the same physical residence. It simply says “Any Adult” and goes on to explain the possible financial consequences that might occur since that other adult would have access to your credit card information and could place orders using it. He lives in Florida, I live in Rhode Island.At some point my father decided to purchase some Dots in case my mother needed to get help due to a fall or some other emergency. She still vividly remembers the German air and rocket attacks on England during World War II, so that should give you a idea of her age and why he would do so. He also purchased an Echo Connect.Prior to Christmas, my wife was visiting a friend who has an Echo device, witnessed some of its capabilities and decided to get me an Echo 2 as a gift. Upon realizing the potential that my wife would be able to use the drop in intercom feature to contact me for assistance no matter where I was in the house, I decided to purchase four Dots and strategically place them in our three story house. Why? Because she’s a pancreatic cancer patient and was (and did) have major surgery January 3rd to remove her tumor. (It’s called the Whipple Procedure, it’s invasive, and they remove whole and partial internal organs and then reconstruct.)Shortly after installing the devices, we started getting incoming call notifications from phone numbers that we did not know and some were announced as being from people we knew to be friends of my parents. We could not stop the devices from doing so by turning on Do Not Disturb to disable "incoming call" notifications as we swiftly realized that by doing so the Drop In feature is also disabled. That part completely makes no sense whatsoever considering that Do Not Disturb and drop ins are two different setting menus in the Alexa App and the Alexa App does not warn the user in any way that drop ins will be disabled. Basically one of the major reasons why I purchased the devices went up in smoke.Upon asking for tech support via chat, something that took over three hours to do because your people can’t read or write English or understand logic, and a phone call from a tech support person we were informed that this is actually a feature built into the programming. In addition, the definition of a Household as far as Alexa is concerned is essentially the same as Prime Family Sharing. The phone representative confirmed that there is no way to have the devices that are in Florida only announce incoming calls on the devices that are there and not also on the devices that we have in Rhode Island. There’s absolutely no logical programming reason for this to occur and not be able to segregate devices since his devices were registered to him and my devices were registered to me and we both had physical addresses entered into the device settings as well as our phone numbers.Nowhere in the marketing for the devices does it say that this will occur, nor in the descriptions of the Family Sharing. Basically, it’s an undisclosed flaw and deceptive marketing.The phone rep suggested that if we set up voice profiles, confirmed our devices were set to the correct numbers and addresses, the call notifications might stop. They did not. After some time of frustration of us dealing with these incoming call notifications, and of a desire to let my wife actually get some rest after her surgery, he dropped himself from our Prime Family Sharing plan. He should not have had to make that decision at all.And guess what? After he dropped we still got call notifications for a little over a day and during that time period it finally dawned on me that my devices were not announcing incoming calls. Upon checking our settings we both came to the realization that what was actually being announced were incoming phone calls to his landline via his Echo Connect. This is something that your tech support people should have immediately asked! “Is there an Echo Connect involved?"Upon doing some research on your support web site, something your company doesn’t even provide a link to from your retail site, I finally discovered in an easily overlooked “Note:" that these devices cannot announce or accept incoming calls from either landlines or mobile phones but can only place outbound calls to such. Essentially, to get actual "call notifications" you have to purchase an Echo Connect, and as I understand it that only works with landlines. Again, a serious flaw that is not mentioned in the marketing at all, so yet another example of deceptive marketing. Especially since you advertise them as being communications devices and show “incoming calls from...” but never disclose those incoming calls are actually Alexa to Alexa only without having a Connect!Most egregious of all, the marketing for the devices do not mention that they cannot place outbound calls to 911 without having a Connect. And these are communications devices that you advertise as being able to place calls to any number? Seriously? Not only should they be able to, they should be able to automatically give 911 the physical address of the device as that is a setting in the Alexa App!This 911 flaw combined with the disabling of the drop in feature of “call” notifications makes these devices nothing more than worthless toys. They are not communication devices at all. That’s just marketing and deceptive marketing at that. Is Amazon waiting for someone to have an accident and not be able to get help, be seriously injured and or die, and then get sued for millions for the design flaws and marketing practices? Eventually, that is exactly what will happen.Additional flaws include the fact that you can create to-do list items by voice, but have to pull out your phone and use the Alexa app to clear them. If you use your voice to set more than one timer, you have to use the Alexa App to cancel one of them. One of the devices suddenly stopped using our wireless network and had to be reset. I could go on, but you should get the picture by now that these things are worthless, hence my returning them.

title: Don't buy into this garbage.  It's not worth the headache.
body: I have 4 of these now within a household. I bought them just a few months ago. I can now equivocally say that I don't like them. There are a lot of little annoyances that Amazon has to work out before they become a good product. Here are a few annoyances that I have called Amazon about over the course of working with these Echos...1. I have a family account where in I am the Prime member. I have put my parents on the account as a household, as we live on the same property and use the same utilities... I share my Prime membership with my parents. This means that we hold separate accounts but within the same household. That being said, when I began setting up a smart home for my folks, Amazon assigned each Echo with a different name and set them up as to be connected to my profile or my parents profile. My profile gets full benefits of prime and has no concerns for lack of features. However my parents profile requires a separate music account, and cannot connect to their own google calendar, or shopping and to-do lists. The calendar for my profile outweighs theirs, or theirs can be set to outweigh mine... But only one calendar and list is aloud. This is however not incapable to overcome. Amazon though wont allow it. They will stop you from sharing music privileges simply by designating profiles to each echo, but not allow you to separate calendars and to-do lists... This is based solely on the greed of Amazon wanting a new $99 prime membership for each household member, instead of allowing us to truly share the account. This is a very detrimental fail for the use of the echos. This means that a household cannot truly function as a free-flowing household, and the features are ultimately not used, because only one person in the household is allowed to use them.2. Bing SUCKS! I have asked countless questions of Alexa that she is not able to answer. She is continually replying, with " I'm not sure how to answer that', or 'hmm I can't seem to find an answer.' But when you go to OK Google, Google has a great, detailed answer ready for you in milliseconds!Bing can't find it's own butt with both hands!3. I live in California, myself and several people in my family have been English teachers. There is no funny dialect in my household, nor is there much improper grammar. But, Alexa still has problems understanding and is most often hearing slurred amalgamations of some weird misunderstanding. Or thinking we said something totally different than what has been said. It's pretty bad.4. Alexa doesn't know what a decimal is, and has actually replied with factually incorrect information. Alexa can't even make decimal equivalents from fractions... This is simply a horrible failure on some poor coder, but still a piece of fodder that should have been fixed long ago! Bing and Alexa have actually given me wrong information numerous times. For instance, Alexa cannot correctly convert an easy fraction like 1/4" to a decimal.... She tells you that deciamls cannot be used to convey measurements...?! What?! Ultimate Fail!!!5. Where is the Sonos integration, where is the Samasung TV integration? Why is it so hard to connect with all of the house hold items, but the big corporate stuff that nobody really wants or needs is the first to be upgraded and brought to the public...?Fix what's broken first, then bring in the new stuff...!!6. Customer service gets you nowhere! I can't tell you how many times some guy in India with a heavy accent has answered the service line and said, 'Hello, my name is Mike...' BS. I don't care that you're outsourcing your calls, but for gods sake, don't lie to me! If your name is Anjali, then say it! Don't tell me your name is Mike and pretend your in Portland while every caller in the background is speaking the same accent as you!Also, if you present them with any feedback that they are failing on, they only tell you to go find the email form and submit a claim there over email.... What's wrong with a phone call? I'm here now, create the claim for me...!7. Did I mention Bing Sucks?!In my humble opinion, you should wait until the Google Home device is really ready to hit the market big and step into that instead of this Garbage! Don't buy into Alexa. It's not worth the frustration!I've only given this review 1 star because where I live I get horrible radio reception, and the one thing I like about the echo, is that I can now stream some of my favorite local and not local radio station during the day. Other than that, these echos are just expensive paperweights!But even at just playing music they are still infuriating. I will have Amzon music playing in another part of the house through my Sonos player, and then in the workshop I will play amazon music through my Alexa. She will continually cut out the song saying 'Another device is streaming Amazon Music, would like to stream here in stead?' Upon calling Amazon, they tell me that even though it's my Prime Music Account I can only stream from 1 device at a time...!!! Ugh! Amazon you suck!

title: Echo is an Excellent Gizmo with Horrible Configuration Instructions and Install Menu - Over time it becomes DISAPPOINTING
body: I changed my 5 star review to 1 star. To find out why please read the section labeled EDIT UPDATE.I really like the Echo device, but it took me way to long to get the Echo configured because the instructions and menu's are extremely poor, to put it mildly.THE PROBLEMS1. First, the instruction fail to say whether the app to download is for iPhone, Android, PC or Mac.I assumed it would be safest to use my Android smartphone, but after downloading and attempting to install I got a message which basically said -this app is incompatible the smartphone. My smarphone is compatible with every App I have downloaded. Because I hate Win8, 8.1 and 10 I still use Windows 7 which does not support "Apps", which is why I did not attempt to use my PC for downloading the "App".2. Setup menu is also horrible, and confusing. For example for the setting of metric vs other does not really give a choice, it has an On Off button which does not appear to do anything. It certainly does not bring up an edit menu.It should really simply have metric vs foot (units) so that you simply put a check mark to the one you want. Same for Celsius vs FahrenheitAs a consequence I still get Celsius when I want Fahrenheit etc. This is because of an extremely useless, frustrating User Interface.3. The Echo froze once or twice, so I had to unplug it and I had to start all over, until eventually it decided that it was fully setup.It should have had an interface like most Routers, which you configure by browsing to the device, which brings up a menu that you configure.If you have not setup an Echo before, you should be aware that once you run the "App" which is downloaded from the internet, you then have to disconnect from your wifi network, and instead connect to amazon-net (I don't remember the exact name) which then allows your smartphone, tablet or laptop etc. to connect your Echo (this of course means that you are no longer connected to the internet).Other than the setup, the Echo v 2 is excellent, with wonderful text to speech conversion that is the best I have ever heard. The one thing missing is the ability to retain the very last task before it is unplugged when you take it to another location. I especially like that you can connect to any radio station, ask for any radio program (Fresh Air, Science Friday, Ted Talk, The Moth, Back Story etc. It's also great to ask about anything that is on Wikipedia. Finally, to my surprise the text to speech on Wikipedia, as well as on Kindle books is far better than I expected, far less like a robot, than what I heard in the past.Edit UPDATE 3/1/2017: (reason why I changed my rating from 5 star to 1 star)As others have said while the device works well at first, over time everything about the device starts growing disappointing.Here are my reasons1. Radio programs such as Freakonomics (via TuneIn) is no longer available2. Radio programs that always used to be up to date Fresh Air, Science Friday, On the Media, etc. are no longer up to date. Whenever you request them you get one weeks older and they newer broadcasts are way out of date if available at all. A lot of programs available on TuneIn cannot be accessed via Echo Dot.Echo Malfunctions:1. Echo turns on for no apparent reasons as if someone said Alexa in the room. This problem was non existent or extremely rare for the first 2-3 months.3. Echo does not appear to understand request nearly as well as when it was new. Sometimes re-phrasing the request for the 3rd or 4th time works, but often I we just give up. This is especially true of requesting playing Audible titles. Echo Dot often confuses Kindle text to speech version with the Audible (human / actor reading of text). With all of the "Continual Improvement" hoopla about Alexa that Amazon brags about, you would think that you could request a book title and get what you want for existing books in your Account.4. You would think that if, while listening, you are interrupted by a phone call etc., you should be able to have Alexa, go back a page or two in the reading of your Kindle or Audible book. This function appears to be ABSENT and no matter how we tried we could not go forward or back by any amount other than by an entire Chapter. This is not Rocket Science and could easily be programmed. Pages do exist even on Kindle, it's a simple matter of assigning a "page" a standard number of words or characters.5. Often, though not always, if you unplug Echo Dot, in order to use it in another room, it will lose information about what it was doing (what book it was reading). This should not happen and would not be difficult to prevent.IN SUMMARYInstead of constantly IMPROVING, Echo Dot functionality has actually declined in our experience as reported by others. This calls into question the competence of the Amazon Software Team. Sadly, like too many things in the computer world, stupidity too often overcomes common sense competence.

title: Dot, you are great... but Alexa, you are no longer making any progress and you are becoming more intrusive...
body: 2nd UPDATE: We will be migrating from the Alexa platform since the home control is the same for our devices on the Google Home platform and Alexa has so many remaining shortcomings: (1) control of TiVo and even their own Fire TV products is still quite poor (whereas the Google Home products actually do better at controlling the Chromecast devices than a phone and/or browser usually); (2) Alexa has more issues on some of our Dots at consistently recognising what we are saying (especially songs and artist names); (3) Amazon still seems to limit all responses to their ecosystem and outside of IMDB and Wikipedia there are no search engines consulted; (4) Alexa wakes often and responds without anything close to the wake-up word 'Alexa'; (5) our living room Dot often reboots and resets the volume level to VERY LOUD each time; (6) 'Alexa' constantly advertises Amazon Music Unlimited even if I have a song in my library that I asked for; (7) there are no ties into apps such as an Amazon only Calendar Nor Amazon-only contacts list (I do not want to share my android/Google date with Amazon since they seem adverse to letting me see and edit it); and (8) 'Alexa' often answers something other than the question asked and asks if this answers my question... so far 99% NO! Since I have filed numerous reports in the app without any resolutions nor improvements, and since I can still stream my Amazon Music manually to any Google Device from the android app, there is not much sense to waste my time with a product that is no longer progressing. We will continue to use the Fire TV devices and Fire Tablets that we have, but for 7" tablets even, we will be going to Nook as it is truly android and it can run any necessary Amazon apps. I just wish Amazon would update features to make customers happier... maybe then I would look to stay mostly in their ecosystem.... currently I am regretting buying my music at Amazon and being locked in. What started out as fun soon made me realise that mostly Alexa is still just a toy and does not really serve a useful purpose in my life... whereas the Google platform can and does.---UPDATE: We have since tried the Google Mini as well. While the Dot is much better at recovering from a WiFI network that goes south, it still cannot track my Calendar, my email, nor any other standard Android app function that would be nice in a personal assistant. Also, I now have privacy concerns about Dot,,, since there is no way to use drop-in without allowing your entire phone contact list to be uploaded to Amazon... and there is no means to truly delete any part of it online -- so basically all of your business contacts and personal contacts are now on Alexa and anyone that has access to one of your Alexa devices can call and/or text any of them whether accidentally or on purpose. I, for one, will not be sharing my contact list with Amazon and I better not find out that it was accidentally copied (since they do not seem to make it easy to permanently disable either). Overall, we still prefer the Dot but only because we are members of the Amazon ecosystem since neither Google nor Amazon will allow each other's products in their video nor their audio applications for their respective devices. If we wanted a "true" assistant, the Google one has more integration with the common smartphone apps that we use daily... Alexa only provides "just enough" because of the support of our existing Amazon products (Fire TV and Music). Amazon, if you are listening... fix the drop-in feature and allow me to selectively upload contacts and edit them properly... Also, please support my phone calendar and email without storing too much info in your outrageous cloud servers to be found later!--- PREVIOUS REVIEW---This product was a Xmas gift for the other half and it is already installed. After 24hrs, she is now a beloved member of the family. While her speaker is a bit lacking, her functionality is beyond wonderful. She is really great at understanding what we are saying for an AI. We have wasted most of the time playing games and music (mostly from our Amazon music collection -- finally a 'YAY' by me for the cloud)....We also connected it to our gen-1 Wink hub for control of our lights, and we honestly think it works much better than the wink app. While the ability to control some of the FireTV apps is appealing, at the moment it is very hit-and-miss. For instance, Hulu works to some degree after the update a few days ago but Netflix is still weirdly missing any support -- it works well for Amazon prime though.We added a bluetooth speaker this morning to Dot, and now the music sounds much more full and definitely louder... a definitely desired and appreciated upgrade. Thank you Amazon for making Dot. She is highly recommended for purchase by anyone with supported home automation hubs and/or by anyone who is already a member of the Amazon eco-system.Oh.... I forgot to mention when Alexa barks ('Alexa, bark like a dog.') or meows it really drives our puppy crazy... he never bothered anything, but now he wants to eat Dot and the bluetooth speaker. :)

title: Worthless schizophrenia garbage-shaped hockey puck.
body: I cannot for the life of me understand how anyone could think this is a quality product. I am only using it for one function (controlling music through Spotify) and it works so so poorly. I am certain that if I were using the Echo Dot to it's full capacity I would identify a million other problems. But here are just a few:I have the Dot connected to my existing stereo system through an $11 wireless bluetooth adapter. I have been using this adapter to play wirelessly music from my iphone for years with no problems. I set up the new Echo Dot in my dining room, and it connected to the Bluetooth with no trouble, and the music sounded clear. I could sit at my table with my coffee and ask Alexa to "play Harvest by Neil Young" and she would do it, no problem. Wow, I was impressed. Now, because I don't live in a one room shoe-box, I have speakers wired in my living room and my kitchen, as well as my back deck. These are hardwired speakers, not bluetooth speakers. I have been using my vintage receiver and high-quality hard-wired speakers since before wireless audio was even a glimmer in Alexa's stupid blue rotating eye; I am not ditching them for some overpriced piece of garbage wireless speakers. Because I listen to music all over my house, I immediately bought another Dot to put in my kitchen because...sometimes I am in my kitchen. I set that one up, connected it to my Spotify account and the Blue tooth adapter and asked it to play music. No problem, worked fine. Then, I walk into the dining room and ask Alexa to pause the music. She says "I can only pause music when music is playing." So...the one in my dining room didn't know that the one in my kitchen was playing music. So long story short, two Dots cannot be connected to work together. WTF? Why?So I looked around for just an extension controller/microphone for the Echo Dot, since I was effectively trying to use one to remotely control the other. I quickly found the Alexa Remote, which is only like $10. This allows you to control your Dot when you are in another room. Nice!. I felt a little stupid for buying a second Dot when all I needed was the remote. Got the remote, and guess what: You have to walk over to it and push a and hold the button for it to listen. Doesn't solve my problem at all. I have no idea why the engineers at Amazon would design a robot assistant that has no ability to hear you in different rooms; you essentially need to have several separate robot assistants, and they cannot even work together on the same simple task, which in this case is just to "pause the music." What a shame. Also! the remote barely works when you do use it. Alexa just does't hear you at all half the time, and often when you ask her to play music through the remote she just plays glitching and clicking sounds. What a POS.Next morning, next problem: Ask Alexa "Play Harvest by Neil Young," and she says "playing Harvest by Neil Young from Spotify..." and then nothing. Try it again, nothing. Silence. This is a know problem. Sometimes it just doesn't work at all! Awesome. You have to wait for it to update it's software for the problem to correct itself, and it updates overnight. So no music, cool.AND guess what, the Dot also just doesn't work well when it is working. When you ask to to play to play an album by an artist, it often says it can't find it, and then you ask it to list albums by that artist and it lists it. So you say again, play the album, and it says it can't find it. Also, if you don't say the name of a song exactly correctly it guesses and plays something wildly different by a completely different artist. It is constantly playing the wrong thing, or not able to find the right thing, or it can't hear you cause there is music playing. Such a pain. Within two weeks the work "Alexa" had become associated with frustration. Boxed em up. Will probably use them as hocky pucks or sell them at a garage sale.And then this morning my wife asked her to "play the cherry tree song by Elizabeth Mitchel." Granted, my wife got the name of song wrong, but Alexa replied. "Do you want to go through the magic door?" WTF? My wife tried again, and Alexa was like "do you want to go to the mountains or the river?" Or something like that. Has she lost her "artificially intelligent" mind? It's like having a worthless schizophrenic robot trapped in a hockey puck sitting around in your house.AND THEN: All of a sudden it has trouble connecting to Bluetooth. Takes it four or five tries to connect and when it finally does, it just plays glitchy clicking sounds through the stereo!All of these problems are within 10 days of use. Constantly trouble shooting, connecting and reconnecting, modifying what you just asked hoping to get the correct response. Just a total cheap, poorly programed piece of garbage.

title: Great device, but far from being a support to real-life needs
body: ** update Jul 15, 2020: Downgrade from 2 to 1 star **You would think they get smarter, but they don't. Amazon still is happy an Echo can tell a joke, but in 50% of a reasonable question the answer is "I didn't get that" or "I cannot help you with this" or a remotly related answer.Also the configuration in several rooms has gotten worse. The wrong Alexa is answering. Connection problems at night will wake you up even you have set it to Sleep - just painful. I am getting rid of 3 out of my 5 Echo devices. They are not fun.** update Jan 14, 2018: Downgrade from 4 to 2 stars **After two weeks of use, I have a better appreciation of Alexa.I am still glad I bought 4 Echo devices (3 three dots and one Echo 2nd gen) that's why I give it 2 stars (and not 1). The potential is huge for the future. It is so easy to get the hook on talking rather than typing. At the rate you come up with new questions that Alexa could answer, you will also realize the limitations. Alexa is very basic. The abilities (skills) are mostly annoying, as in some cases you need to remember exact phrases to even get an approximate response.Alexa is far from being intelligent - today. There are no real interactions. For most cases every question is treated individually, you cannot have a conversation by asking follow-up questions. Rarely Alexa can relate anything to the previous questions (examples: you can stop a timer or tell it, the answer was not useful), but many times you would like to drill deeper into an answer.Amazon is not able to gather real big players to develop skills. I'd say 99% of the skills connecting to some other service providers are from thirdparties or not available. Examples: Gmail, iTunes, Wunderlist, Microsoft To-Do, Shazam are all not available. I would never give permission to a unknown developer to access my emails! And yet these integrations would just be perfect. It's great to set a timer or reminder (which is probably the thing I use most currently), but I would like to have Alexa translate it in a natural way, not begging a skill name in an obscure way to translate. Even the new built in translation skill is only from English to another language. Sad again, as when you are reading a book in let's say French you would like to ask Alexa to quickly translate a word to English - not possible, not even with other skills.Actually it is frustrating because I have that many questions to Alexa that today do not work, and Amazon would be in a position to bridge the worlds of Google, Apple and Microsof. I am convinced all these including Amazon want to protect their own universes and forget the clients. Why can Google not be on board and be part of Alexa? It would be great to at least listen (or in the future on some newer devices watch) YouTube. Have Alexa read my emails would be a dream.Why has there no advance with Microsoft Corana to combine forces with Alexa?Why can it not integrate with Apple? I'd love to control iTunes through Alexa?Why can I not configure a different To Do list like Wunderlist or MS To-To? Using the Amazon app to shop in anything other than Amazon is downright unusable.The lack of native/original skills is a big hindarance for this being your digital assistant. It is still a far shot from being an assistant. Right now it is a monkey with mask of Einstein ;-).** original rating 4 stars **The device itself is great once set up (5 stars), its sound is basic for music, but good and clear to give you answers.Alexa itself is also cool - 4 stars:1) You can tell there is not that much AI yet as everyone from the tec communitiy would like to make you believe, but the great thing is that this will keep improving.2) Unfortunately not all countries are supported (e.g. Switzerland).What is rather unpleasant is the set up process as soon it is not the standard or you need to fix something - 2 stars:1) My app shows Japanese rather than English and I have no idea where it takes that from.2) Echo does not seem to work on all networks as I have trouble to connect it to some of my WiFi networks.3) There is no error guidance from the app - you need to go to a browser for trouble shooting.4) The different light modes are not explained in the app.5) There are no global settings for your Echo devices, you need to configure the values like location for each individually.6) The app is not nicely done, actually it does just the basics.

title: -Installation support by Amazon is POS - Be prepared to take over one hour to install this Echo Dot 2nd Gen if you get an error
body: Set up did not go well--at all! I am tech savvy, I follow instructions well and cautiously. All operating systems on the Samsung Note 4 up to date.Tried to use the 'simple' set up for this device- Alexa app crashed - giving pop up "certificate error- Please update Android System Webview", the Alexa app takes me to Google Play store, and guess what folks - my Android System Webview is current - no update available. So, instinctively I uninstalled Alexa app, restarted phone- arrive at same error on set up! I then uninstalled Alexa app- again, checked all GOOGLE apps were current (all of them were current)!. Restarted phone, installed Alexa app a 3rd time - still same error "certificate error- Please update Android System Webview". Called Amazon customer service, first female voice had such a thick accent, could not have a conversation-Amazon customer service rep (she) put me on hold. So of course I Hung Up. Called Customer Service again- got "Harry", another person very difficult to understand,-at least Harry stayed with me for all his 'fundamental' questions, taking about 15 minutes to find my account because of the difficulty in understanding english. BY Now I have wasted 1 hour of time. Harry is asking me to disable chrome ? ! Next We went to system settings - and for the second time I've told Harry I have no 'update' possible for Android System Webview. Only the word "uninstall" appears. When will I ever get to speak to a person that has technical knowledge?? Why is Amazon hiring people we cannot converse with over the phone without heavy accents? and are obviously unable to handle technical installation questions?Harry asked me to uninstall Android System Webview (ASW)- now this says this app (ASW) content is powered by Chrome. I'm LOL now because we de-installed Chrome before going down this poorly designed set of call center instructions Harry is following that I'm living with today.So far the set up is now 1 hour 35 minutes and still not connecting to the Dot.***I bought this echo dot for the sole purpose of leaving the Dot at my 87yr Mom's home to play music for her from an Amazon Music list of my design that contains her favorite songs while she is on HOSPICE! FYI, music may be a significant benefit for dementia and Alzheimer's patients.***By now, I've uninstalled ASW, uninstalled Alexa app, all with Harry, Reinstalled ASW. Reinstalled Alexa app with Harry. And guess what:"certificate error- Please update Android System Webview"--AGAIN>Now we are headed to guess what, enable chrome, because as we know, ASW is integrated with chrome.Tried again, same certificate error.ASW is upto date. De- installed -- again -- what a LOC. Re-installed ASW again. God help me.Same ASW certificate error - we reset the echo dot using a pin in the pin whole in top center of echo dot. Attempted set up.Same ASW certificate error.To troubleshoot, we are now putting away the desired Samsung Note 4 (Android Marshmallow v6.0.1) we've tried for set up and moving to another 2nd Samsung Note 4 by a household member that already has a 1st gen Echo Dot (Black) installed. Are you as tired as I am at this point? Amazon Rep Harry accidentally hangs up on me.Fairly quickly Harry calls back, now we are trying the 2nd Samsung Note 4 (Android Marshmallow 6.0.1) phone to connect to the 2nd gen Echo Dot (white). We get much further, at least this time we get to select language, we have to watch the entire video, now we are stuck watching the instruction video (again further than we were above, but now wasting time). Next button finally comes up. Alexa is now working.So what is learned? Well not a whole WHOAL because the phone needed -phone 1 that would not setup - is needed to run the white 2nd gen echo dot. Now at 2 hours.. I think there is enough information to prepare buyers that not every thing will work as smooth as promoted by Amazon or its instructional materials. Sad very sad.

title: Months of trying to get it to work...threw it in the trash.
body: I bought 2 of these in October of 2017. One for my mother who had 2 strokes and 1 for my home. The one for my home I had an interest in mainly streaming music as I work from home and thought I would use it in my office. I also bought 2 Harmon Kardon speakers. Initially setting it up I had no problems and gave it a 4 star review. Mainly because you can not hard wire the device to internet connection as well as the lack of a music app on the alexa app and the difficulty in getting Alexa to play songs from your Amazon music library.Very shortly after the review I started having issues with the device not being able to stream music and stopping in the middle of songs, not being able to understand me.And constantly losing its connection. I called support which was fantastic and ran me through a series of tests. They recommended that I open some ports on my modem. I open the ports, reset the modem and the same problem persisted. I called my internet provider and they came to my home installed a new modem, new fittings.Still the echo will not stream music. Constantly flashing red, saying it lost its connection. Pausing for up to 2 minutes in the middle of a song. All this while my Fire stick streams music non stop upstairs on the same Wi-Fi connection. Multiple calls to support resulted in more Modem resetting, faster internet, new modem.. Meanwhile every other device in my home was performing fantastic. I did consider maybe I had a faulty device so I bought another, plugged it in and Same thing..could not play one song with out stopping losing connection. So I just started using my phone to stream music and absolutely no problems streaming music. The Harmon Kardon Speakers are the highlight of this story as they sound absolutely AMAZING connected to everything besides the Alexa Dot. I honestly can not believe the amount of time and energy I spent trying to trouble shoot this device. I only wanted it to do the most basic thing and simply stream music. I was so frustrated with it, I actually opened my front door and threw it in the garbage can outside. Exactly where it belongs. I would honestly not recommend this device to anyone as it performs horribly.I will say Amazon support is great, they did try to help me each time I called and with the exception of one rep saying he would call me back who never did, all the support were great. On my final call about the device today I spoke to a manager who offered me a refund on the device and was very professional. I still honestly feel that the entire experience is a 1 star experience. The most infuriating thing was when the Alexa would fail i would use a 5 yr old first gen Samsung Tablet the whole family uses and it would stream music perfectly from the same Wi-Fi connection. Maybe if these were able to be hard wired to a connection they would be good, but the device as it is has a very hard time connecting even when just a few feet from the modem. I tried absolutely everything to get it to work until it became painfully obvious that the easiest solution was to throw it in the garbage and simply use the devices I already have to stream music. The voice control is nice but I would much rather be able to listen to the entire song. I have purchased a total of 3 of these. My mother uses hers a little bit and it works. I have another here and it does the same thing as the one I threw in the trash. So I just use it to control my fire stick. It will do those simple tasks but if try to use it to stream music it fails.

title: DO NOT BUY - It will not play my purchased Amazon Prime Music ANYMORE. I'M NOT SURE WHAT WENT WRONG.
body: So I purchased this awhile back and it was useful for me for news, weather, set timer and playing my purchased Amazon Prime Music, until recently.After owning two of these (Echo and Echo Dot) since 2016, two months ago it stopped playing my music collections (which I purchased through Amazon Prime). When I issue a command " Alexa, play my music", it would respond with "Playing your music from Amazon music" followed by a long pause with a few seconds of the blue ring of light and not play any music and an audio prompt "I'm not sure what went wrong". I would try to issue the same command several times to no avail. I tried to disconnect the device from the power for a few minutes and that would INTERMITTENTLY work but most of the time it would not play the music at all. I checked all the required firewall ports from my router and no issue there (they're all open inbound and outbound). The device is also located next to my WiFi router so there is not a "signal strength" issue. The device can provide me the weather, the news, the traffic or any other information I asked without a problem so it's not a "signal strength" issue.Finally I called up Amazon support after I've exhausted my research resolving my Alexa music playback issue. Amazon support personnel:- First solution (from their troubleshooting script) was to "disconnect the device from the power for a minute and reconnect it back" and see if the fixes the problem. That's a brilliant and lazy advise but I did it anyway to no avail.- Second, she asked me how far was the device from the WiFi because a low signal will not play the music. I told her it is located next to the WiFi modem. She asked me to disconnect the modem from the power for a minute and reconnect it back. So I did what she asked and it still would not play my music collection. After this went on for several minutes to no resolution, I was frustrated and hang up. Useless support.I knew their purported "my WiFi issue is the culprit" because I can ask Alexa to play a specific song individually from my music library and it will play it with no problem. Which means "signal strength" is not the culprit, nor "required ports are not open. Alexa will not play my PURCHASED Amazon Music collection but it WOULD play my specific playlist or SPECIFIC MUSIC INDIVIDUALLY.My THEORY of the problem, when you ask Alexa to play your music, it checks to see if you're a "MUSIC UNLIMITED" subscriber first and if you're not, it does not comply with your command and does nothing. You can issue a "play music playlist" (or play individual specific songs) command and it will play your playlist with no problems. I have a PRIME MEMBERSHIP but not MUSIC UNLIMITED, so it cannot distinguished from the two when trying to play your purchased Amazon Prime. This is a load of b0ll0cks because it's the only reason I bought the device in the first place and the only reason I purchased music from Amazon.

title: CAVEAT EMPTOR!!!
body: Buyer beware, folks. I have little to say about features / functionality in this review as I barely used my Echo Dot. I purchased the device in late May of 2017, set it up, tested it out for a few days, and more or less forgot about it. Pretty much never touched it other than to unplug it at some point because I needed the outlet. Fast forward to early October when I decided to move the device into the bedroom to test out the new Sonos skill...and found that on booting up the device it that it would not work properly. More specifically, it would initially make a few rhythmic, escalating ugly tones, then settle into a faint rhythmic clicking. When attempting to respond to a command, the speaker itself would cut in and out in a clipped fashion in sync with the clicks. Hardware failure for sure, with a call to Amazon confirming as much. Here's where things get ugly:- The device only comes with a 90-day Limited Warranty (extended warranties are available for purchase, of course)- Amazon's policy is that it is at the discretion of the Amazon CSA as to what to offer to a customer for an out-of-warranty device- My device was barely used, effectively still "out-of-the-box"- I stated as much and certainly my usage history of Alexa (cloud-based service after all!) would corroborate- I made it clear that my expectation as a loyal customer and user of other Amazon hardware (Kindles) was that a device that failed in such a manner was effectively a lemon and should be replaced regardless of warranty period- I was instead offered "the best they could do" of a 15% discount on the purchase of a replacement device; basically telling me that they'd rather have my money (again) than fix their shoddy hardwareMy take here is that Amazon is well aware that their hardware is prone to fault. Anyone who offers no better than a 90-day Limited Warranty on a brand new device is effectively signaling this to the market. Likewise, a company unwilling to extend a modicum of flexibility to a customer who is clearly stating that their product was *effectively a lemon* has no faith in their ability to replace the bad device with a product of better quality.AMAZON: You well know that the Cognitive Assistant space is tight and competitive. Differentiation is achieved via feature, functionality, and scope and breadth of ecosystem. So if the front door into the ecosystem for the average consumer is primarily your own hardware...and that hardware is shoddy and poorly supported...how do you think you will end up positioned against the competition?CONSUMERS: I'm not partisan by any stretch - nor am I a fan of Google - but their competing product, the "Google Mini", is a solid match on a feature-by-feature basis, can be bought for the same price, and comes with a full 1-Year Warranty. And perhaps they're also more accommodating when it comes to a customer who's had a very poor experience with their hardware? I don't know yet myself, but let's find out...

title: Device software and web interfaces can be improved for a blind person who doesn't own an smartphone.
body: I’ve had my echo dot for a few weeks now. I’m giving the review a low rating being I do not own a smart phone and I don’t have Wi-Fi on my desktop. It is on the modem only. I feel this should be setup by USB if a person has that. I had to get one of my Nabors to help me. I had to give them my Wi-Fi network and password along with my Amazon password. Granted I went in and change the Amazon password after they left. But I couldn’t go and change the Wi-Fi stuff for the echo dot after it was connected to an network. It wanted me to put it back in setup mode and at that point, it disconnects from the current network and my neighbor has the password for the Wi-Fi network. I had to go out and buy an small USB Wi-Fi dongoal to be able to change the Wi-Fi stored password on the Echo.Other things I think could be improved for a blind person.When you press and hold the Microphone and volume down buttons to reset the device! The Echo dot should give an beep when you can release it. The lights changing colors don’t do anything for a blind person. It would also be helpful if the Echo dot gave a beep to let you know it is starting to boot up. Again the lights coming on don’t do anything for a blind person. You just have to guess it is coming up.Web interface alexa.amazon.com needs to be improved to add missing functionality so a blind person who doesn’t own a smart phone can take advantage of being able to add contacts, be able to turn on being able to make calls and sending messages and turn on the drop-in functionality. We should also be able to see whose echo devices are on-line etc… Just like people can do that own a smart phone.Notification. Currently as far as I can tell, if you miss one! The device doesn’t play a reminder tone every so often to let you know you have unlistened notification. Same goes for messages and the like. I think the device should do that. Maybe an option in the Sounds in the web interface.Being we are on the topic of sounds! I would like to be able to upload my own alarm sound effect as an WAV or MP3 file to have the Echo dot play it when the alarm goes off. I would also like to be able to change the Timer sound when it goes off.I think the timer function needs to be improved so when you say to set timer for 10 minutes! The device automatically announces how much time is remaining every 1 minute until it gets down to 3 minutes and then every 30 seconds until it gets down to 1 minutes and then it should announce every 10 seconds until it gets to 10 seconds and then it should announce every 1 second until it gets to 0 when the finish timer tone goes off. Again maybe something that could be added to the setting tab for a device on the web interface for those people like myself who would find it very useful to have.Other than above the device works well and I like it! I just feel it can be improved a lot to make it even more useful to everyone.

title: I’m a strong Amazon fan and Customer but No Alexa ...
body: Sorry guys, I just can’t lie about my review. I finally decided to give this echo a try and that’s mostly came out of the fact that Amazon introduced the second version of the Alexa and I started with the cheapest one in this series, echo dot.I know there are thousands of positive feedback about this device but for me and almost after a month and half trying my best to make a bond with her, I should testify that I failed.We couldn’t simply understand each other language and you can’t make the echo work unless you speak exactly the way it was programmed to understand and I really didn’t have time and energy to learn it’s language.I could do many different things with my device but it wasn’t like I couldn’t do ine without it.Yes, what I expected or maybe my false assumption was this device has some sort of A.I. which makes it learn during the time but I was wrong.I finally ended up doing the stuff I used to directly by my phone and with it’s own app much faster than talking to Alexa.For example I wanted to create a to-do list other than what it was built predefualt by the app and I couldn’t make Alexa doing it for me so I used the echo app to create what I wanted and I even added some special tasks for that to-do list I customized but again when everything has already been made, I asked Alexa to add a item to the list which she couldn’t or even telling me what it is on that list, totally failed.And this was the time that I had already convinced myself Alexa is just an organizer as well as a music player (if you don’t want to include the music providers she is not programmed for such as google).I did connect the echo dot with TP-Link smart bulbs which I also bought from Amazon and these bulbs are so easy to connect with kasa but when everything comes to Alexa, I experienced a negative response here because almost five out of ten tries to ask Alexa to turn on/off something or change the color or brightness were usually ended up with “TP-Link doesn’t respond”, so again I had to open up the app on my phone and do what I wanted directly by the app and without any problem.Overall, I had big hopes for this little device and much bigger expectations and I never thought that I will finally return it back to Amazon.Now I’m planning to buy google home and try that device and this time without any expectations because it’s very likely that I found the reality behind all these advertises that we still have a long way to go to make what I was expecting to what they really are.Time will prove it google home can be even a bit more smarter or practical.Finally please don’t judge echo devices and Alexa based upon my personal feedback because it might be me and only my problem to understand how it can work, although I called at least twice and shared my concerns with related Amazon department and I read almost everything I could find on the internet.

title: Unable to connect to 5GHz WiFi though my Echo can. App is horrible... still.
body: i just received my Dot v2 and can't get it to connect to my WiFi network. using the app has reminded me how bad the user experience was when i first tried to setup my Echo. at the time i let it slide because those were definitely early-adopter days. surprising for a company with so many resources that they can't create a decent mobile app for these group of devices – even considering the app is the main way to interact with any Amazon Echo device.the app starts to get very sluggish after a couple of attempts to connect. scrolling is horrendous and keyboard taps take several seconds to show up (i.e., when entering in password, etc)i've restarted both my router, my phone, the Dot several times – Tomato WPA2 Personal + AES[Update Dec 3, 2016]TL;DR if you're having trouble connecting to a 5 GHz network, try connecting to one configured to 2.4 GHz.it's good to know that Amazon is looking over these reviews – they read my bad review and emailed me within a day, asking me to contact Customer Service. (i didn't do that because from what i read on various forums they probably weren't going to be much help, sorry Amazon).today i took some time to run through various network permutations to see if i could get the Dot to connect at all.i took an unused router and configured it for the same SSID and password as my current network, etc. starting with 5 GHz i tried WPA2, WPA, and even NO security. the Dot *could never connect* nor did the network name ever show up in the app under the list of networks, even after dozens of repeated scans.i then changed the network to 2.4 GHz and started with security set to WPA2. the network ID immediately showed showed up in the app. and shortly after putting in the network password the Dot was able to connect !since the network name and the password were the same for the 2.4 GHz/5 GHz networks, i changed the router back to 5 GHz to see if Dot could connect since it had the proper network/password saved. i restarted the Dot, but unfortunately it could never connect.Amazon's promotional materials state the Dot can connect to both types of WiFi networks – "Dual-band Wi-Fi supports 802.11 a/b/g/n (2.4 and 5 GHz) networks". considering that, i would say that my Dot has a hardware defect. so, for the short term, i activated a separate 2.4 GHz network on my main router so that the Dot can connect until i can get it replaced with one that works.ps, my main Amazon Echo, which i've had since the very first rollout, has had absolutely no issues connecting to my main 5 GHz network.pps, the Alexa mobile app is still horrible – it's nowhere near the quality of the main Amazon shopping app.[Update Jan 7, 2016]Amazon sent me out a replacement Dot. Same issues all around.(thanks to OlsonBros for the reply)

title: A Great Hockey Puck
body: WOW. What can I say about this futuristic piece of garbage?It can't understand me constantly even after voice training.It doesn't stick to one wi-fi.When looking to "forget this wi-fi" on the alexa app, it directs me to amazon customer service to forget a wi-fi network. Is it that serious?It skips on my music all the time and sometimes just stops.After having issues with my first one always saying "Sorry, your echo dot lost its connection" they send me a different one that is just as terrible.You cannot have a scene where music starts as a part of it (HOW BASIC COULD THIS BE).I say "Watch Fire TV" and she thinks I saw "WHAT'S Fire TV." She then begins a spiel about Fire TV and it takes longer than necessary to get her to stop because she has a hard time hearing me over HER OWN VOICE.When I use the alarm I want to chuck the entire thing out the window because she can’t hear me say stop.I work in IT and we have discussions about the latest technology. It is common for us to report our latest alexa issues. Edit: I would like to thank the Amazon representative who tried to assist with all these issues. What I was told was that, Alexa learns as you continue to speak to her, Comcast is the reason why music has not been working recently ( doesn't solve my problem) and they are working towards adding the music alarm/scene features.That's nice. I appreciate the time she took to explain, however echo dot has far too many little issues that form a cumulative pain in my ass. I don't want several features that somewhat work, I would rather a few features that definitely work.So, as short as possible, these are the features I've been having trouble withAlexa understanding me (I speak perfect English and have given plenty of speeches in the past. I enunciate my words. I do not have an accent) Alexa hearing me over her own voice Her misunderstanding me No music for an alarm or scene Her talking far too muchBluetooth not working (yes I did disconnect and reconnect. It says connected without playing any sound)Edit 2: Alexa has not been learning. Probably the opposite. She understands me less and less, but keeps talking like she does. What I mean is, she needs to stop explaining to me. If I say connect to Bluetooth, I don’t want to hear “could not connect to bluetooth please check blah blah blah or blah blah blah in the connection center blah blah blah I DON’T CARE. She needs to stop explaining things to me when the issue is with her. How many times am I supposed to hear the same instructions? You don’t think I got it by now? I really want to chuck this thing out the window but my windows are sealed for the winter.Will update in Spring.

title: A Don't Like-Hate Relationship That Makes Me Love Siri
body: Siri sometimes annoys me and I don't care for needing an iPhone or iPad, or 'Hey, Siri...' to summon her. So I thought that getting a Dot and augmenting my meager home automation would be just the ticket.So far I have been supremely disappointed. Setup was easy, but the Dot has failed to work so many times, and keeps reporting itself as disconnected when more often it's just actually confused and gives itself a break. I've relocated it to be within 2' of the router and it still says it's disconnected. When it does respond it usually won't do so for a subsequent command and takes another rest break, telling me to 'try again later'.The app isn't well written and worse— it takes forever to respond (as did the Dot in Setup). It's often out of sync with what the Dot is doing, if and when it actually does something. Most of the time it responses with theb blue light for several seconds and then shuts off without an explanation.Playing music is also frustrating. Occasionally it will play something from my Amazon online cache, more often it will play a different song of the same or similar name from Amazon Prime, most often it just stops. If told to play a genre such as jazz or 'relaxing' music (as mentioned by Amazon) it lights up, goes dark, and stays silent. If it does play some songs, it stops arbitrarily after a few minutes.I was about to send it back (and there's STILL a lot of time) when some extensive surfing helped a little with some basic functionality. Amazon support hasn't been helpful AT ALL.PROSEasy to set upSmall footprintCan use high-quality wired speakers as well as BT speakers (when convenience is more important that quality)Hears you anywhere in the roomPotential for expansion of functionalityCONSPoor and inconsistent performanceRequires very literal commandsRequires scouring the web to learn basic functionalityWith over 24,000 mostly positive reviews [many of them fake on/two word 5-star reviews from people who give the same review for EVERY product they 'review'], no doubt some of this is likely my unfamiliarity with the learning curve. But given Amazon's definition of stars, I can't rate it higher at this point. For me, this is all uphill, and it's frustrating. That's without venturing into the whole third-party skills thing.If I can grok the Dot— and I WANT to, the rating will improve, not that 1 in 24,000 means anything. But it shouldn't be this difficult to get good, consistent, reliable, BASIC results, let alone advanced Skills and IFTTT. Oy. How-to-Geek has been a big help. But somewhere there must be a fairly thorough tutorial on how to go from 0-60, 100, and infinity and beyond. Right now it's all I can do to not fire it against the wall. An intervention may be in order.

title: Not Ready For Prime-Time.  VERY Buggy.  Needs Testing.
body: I wish I would have used the first one longer before I ordered 5 more! I love Amazon, don't get me wrong. This device is fun for a while, but it's incredibly buggy, which gets REALLY annoying after a while. I'll just leave a bulleted list of my pros/cons based on my personal experience so far.CONS:1. Quits connecting to Bluetooth speaker it's been paired with after several days. Must remove the Bluetooth speaker and re-pair.2. Spotify will play some songs, and then just abruptly stop playing in the middle of a song. At this point Alexa is dead and won't play Spotify anymore until you unplug and plug it back in. Once rebooted, she'll play another song or two before freezing up again. Amazon support's only response is to un-link Spotify and re-link.2a. As a side note, more of a Spotify thing, but once you link your Spotify account, it is linked to all of your Echo devices. You can't currently link multiple Spotify accounts. The downside to this is that someone at home will ask Alexa to play some music (when I'm driving or working, listening to music as well). My music abruptly stops because my account via Alexa is being used at home. This isn't the same issue as 2. 2. is a bug, this one is just how Spotify Connect works. I wish they'd remove that limitations when Alexa is being used, or add the ability to use multiple accounts.3. The Echo app is very buggy and seems slow. Time and time again it will say that it's 'offline'. I have to force close the app and open it again for it to realize that it's been online the whole time.4. Frequent audio glitches in Alexa's voice when responding.5. For being a second generation product, I would expect this to be seamless with much fewer bugs.PROS:1. It's so awesome to be able to turn on/off lights and other smart home devices simply by asking Alexa to do it. This actually works pretty well as long as you name your devices uniquely. The only issue I've had is when I have two devices that have some of the same words in the name, sometimes she'll say I have multiple devices with that name and ask which one. At that point, it's hard to get her to pick the one I want. After renaming my devices, it works better.2. Alexa seems so much smarter than Siri. The voice sounds similar, but more realistic and responds better to more natural language.CONCLUSION:The Echo Dot is a cool device, but it really isn't ready IMHO. It should be a $5 device since really we're the one's doing the testing for Amazon. A device that is supposed to make your life easier tends to need a lot of user help due to all of the bugs. In reality, it causes me to waste MORE time, instead of saving it. I hope that some firmware updates in the near future will fix some of these issues.

title: Extremely Frustrating, Extremely Disappointing
body: Where can I even begin? I loved this stupid thing when I got it about a month ago. Loved it to death. Changed the wake word to "computer" and I was good to go. I had so much fun asking Computer what the weather was going to be and to play certain songs. Even linked my spotify. It was great, my echo understood everything. This thing would play ocean surf sounds for me as I went to sleep to make me feel more at home. So cool. But then, it stopped being cool. Echo began to not recognize phrases or even log them in the app for me to check. I would have to shout at the top of my lungs for it to even try to understand me. Spotify got worse on it. I once had to ask it to play "My Way" by Frank Sinatra 15 times before it finally played "My Way" by Seth McFarland. Only after 5 more times did it finally understand that I wanted it by Frank Sinatra. Now this wouldn't be a big deal but no one want to have to be forced to scream at their echo upwards of 40 times to play an incredibly famous song. I tried to deal with this and I ended up only using my echo dot for timers and to play nature sounds because that seemed to be the only two things it could figure out anymore. But today I made the mistake of asking it to play "The Audition" from LaLa Land on spotify. I kid you not I asked it more than 50 times to play the song and each time it would say either it could not find it on spotify or that "here is a sample of the audition by emma stone from amazon" so I know it knew what song to look for. So I went in to spotify to search the song and of course it was there. I play it manually and it plays fine. But the whole point of an echo is the voice interface so I ask it repeatedly again and again to play the song giving variations of the same command and title. Nothing. I lost count of how many times I told this stupid hunk of plastic to play the song. But it got to the point where the echo would interrupt me to tell me it could not find the song before I even finished saying the title. Now, I'm a pretty calm dude, I work in customer service so I know how to keep my cool in frustrating situations, but this was too much. I ended up becoming so frustrated with this echo that I gave it one more chance and when it interrupted me again I threw it on the ground and took a machete to it. And my machete is only for decoration so I hope you realize how annoying and absolutely frustrating this device was. The only plus I have for this is that it is well made cause it withstood being hacked with a jungle clearing knife and remained largely intact. I really, really hope that my experience was a one-off situation and that it is not the norm, but based on my experience with this device, I cannot give it more than one star no matter how much I want to love it.

title: Audio lag is TERRIBLE if used as bluetooth speaker.
body: Unfortunately, the audio lag is huge if this is used as a bluetooth speaker. Lag between any video and audio output on the echo is between 0.5 and 2.5 seconds, which makes the video unwatchable.I tried my phone, computer, samsung TV, and shockingly the fire TV stick as the audio source. You would think that amazon products would at least sync flawlessly, but the fire TV stick was the worst. Regardless of how far away the device is, while playing or when pairing, there is always a terrible lag between the video and audio, and worst of all, lag is inconsistent (sometimes half a second, sometimes almost 3 seconds).I specifically bought it so that I could connect the fire TV stick to my projector, and listen to the audio of my apps (Netflix, Hulu, Directv Now, etc.) through the Echo Dot. I know sound quality was not gonna be great, but at least I would be able to place it underneath the video projection, and have slightly higher audio quality, compared to my projector's in-built speakers.However, no matter how I pair them, how close they are to each other, how close they are to my internet router (don't think that makes a difference anyways), the audio lag makes it unusable.I realize this is not the only function, and if I had bought it for some other reason, I would give it more stars. I also understand that the product has other strengths, but for me particularly, is a 1-star review. If you are buying it for a similar reason, skip it.UPDATE: I received an email from Amazon asking me to speak to their customer support to solve the issues. After not being able to improve the lag, customer support explained to me that the lag is expected due to the "low processing power of the Echo and Fire Stick".Although this may be true, I've tried the Fire Stick with other bluetooth speakers, and they work flawlessly. Also, despite experiencing severe audio lag even in the Fire Stick's user interface (selecting apps, movies, and tv shows), SOME apps seem to handle/predict the lag, and result in perfect sync, specifically, DirectvNOW and twitch (you can even notice that the Fire Stick freezes the video momentarily to sync the audio and the video when changing the channel in either app).It might be a combination of the processing power of both devices and the programming of how lag is handled by specific apps, including the main menu (user interface) of the Fire Stick (which also shows an unbearable audio lag).Again, the device might have other great features, but the one I got it for just doesn't work for me, and for this reason I'm giving it a one star review that might help other potential customers looking for the same configuration as me.

title: Sad really....and it had so much potential...
body: Great concept.....poor execution.....Pro's:When it works, it's awesomeCon's:Getting them to work consistentlyI purchased 8 of these as gifts, my advice, don't. Here's a list of why.Connecting them to the internetConnecting them to your networkRe-connecting after the lights flickerApps continuously crash on iPhone & iPad [and yes, I've installed, uninstalled till I'm blue in the face]App is VERY slow.....no matter your network speed [yes....it's the latest version of the app]One more person telling me to read the support/troubleshooting informationI thought I found a fix with the desktop site alexa.amazon.com......nope....no longer works eitherI've tested on at least 8 different networks in helping others set them up. I have 2 in my home and one is always disconnected to the point I've unplugged them both and I have 2 in boxes brand new that will never get set up. Most frustrating piece of "so called tech" I've ever owned. When they're working, you get used to them being there. Connect all your lights, set scenes, etc. Then you say "Alexa, turn on the living room lights" and that dang red light of death shines at you as she gives you her pre-recorded "Sorry, I'm having trouble understanding right now.....please try a little later" Your head drops, your blood pressure rises and you're reminded once again that you've wasted your money, your time and your patience.I purchased these for my elderly mother as a security mechanism, so she simply had to tell Alexa to call me. Trained her on it, and all for naught. I know they're not sold for that, but it gave me a little piece of mind that she could reach me if an emergency did arise.Oddly, you will see the good reviews and be jealous as this doesn't happen for everyone. So who put the rain cloud over my head. Wait.....I have the answer......Amazon! Don't get me wrong. I love Amazon and they have made my life easier in both business and personal. But Alexa is a sliver under my skin I just can't seem to get out. I truly wish you luck my friends and hope you don't have the same experience. And the other 6 I purchased.....collecting dust in friends and families homes because they didn't pay for them so they're not using them any longer......TOO MUCH FRUSTRATION. Not to mention all the "smart" outlets & bulbs purchased in hopes of being the "magic son/father/friend".There....I finally got it out of my system. I'm shopping for a Clapper now as those are more reliable but I'm also considering getting some exercise and turning the dang light on the old fashioned way. Good riddance!

title: eh.
body: I got myself one of these for black Friday when it was on sale. I was really excited about it, and initially, I used it quite often. I always hated the idea of speaking in a room with no one in it, and this is no different, you're just talking and it *should* respond appropriately. Unfortunately, I'd say mine has decreased in it's ability to understand me over time. I'd say maybe 50% of the time, it responds appropriately. 40% of the time, it doesn't respond at all, and 10% it responds with something unrelated.I have used it for games, the best is probably song quiz.I've used it for trivia with the echo buttons, that can be fun.I've used it for the hue lights, that's probably the most useful.I've used it for weather and news, but you can do that on your phone/computer and get a bigger picture of the weather/news faster.I've used it for math.I've used it for spelling.I've used it for a timer, this is useful.I've used it for a thesaurus. Nice feature, but the words are so much more limited than you would get using a computer.I've used it as a dictionary.I've asked questions, many of which is has no data for an answer.I've used it for appointment reminders/calendar, but it's pretty slow to tell them, it's faster to look at the calendar, and you get a bigger view.I've used it for an alarm.I've used it for playing music from my soundbar via online services, and that was pretty cool, and easy to set up, which is a bonus, but I am not sure I see any benefit to using this for streaming music over my xbox. It does stream from Prime which can be usefulSince I got it, and I tried all those things, and probably many more that I don't remember, I noticed that it's ability to respond appropriately has decreased to the point that it's kind of frustrating. At this point, I attempt to use it to turn on the lights, which is great when i don't have to repeat myself 5x, I use it for a timer, and I might use it for a game of song quiz or trivia, but everything else, I do the way I did it before having this device. Even the lights I often end up using a phone app to adjust, simply because I don't want to speak, and worse is when I have to repeat a few times.I was actually logging on to return it, but I missed the date by one day. So, I figured, I would write this review.*UPDATESince writing this review, the alexa has only been a problem for me. It would say things randomly, and answer incorrectly all the time. I finally disconnected it a few weeks ago and put it on a shelf - like a paperweight. Really poor use of $30.

title: POOR WARRANTY NOT RELIABLE RUDE/ INAPPROPRIATE CUSTOMER SERVICE/SUPPORT
body: I purchased this after buying an Echo, Alexa Fire Stick, Amazon tablet with Alexa all with 1 year warranties except this one. Even 1st generation has 1 year. This one, 90 days. Clearly they have no confidence in this product either. Had problems with it continually shutting off and rebooting. That wasnt the worst of it. 1st service rep insisted my 10Mbps internet was too slow for it I told her that is not true and said I have been dealing with this for 1/2 hour . She abrupt replied "no sir, you have only been on the phone for 19 minutes". I told her I was on chat before that for longer, she is the 3rd person I talked to, and am quite aware what time it is. After that comment I asked to speak to someone else. The next person told me he could replace it at the discounted price of $45.99 because the warranty was expired to my surprise. I paid $39.99 for mine. I told him I was looking at he product page on my phone and there was no warranty information. He said I had to use a PC and was there and that I should have seen it. I bought it with the mobile app and asked him to go tot he mobile app and look for himself, there is no warranty disclosure there, if there were I would not have bought it with such poor coverage. After 20 minutes of him essentially calling me a liar, he finally looked and yep, not there. I then took him to Amazon's warranty page to show him everything has a 1 year war...... "oh wait, in tiny little type it says in parentheses, 1st generation only." He said they will have to fix the page for future customers. Not sure how that would help me. Clearly he was done with me, told me he would have his supervisor call me and asked me when. I clearly stated business hours M-F 8 to 5. The next day, Sunday, someone else called me over and over leaving multiple voicemails like a stalker. He stated repeatedly in these voicemails (and I still have them Amazon) "you agreed to be called today and I want to talk to you but you will not answer the phone. Seriously, I was starting to feel threatened. I wish I could post a VM to share, it is creepy. Now it is Monday, business hours between 8-5. No more calls.I love(ed) Amazon and Alexa. I even told the reps after 5 years of Prime Membership and tens of thousands of dollars in purchases you are willing to lose me as a customer over a $40 defective plastic puck? I guess so. Perhaps Bezos needs to stop spending billions on his rockets and robots and pay attention to what is happening to his company. I am not sure I want to do business with Amazon anymore after this bizarre experience!

title: Bait and Switch!!!!
body: Buyer Beware!!! We bought an Echo in Nov of 2016 and we bought a Dot shortly after. We subscribed to the Unlimited Music service. We were able to play any Amazon music we wanted on either of the Echoes and have different music streaming to both at the same time. We added more Echo Dots in other rooms so my wife and I can listen to different things in different rooms all over the house. We have had this setup for months. We would put music on for our young kids while they slept and listen to something else in the kitchen while we talked. Well, all of a sudden on August 29th we lost the ability to stream to more than one device unless it was the same stream using the multi-room playback feature. We thought this was a bug since it happened on the same day they rolled out multi-room playback. It turns out that it is not a bug and they made the change on purpose. The multi-room playback ONLY allows you to listen to the same music stream on multiple devices. We can no longer play music for the kids and listen to our own stuff. In order to return this functionality, we are being told we must subscribe to the Unlimited Family plan. Well, here is the kicker, now you must pay for Prime and the Unlimited Family Music plan in order to use more than one Echo at a time which means you are paying an extra $70 a year above the Unlimited Music plan for a total of $248 a year to use multiple Echoes in a single home if you want more than one playing at a time with a different Amazon stream. You could try to use the other streaming services that the Echo supports for a combination of streams running on other devices but to get commercial free those cost money too. Oh, and don’t get me started on how kids under the age of 13 are not even allowed to use the Unlimited Family Music Plan…great planning on the feature removal Amazon. No we shouldn’t have to lie about our kids age to get what we want.We never would have bought the other Echoes had we known they would change the way the service works. Limiting a product after they have been purchased is a complete rip-off. I’ve never owned a product before where major features are removed instead of added. We were not given any warning nor any compensation for the change. Now we have a bunch of useless Echoes all over the house. Well, not completely useless, they have been reduced to hockey puck looking alarms clocks and timers. I’m currently looking into the cost of moving to Google Home. We will also be significantly reducing our spending on Amazon. You have been warned.

title: very dissapointed after many months, now, more months later its gotten worse.
body: The interface on my android is very poor. there is no search tool for artists and the music unlimited is missnig many key artist's work, such as Santana's Caravanserai. The android app is horribly slow and crashes. Alexa doesn't understand me 50% of the time and reacts to my tv even when she's not being addressed. very poor job so far.Update: My Dot is getting much worse. I have to literally scream at it to stop when I use the timer function. Even when no other sounds are in the room, and I hold it close to me, telling it to stop doesn't work, and even unplugging it and restarting it just brings back the alarm and me having to yell at it.It isn't improving, it's "skills" aren't growing. I understand that Amazon has to hand input its decision making powers rather than any good AI processing. With my phone, when I say "ok google" I almost always get a useful result, though sometimes it's in text rather than sound, that is rare.I also suspect that this device is listening more than we think it is. I will be talking about something and suddenly I start seeing more ads and more stuff about what I was talking about. That isn't my computer or my searches, because I only use Linux and the secure search too start page, which doesn't spy on you.I am dropping this from 2 stars to 1 star.Please understand that I normally tend to lean towards good or reasonably supportive reviews, so the fact that I am this negative about this product and service should be very telling. Why would my device be getting worse rather than better? It's not dusty or dirty, it's not abused or dropped, and it gets a decent amount of use. It also disconnects from my bluetooth speaker more often now.I honestly worry that this device and Amazon, and Facebook, and Google, and Apple are all developing much more extensive and personal telemetry and information on all of us than we can appreciate.
