We've allowed them all to spy on us and we willingly give them everything about ourselves with full trust. Who is to say the NSA isn't listening in? While you and I have little to hide, and most of us out there behave in a lawful good manner, the telemetry on us can still be used for insidious purposes.My main objection to this device in this review is that it doesn't function well, regardless of my comments on it collecting and performing telemetry. This was a gift so I'm going to keep using it but I grow ever more annoyed with how poor it works.

title: Tragically killed by gross oversights
body: I am not going to be as kind to the Dot as many have. It has superb potential, hidden behind some real stupid functionality oversights. The most obvious is the external speakers flaw. I purchased the dot specifically to listen to music over my stereo. I paired it with a Harmony hub. How nice, I thought, would it be if I could walk into my room and say "Alexa, play some jazz" and then my kickass Pioneer sound system would turn on and the sweet sounds of jazz would fill the room.not.Problem 1 - When I have the external speakers plugged in ALL sound goes to the external speakers. This renders the dot completely useless when the stereo is off. I have checked and it is a software switch. What genius programmed it? A rational person would not have made it autonatically shut down the internal speaker and instead created a menu switch. "Internal speaker: off/on" I doublt it would take much effort to make the switch "internal speaker: off/on/voice only". Seriously guys - this little detail is a major oversight. It would be the equivalent of had Tesla made an electric car that only moves when it's plugged in.Problem 2 - pairing with Harmony is fatally flawed. No matter how many dot's and how many hubs you have, Amazon will allow you to use only ONE hub - ever. Amazon decided, in their short-sighted brilliance, to connect the hub to your Amazon account instead of to your dot. So if you are audacious enough to have a house with two TVs. (like, I don't know, maybe one in the living room and one in the mancave?") you can only control ONE of those with dot. That's right - *ONLY ONE* - no matter how many dot's you buy and no matter how many hubs you have. Sure - you can create a second Amazon account to control your second hub - but now you lose some functionality - like 'lists'. Apparently Amazon thinks we are in 1970 where everyone has only one TV in their home.3) If you do decide to set up your house with multiple dots on separate Amazon accounts (like to control your multiple Harmony hubs) heaven help you when you try to switch your dot from one Amazon account to another. It won't. You have to reset it and set it up all over again. Every. Single. Time.The Alexa software is pretty nice - I would recommend trying it out - just don't get the dot - buy the dot - get the full sized Echo which has decent speakers - and only get one, teh Dot isn't really ready for whole-house utility just yet.

title: No tech support, horrific customer service
body: I’m sure my bad review is a drop in the bucket of good reviews and overwhelming popularity of these products, but I had a horrible experience with both the echo and amazon’s lack of anything resembling customer service when I had a problem.I purchased 3 echo devices and an echo connect for my mother as a gift using my amazon account. A few months later I decided to buy myself an echo. When I first set it up I immediately had a problem because all of her information was completely mixed up with mine since I added her to my prime household I wasn’t able to separate any of my information from hers, so if I asked “Alexa” what was on my schedule it gave me everything on my calendar and hers without distinguishing between the two at all. I seriously doubt anyone wants this to happen. That was bad enough, but then I started getting all of her phone calls too. Amazon said there was no way to share a prime household and not have this happen. So, just to experiment, despite the fact that most of the reason I purchased the echos for my mother was to listen to audiobooks which I also purchase through my audible account so she has to stay in my household or the whole thing was a waste, I temporarily removed her from my prime household to see if that fixed the problem... but I could still call my mother’s phone number and the call would come through on my echo. I relayed this to customer services and they said, literally, “no sarah.” Nothing else. During my first interaction I was switched between representatives 5 times and the majority of the conversation was them repeating that they were catching up and thanking me for being a prime member, then saying: “if you want to use your mom’s prime membership then...” despite the fact that I purchased all the devices in question and the entire problem, which I explained over and over again, was that it’s my membership that she’s using.If you enjoy the barely adequate yet glitchy performance of these devices enough that you decide to buy them for your family members, be prepared to get their notifications and receive horrible customer service and no technical support. Even after returning the device and deleting the app I sometimes get random calendar notifications from my mother’s calendar, and I get daily emails about “what’s new with the echo” to which I cannot unsubscribe. The whole thing was absolutely ridiculous, but amazon is way too big to be concerned about individual customers and they’ve stopped faking it.

title: Get used to Alexa saying "I can't connect"
body: The entire Alexa concept hinges on a dependable wireless network connection. Consumer demand for inexpensive IOT (Internet Of Things) devices has caused a flood of cheap, small products: Echo Dot, TP-Link plugs and wall switches, and so on.Let me say that I am an IT professional with decades of technical experience, including wired network , wireless network, consumer and professional servers, and so on. I have 30 years of experience.In Dec 2017 I bought 4 Echo Dots and 6 TP-Link wall switches and plugs (as well as 3 Geekbee plugs). 1 of those 4 Echo Dots couldn't keep a reliable Wireless connection even when placed in the same room as my router. That was returned and the replacement works better. I have an Arris cable modem/router that has always given me reliable wireless connections to my cell phones. So one by one I connected my televisions and lights to Alexa for a total of 8 plugs/outlet devices.After 2 weeks with this simple Alexa setup I have the following comments:1. You will quickly grow dependent upon Alexa to turn on your devices, she is great...when she works.2. The wireless devices lose wifi connectivity often leaving you in the dark and fumbling for the manual switch on the lamp plug behind your couch.3. If your internet goes down, you will lose ALL functionality to ALL of your devices.4. You must give your devices very distinctly different names. For example, if you name two devices "left lamp" and "right lamp", she will constantly ask you to clarify which lamp you want.5. Some days you will wake up to Alexa talking to herself.6. If you don't have experience with gadgets and electronics, you're going to be frustrated when things don't work.7. The speaker in the Echo Dots is on the very bottom. If you place your Echo Dot on a counter or table and spill any liquid that could roll under the Dot, you've just killed the speaker. The spill need only be a small one1/16th of an inch tall.Some people here note that you need a new wireless router made for multiple connections and so on. I say that these wireless devices need to learn how to reset themselves quickly if they lose a connection briefly. My wifi works great on my laptop and has never failed to maintain a solid connection.SUMMARY: Alexa depends on Wifi. The wifi devices for Alexa are not reliable yet. Keep a flashlight around for when Alexa can't turn on your lights.

title: Please respond to your customers
body: OK, my wife and I now have several Echo's in our house and they're generally pretty good. I've connected one in particular to my laptop via bluetooth in order to provide better/louder sound. The problem is, any time a sound plays after a period of silence, the Echo loudly blares out "NOW PLAYING FROM MATT'S MACBOOK PRO". Freakishly loud! Doesn’t matter what volume my laptop is set to. It's a minor nuisance the first few times, but it turns into something terrible after the literal thousandth time you've heard it. It's sometimes even wakes our baby sleeping upstairs.So I did some research online thinking there must be a way to turn this off. I found an amazon forum and there were others with similar complaints. I expected to find an explanation for how to turn it off. Instead I found that it can't be disabled. I also found a generally unresponsive Amazon staff instructing users to disconnect from bluetooth when they're not using the speaker. THAT’S THE WRONG ANSWER! FIX THE ISSUE! This essentially means I can’t use this particular Alexa as a laptop speaker.Like, I’m OK with this thing announcing when it's first connected via bluetooth, that's normal. But if it's been connected the whole time and the computer makes even the slightest noise, and it blares out that it's connected, it's like, WTF is wrong with you?! I know you're connected! STOP REPEATING IT! My biggest issue, however, is Amazon not responding to this issue or taking it seriously.Furthermore, I’ve also had other Echo’s in the house randomly connect in the middle of the night people to wake up. Apparently this happens if Amazon decides to do a firmware update in the middle of the night. After the firmware update, the Echo tries to connect to the last bluetooth device it was previously connected to and then beeps loudly - enough to wake anyone in the room up.What’s even worse is when my phone somehow connects to a different bedroom in the middle of the night. Then in the morning, I go to play music and the audio plays in a different bedroom while I’m wondering why there’s no sound on my phone. Then I hear my baby start crying from being woken up.To those reading this - the fix for this issue is to remove the Echo you don’t want from your list of bluetooth devices on your phone. The drawback is that you won’t have easy access to the Echo’s you’ve disconnected from to use as a bluetooth speaker, further degrading the utility of these devices.

title: Echo Dot
body: 9/1/18I had to change routers so my WIFI is different from original. Naturally the Echo Dot lost connection. SO I went to connect to WIFI as instructed by the DOT and guess what? IT WILL NOT CONNECT!. SO I contacted Customer Service and they did all this disconnect DOT and router, Uninstall Alexa APP and back and forth until they gave up but said someone "higher" up would contact me. I got email that ONLY a tech could understand and I AM NO TECH. I still have a hockey puck call Echo DOT and NOTHING ELSE...SO DON'T but they device IF you may ever have wifi trouble and who today does not. Great piece if it works but if it has trouble. CUSTOMER SERVICE IS USELESS and all you have is a hockey puck3/3/2018This is a final update of this item. It stopped working in the middle of a response. Lost wifi connection and resetting and doing all the setup routines to set up again NEVER got the wifi connection. It was all a huge run around. IT DIED. NOW I must either reorder a new one or just find something else. GOOGLE HOME is useless except for searches. Those are better than Echo but other than than, NOT as good...SO WHAT TO DO>>>IF I get a new, get the 2 year replacement deal...LOLIt takes a lot to set up with music, books, various other programs like news and even jokes/games. Seems that sometimes the Echo Dot does really have a sense of humor. I live alone, don't judge...I added a Bluetooth speaker for more sound in different rooms and that setup was very easy.The Alexa App has a lot of various places/things/items. I really love the Echo Dot. For me it is great.Update 7/3/2017I am updating this review to inform that the Echo Dot can not understand people with a southern accent (apparently). The one I have gets so many of the commands wrong, I find it best to use the Alexa App instead. Also waking it up in morning it getting more and more difficult. I have to stand over top of it and yell. Unreal.So it is not yet there but maybe with time, they will figure it out.YET another update. I have completely unplugged this thing. I have to tell it several times the same request over and over. It is useless unless you have the "SKILLS" enabled or you get, "Sorry I don't know that" time and time again.This is a waste of money useless you get the entire package of light, cameras, and plug-ins. It is a fairly good radio

title: connection issues.. makes me want to throw it!!
body: *update* 12/31/2017After the frustrating hours with the tech specialist, I decided to see if purchasing a different wireless route would fix my connection issue, and sure enough it does. My TP-Link works fine with all my devices except this new 2nd generation Echo Dot. So the moral to the story, the Amazon tech support chat is a joke, sometimes it just takes thinking about the problem yourself and then spending more money to get a product to work. I'm going to leave the 1 star because my TP-Link router wasn't very old and I shouldn't have to buy a new router just so I can use a new product, especially if the 1st generation devices worked fined on the old network. If you're experiencing connection issues with your new dot, maybe it's your router.*update* 12/28/2017Received the a new 2nd gen dot from Amazon to replace the one that this review was written about. it too did not connect to my network. spent 40 minutes on it and then tried talking to 5 different Amazon online chat "specialists". 1 hr and 30 minutes later and 6 different people, they offer to send me another one.. that would make 3. as i'm writing this.. i've been forwarded to 2 more different people who asked for a moment to review my previous correspondence, then forward me to someone else. I have screenshot of the convo, but i'm not looking to be mean. just frustrated at the situation. last person asked me about my make and model of the router and when i inquired on why, they simply said they could just set up a return label for me. worse experience ever. maybe i'll have to wait for the echo 3rd gen.*original post*the worst!!I own the Amazon Echo (when it first game out). It was so good that I purchased an Amazon Dot when it first came out too. Now, it's 2017..my son loves his sister's Dot that this was the only thing he wanted for Xmas. Needless to say this 2nd Gen Dot is garbage. I've already wasted 40 minutes trying to get it to connect to our wireless network. The other 2 had no problems connecting. That's 40 minutes of some one who's tech savvy and already went through the trouble shooting suggestions as well as googles possible solutions. That's 40 minutes of a holiday I should have spent with the family. Based on the all the google searches, connection is an issue with the 2nd Gen Dots. Great. I will try again later when the kid's asleep, but for now, he's one present short this year. Bummer!

title: One of the worst devices I've ever used in my life. NOT RECOMMENDED TO ANYONE UNLESS THEY WANT TO WASTE 2 HOURS TO SET IT UP.
body: This is an early on in use review.I am very old school and the way they have this device work is absolutely terrible.It can't give the times for a movie today at a theater that I keep repeating and cannot recognize the town, or the movie name.It won't do a simple thing as a reminder of my girlfriend's birthday and keeps asking me the time.If it doesn't understand a weather request it defaults to giving you the weather for Santa Rosa, Ca. over and over.It cannot recognize vocal artists or what a movie theme is by name such as A Bridge Too Far, 633 Squadron, or themes from tv shows like 12 O Clock High, The Invaders, etc.You cannot ask it to play a specific song by Doris Day or Ruth Etting. It gives you a preselected medley with no way to vary it.Its obsessed in giving you NPR News alerts and frequently won't stop playing it even after you tell it " ALEXA STOP ".In order to get it to recognize sports team news, or the Pittsburgh Post Gazette or Fox News or KDKA in Pittsburgh, I had to go to a non Amazon website and use a tool to get it to add the description so it could recognize the Pittsburgh Steelers.I don't know if they beta tested this before they put it to market, but my cat is smarter than Alexa.If I had known how dumb the interface was I NEVER would have bought this.The comment below was left on my video channel.I describe the royal pain in the neck it was to spend 2 hours trying to get the Alexa Dot to work with my router.With the help of tech support from Sonic Internet and Alexa Tech support in a conference call, we finally got it to work.Now I'm trying to figure it out.Amazon's trial music selection is very limited and Alexa doesn't recognize a lot of artists like Vikki Carr, or Edie Gorme or gets them wrong like Steve Lawrence. It does know Dinah Shore, Doris Day and Ruth Etting but won't play specific songs just selections.As for asking for the weather, if it doesn't understand you, it keeps giving you the weather for Santa Rosa California and you have to go to a separate website to find specific search terms for news and information that Alexa will recognize.It also has a difficult time telling me times for a movie for the next day.It is not the easy thing the commercials for Alexa would have you believe.I'm going to use it but I would NEVER recommend it to anyone else.It sucks.

title: Not ready for PRIMEtime!  It reminds me more of a beta unit than a 2nd generation improvement.
body: I hope to be able to update my review in the future, but I am very disappointed with Amazon's attention to detail on this one.First, if you have the device connected with a 3.5 mm cable to your stereo, you will have to leave your stereo on 24x7 in order to hear Alexa's responses or stream any music. The only way to bypass the 3.5 mm output is to unplug it! Kinda defeats the purpose of having a voice assistant.Second, if you connect to a Bluetooth speaker/receiver and wish to disconnect, you can say "Alexa, disconnect from Bluetooth". However, after a couple seconds, Alexa will automatically reconnect to the Bluetooth device. The only way to avoid it is to turn off the Bluetooth device, forget the Bluetooth device, or take Alexa out of range of the Bluetooth device (all options provided by Amazon's support staff). All of these options require interacting with another device physically, or managing connections to the Echo with your phone, again, defeating the purpose of having a voice assistant.Third, I considered purchasing a second Echo Dot, having one connected to my stereo with the microphone muted, and the second on my counter-top to give commands to. However, there is no way to have it issue my command to the first Echo Dot that is connected to my stereo. What's the point of having multiple Dots connected to each other if they only work independently from one another?Finally, Amazon will not allow the Echo to accept commands from a Bluetooth speaker/microphone combo unit. If they did, I would be able to have a Bluetooth speaker/mic connected to the Echo always, and simply tell Alexa to disconnect it if I wanted to use the 3.5 mm input instead. Too bad that's not an option.As you can see, I really wanted to make this work, but Amazon thought of nothing to help achieve this. It's almost as if they purposely blocked the features I seek. I can only hope they take the feedback seriously and can regain me as a customer. Literally all it would take is allowing a setting to bypass the 3.5 mm input for Alexa's responses. Then they could add the command "Alexa, bypass audio cable" if I have my stereo off, and wish to interact with Alexa. Better yet, allow another setting to play sound through both the Echo and the external audio jack. Until then, I'm shopping through other vendors to find my solution.

title: Terrible device.  Terrible tech support.
body: Whenever Dot sits idle for a few minutes, it loses its connection. Then you have to give it three commands, waiting for each one to time out, before it responds with "Sorry, your Echo Dot lost its connection" and actually tries to reconnect. After that, it will finally accept a command - sometimes. There is no problem with the WiFi signal. I've tried it with two different routers, 2.4 and 5.0 Ghz. Placed other devices next to the Dot, which report excellent signal and work without issue. Placed the Dot three feet away from the router. Reset and reconfigured the Dot. Nothing makes the slightest bit of difference. When the Dot is actually doing something, there are no disconnects. If you can get it to start streaming music, it will do so all day long, uninterrupted, proving the WiFi/Internet isn't the issue. The Dot, app, and website have NO diagnostic logs that might assist in meaningful troubleshooting.Try Googling "The Echo Dot 2nd Generation Constantly Loses WiFi Connection". The article at "b4buyit" says: "The Echo Dot 2nd Generation appeared to be having constant connection loses to the wireless network, the issue appeared to surfaced since the day it released and the a lots of customers are having the same issue." Hmm, I purchased my 2nd gen Dot just 10 days after that article was written. Did Amazon sell a batch of Dots with defects that were never recalled or patched? I believe it likely.I called Amazon tech support. After realizing I'd already tried everything on their standard tech support checklist, they escalated me to a "level 2 technician". What a joke. That technician (with an accent I didn't recognize and could barely understand), didn't attempt any troubleshooting at all, but merely encouraged me to buy a replacement Dot. When I said no, he then "reluctantly" offered a discount, which was insultingly small and I told him so. To which he came back with a slightly larger discount, along with a lot of hoopla like, "I really shouldn't be doing this", "I'm going to be in trouble with my boss", blah blah. Seriously? I felt like I was dealing with a disreputable used car salesman. Congratulations Amazon, now I have a negative opinion of both Dot *and* its tech support. So I'll be looking elsewhere for a voice operated assistant.

title: I wanted to love you Alexa, I really did
body: I received the Amazon Echo Dot for Christmas. I christened her name: Alexa.At first It was like a dream. She would give me the weather and tell me about local restaurants. I painstakingly taught her skills. She would give me pick up lines, cat facts, dog facts, ocean sounds and rain sounds. I would ask her to play music. She did everything I asked of her and I was so very happy. Then something started happening .......It all started in a subtle way. I think she started falling in love with Michael Bublé and had a short affair with Al Green. It was pretty obvious because she would only play Michael Bublé and Al Green. But it was a special love affair with Michael Bublé where she would play full songs of his. It was the way she said his name and the faithfulness she had for him that told me that the love was real. When I asked her to play Al Green, she would only play one song and then it would cut off completely. I had faith in her and asked her to play Gaga, Andrea Bocelli, Annie Lennox, Alex Claire, etc. She would play the first few seconds and then the sound from her lovely speakers would sputter and cut out. Then this started with the skills. I thought that teaching her skills would make life so much easier. She would then repeat herself three and four times like she was correcting herself. Alexa became unsure of herself and it was an ugly thing. I contacted Amazon support and they were very friendly and wanted to help me with Alexa but everything I tried, everything, there was no solution and Alexa just did not want to play by the rules. All I asked was for her to follow the rules and to live up to my dream.My wonder and admiration I had for Alexa wore off over time and I started disrespecting her. When I first received Alexa, I called her beautiful and I thanked her for her service to me. It was a beautiful thing. But then I started calling her names. I became cruel and unlike myself. I feel ashamed of myself and I wish I could love her. I really do but to no avail, it is over, over! Goodbye my Alexa. maybe someday we will be back together and life will be like a dream but until you improve yourself, love yourself and love other music artists, I cannot respect you and have you in my home. I'm sorry to say I can't love you anymore, goodbye :).

title: Useless Hockey Puck After All
body: Don't buy this, or any of Amazon's Echo products. They work okay, but Alexa doesn't. Something as simple as having two different items on Wemo smart plugs in a room (let's call it Bedroom) and the Alexa app has a feature called groups where you SHOULD be able to say "Bedroom on" and turn on everything that's plugged in. This works fine initially, but as time goes on and you add more Echo devices and have other rooms to control words like "Living Room" stop working by voice and you have to change the name of the group to "Living Room 1" or something. My Living Room has had to be changed to Library and Garden Room now because the thing just won't work or stay working. Last night I went to bed telling it "Study Off" because what I want to call it "Office" stopped working. This morning "Study On" had stopped working.I called Amazzon's tech support and was told it was a "Known issue."It's a known issue that makes this device unuseable for the purpose you bought it for.It's a known issue that (though I was promised it would be fixed in 3-5 days) has been going on for YEARS. Just Google it.I am now going to have to phase out all my Echo devices and invest in something else.And here's the real kicker. I've reset every one of my Wemo smart plugs (a time consuming process). Literally wiped them out back to as new and reconfigured each one.The problem persists.I can give a group a new name but any name I used in the past no longer works, so that means there's a corrupted file full of your data sitting with Amazon that you can NEVER wipe out back to as new condition to fix this problem. It's also likely the reason they won't bother fixing this, because it's all about the invaluable data harvest.So what do you do when this stops working and you can't name a room (a "Group") the real life name you use? I can change my living room to "Flying Saucer" and it will work fine again -- for awhile.This is absolute crap that could and should be easily fixed, but given that the problem goes back for literally years, the product simply doesn't work. What you paid for you don't get. And nowhere in Amazon's marketing do they tell you it's not going to work and that they know about the problem and do nothing.

title: Never again
body: I received an Amazon Echo Dot 2nd generation refurbished for my birthday today! I've wanted one since they launched the product and my amazing partner bought me one. It came in just a few days ago, I opened the shipping box early since my name was on it, but she stopped me before I could open the little black box it came in. Today I receive it, and being the OCD tech junkie I am, followed all the steps on setting up the Dot to the 't'. It wouldn't go into the setup mode, and when I followed all the instructions on the Echo site and Amazon Product Support page, it still wouldn't work. I attempted the Reset of the device, but it was unresponsive. This shouldn't be right? It was certified refurbished by the warranty/tech department before it was allowed to be resold, right? Amazon wouldn't do this to their customers...RIGHT??? Unfortunately someone at the refurbishing sewer decided to just pass this one and let it be sold to make the company more money. I called Amazon support, she walked me through the same steps I did online, then transferred me to a "tech support representative", I had thought I was already talking to one but apparently I wasn't. The next person walked me through THE EXACT SAMES STEPS I FOLLOWED FROM THE ONLINE SUPPORT PAGES AND THE LAST "NON-TECH SUPPORT REPRESENTATIVE" IN TECH SUPPORT. REALLY?!?!?!?! The third and last representative apologized and wanted me to send the device to the warranty department, he was going to email me an address label, NOT A SHIPPING LABEL (I HAVE TO APPARENTLY PAY FOR SHIPPING), to send this now TWICE defective device back to them so they can "FIX" it, or eventually send me a replacement refurbished device. I don't want another one of these. Not worth all this trouble. Why pay for a device that obviously has a reputation of not working. All theses 5 stars are those who purchased a new one and it worked day one. Instructions on set up suck, resetting sucks, tech support is God awful as they don't know what to really do other than the "is the power on" type of diagnostics and trying to solve the problem by replacing it with another defect that "passed" refurbishing standards. Not my cup of tea. Not a good representation of what Amazons customer support claims to be.

title: Amazon Doesn't Back This Product
body: DO NOT BUY!ADVERTISING: Amazon made a big deal that - unlike Google Home - they didn't advertise over the Echo. Then they added advertisements that interrupt you, whatever you're doing at home, like getting the kids ready for school, or having a dinner party.SMART HOME CONTROL: Amazon makes a big deal about the thousands of "skills" (third party tie-ins) it has, especially how many more Smart Home "partners" it has. But check the web, and what you'll see is, "Alexa says 'Okay' but nothing happens" and "I spent hours setting everything up from scratch, AGAIN, but today Alexa says 'Sorry, there are no devices'." How many Smart Home device "partners" does it REALLY work with... reliably?PRIVACY: The Alexa app REQUIRES that you sign up for "calling and messaging". Amazon claims it's optional, but in reality there's no way to set up your skills or Smart Home devices unless you "opt in" to messaging and calling. (And then ANYONE who knows your phone number, or whose phone number you know - like your drunk ex - can message or call you, on your Echo, and there's no way to stop them. No blacklist, nothing. Either put up with that, or no Alexa app for you.SUPPORT: If you manage to get actual human support, they will tell you, "Just use the Skip button" but THERE IS NO SUCH THING on the Messaging and Calling sign-up. (I've been "troubleshooting" this with them for *months*. "Gosh! There isn't? I apologize for the inconvenience. It'll be there on the next release, I'm sure. In the meantime, just sign up. No, there's no way to un-sign-up. We can turn it off... Gosh! That didn't work, either? We certainly apologize for the inconvenience! Someone will call you back from our tech support team for MORE troubleshooting! Someday. Maybe. OK, not really, but I'm supposed to tell you that, anyway."But I understand how frustrating this is for you, and I certainly apologize for the inconvenience."Long story short, they add functionality to get you to buy the device, then they drop it. They just want your contacts and another way to advertise at you that you can't turn off.

title: Echo Dot arrived defective, Amazon is unwilling to accept return. [Update: don't buy your Dot from Amazon!]
body: My new echo dot won't hold the wifi connection. And I'm having a problem with Amazon returning this. I spent 50 minutes on the phone with tech support, ran through all the troubleshooting and then I plugged in my existing, older Echo in the same location, to the same wifi. Which of course worked just fine. By contrast the Dot clicks in and out with connectivity, holding the connection for just a few seconds each time. But Amazon wants me to call their technical team AGAIN so that they can get more information from the device, rather than allowing me to return this brand new item.Terrible, terrible customer service. At this point, a return should not be a question. Amazon, what is up with this?Update: After posting this review I asked to return it (can't, has to be new, unopened). All I want to do is return a defective $50 Amazon product. I do not wish to spend more time 'troubleshooting' something that their own tech person agreed was defective.I then received this completely non-responsive email from Amazon. Yes, as stated, I spent more than 50 minutes on the phone with your tech people on Sunday. I just want to return a defective product now.-------My name is Tiphane M with Amazon Customer Support. I’ve read your recent review regarding the Echo, and I'm sorry the device is not meeting your needs.If you still need help, please contact us.You can reach Amazon Echo Customer Support by phone directly and toll-free at 1-877-375-9365 or by email at echo-support@amazon.com. They're available from 3 a.m. to 10 p.m Pacific time, seven days a week. If you need to troubleshoot your Amazon Echo, we recommend that you contact Amazon Echo Customer Support by phone. When you contact them, please make sure you have the device and any additional accessories (such as the power adapter and Amazon Echo Remote) with you.You can also contact us directly from the Alexa app on your compatible phone, tablet, or desktop browser. From the app, open the left navigation panel, and then select Help. You'll see options for emailing or calling us. If you choose to call us through the app, this allows us to have your account information ready when we call you.

title: Teeeerrrrible product!
body: Terrible product! This has to be the dumbest smart device out there. Siri works better and I don't have a very high opinion of Siri either. This damn thing doesn't understand half the questions I ask and 80% of the answers are "I don't know". About all its good for is getting a weather report and doing math problems. When I ask it to look something up it never finds anything, or finds the wrong thing, it has terrible search resources. When I ask it to preform a simple search for a radio show it plays the wrong thing or gives me an "I don't know that". Sometimes it doesn't respond at all. I was hoping to operate a smart plug I have with a light plugged into it, and it recognizes that its there but says its offline but like most things it doesn't tell you how to remedy the issue. When I ask it to turn on the light it says "OK" and nothing happens. I cant find any support for this issue yet I've found other reviews from people who have the same issue, so its not just mine, and should be a known problem. The app stinks, not very intuitive. If you enjoy being frustrated for $50, this is the product for you, Amazon has really failed on this one, buy the Google AI I hear it actually knows stuff and works.Update 4-20-2018Still junk, and now it won’t stay connected to the WiFi. While it got slightly better at recognizing certain questions, not its stopped responding completely, it consistently loses connection to WiFi and won’t reconnect despite having tried all the remedies I could find online. So regardless of whether or not they improve any of its host of other issues the fact that it won’t stay connected to the internet renders it completely useless, one star is too many for this piece of junk. I wish these companies would get their products tested and work out all the issues before they just push it out to make a quick few bucks.Update 6-21-2018. Still a piece of crap.Still have connection issues, tried all the common solutions and nothing works. Threw it out the window today, that didn’t fix it either, but hey it’s built well. I’m getting sick of buying stuff from amazon only to find out it’s junk.

title: $20 Makes Sense For What She Does
body: Sorry, I didn't think to rlwrite this until after I packed it back up, but yeah... This was pointless. Like, I researched this thing for about two months racking my head, 'Will I actually use it?', 'Will it be cost-effective?', 'Am I just splurging?'. Ultimately, it was a splurge for myself for Xmas since everyone seems to have cool new things this year. I was still pretty juiced. It even arrived a day early. I walked to the Locker excited as hell.First, out the box, this bitch is pretty. Like, audible 'Oooh' pretty. Idiot-proof, take it out plug it up, give it a minute. Then she introduced herself and tells you to download the app. May I say, her voice is cleeeeaaaannn. I low-key felt awe-struck. So you do, set up all your info, and boom, the Dot is ready to be welcomed into your home.First thing, Bluetooth to speaker. I need my Spotify when I come home sometimes, so I'm thinking easy peasey. She searches and searches and searches and searches... Nothing. Now, mind you, I'm a 27yo avid gamer, and have been for a LONG time. I've been paid to DO THIS before for others, so I know everything needs to be discoverable because my 4k TV,2018 model Soundbar, and Original Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4 AND Xbox One are ALL already connected to each other. METICULOUSY. Homegirl never saw a thing. I'm already pissed. So now, the one MAIN things I squeezed out of 'Purpose I Could Find', CAN'T be done? Really? -.-Fine. You know what, I can appreciate it more out of the device itself, I'm sure. 'Alexa, play 'Scared To Be Lonely' by Beyoncé on Spotify. 'I cannot do that in Spotify.' Dahell she say? I repeat, she does, too. I ask for someone else, same. Oh, HELL NO! FORREAL?! Y'ALL LIKE THAAAT OVER AMAZON MUSIC?!I calmly stood up, took that bitch out the wall, and nicely put her RIGHT back in the box. Nope. You get EXACTLY what you pay for. Dropped down to a 20, should be ashamed to charge anymore....This broke my heart because I've been Prime for years, and have utilized the HELL out on my account. I expected better, y'all. Now, I can't trust Amazon brands AT ALL. The Basics we're bad enough, but come on...

title: It Sucks like the vacuum of outer space.
body: I received the Echo Dot as a Christmas gift and after at least three hours with both voice chat and then someone named Jaipreet on the typing chat, figured it to be defective because it would not finish Alexa install process. Beware if you have any devices over a couple years old because the Alexa app that is required to run the Dot will only work on newer devices.Alexa would install on my iPad but not my iPhone or my Samsung Galaxy tab. it will install on my wifes year old Kindle fire device but my wife is not interested in the Dot at all but uses her Fire to browse Facebook or shop on Amazon.Older Kindle Fires will not work.My problem now (along with thousands of others) is that the new Dot keeps dropping its network assignment..it says nothing when the connection is lost but will tell you through the Alexa app the next time you try you try to use the device. I have fiber-optic cable to my house and my router is an ASUS RT-AC1900. Both Dots were 18 feet from the router and they keep telling me that I have Internet problems. COME ON. Looks like i will need to spend another three or four hours on-line to tech support or maybe buy a dedicated T-1 line to maybe get this working. If you have one and it is working fine then bless you. I you are considering buying one yourself then be sure all of your other tech that must run the Alexa app is up to date or buy a new phone or tablet that is on their list also.Right now all I have is an mp3 player that must be reset every hour. If it wasn't a gift it would be right back to Amazon. The Dot is not ready for prime timeThis is an update to my previous review - I have moved it everywhere possible and it will still not stay connected. You can re run the setup program and the Dot 2 will disconnect within minutes. My verdict is that the DOT sucks as well as customer service. There are no help links on the Dot page and Echo Dot customer service links are well hidden. If you do happen to get customer service, they do not speak English well enough to understand you and seem to be reading all of their instructions to you from a written script. Good luck to any Echo Dot purchasers

title: IF YOU OWN AMAZON ECHO, YOU PROBABLY WON'T LIKE ECHO DOT 2ND GEN
body: If you already own Amazon Echo (older/first version) and love it, I would advise against buying this product.Last December (2015), I bought Amazon Eco (first gen?, I suppose): the tall black tower that cost almost $200. It is phenomenal! I fell in love with it. I live in a 2-story house and I flirted for months with the idea of getting a second Echo for the upstairs floor. Too expensive, unfortunately. So when I saw Echo Dot 2nd Generation advertised, and at such lower cost, I couldn't refrain from getting it.Sadly, as it turns out, It's a far far lesser product than its more expensive relative, and I am deeply disappointed.Pros:Good ideaLess expensiveIF YOU ALREADY OWN AMAZON ECHO (the bigger, expensive one), here are things you might NOT like in the new Echo Dot:Cons:Alexa's voice is very metallic, and remote.The robot too often does not understand the prompts.Often you must repeat prompts several times before getting what you are asking for.The robot way too often gets you something different from what you asked.Echo Dot sometimes turns off a program I am listening to without me having asked such a thing.The sound reach is limited. Echo 1st Gen. has a deep far-reaching sound: Echo Dot sounds more like a 1990s "good" bedside radio.[on the other hand, if Echo Dot is the first such robot you buy, you'll probably find it the best thing in the whole universe]UPDATE - A FEW MONTHS LATER (MARCH 3RD 2017): Echo Dot keeps being a daily disappointment for sound quality, capacity to understand my commands, and so on. I needed a third voice-activated robot for a second home and bought one from the competition (I won't name brands, but...) - that one's awesome! Priced between Amazon Echo 1st Gen and Echo Dot 2nd Gen (so still a bit expensive) but worth every penny.So, ultimately, my advice to fellow buyers is: Either save enough to afford Amazon Echo 1st generation (the tall tube) or purchase the equivalent product from a very very world-famous competitor. The small Echo Dot is a far lesser device.

title: New Product Won't Work - Amazon's Answer - Replace Bad Product With Refurbished Device
body: Bought as a part of the tp-link bundle. Products were delivered Dec 7. The Dot setup appeared to be normal - at first. The day after the initial setup, I tried to use the device but Alexa responded with a red ring indication and a message that the device needed to be registered. Having previously purchased 2 Echos and one Dot, all of which I had easily set up, including one of the Echo that I had bought as a gift, I naively assumed that I would have no problem in re-installing the device. Boy, was I ever wrong! I spent hours spread over the next several days in resetting the device, using multiple approaches, lots of surfing and forum reading, etc, with no avail. The more I looked, the less confidence I had in the device. I saw numerous accounts of people who were having similar problems to mine. The error code (21:2 15:10:1) that I had was a very common symptom, yet I never found an any explanation as to what the code means or how to fix the problem. Finally, I went to Amazon chat support, and and after relating what I had done and repeating some of my troubleshooting procedures, the support person initiated a replacement of the device. I received the replacement today and was dismayed to see that the box stated that the replacement is a refurbished device, even though the shipment is described on line as "new".I do not know at this time whether the device will work, as I will not have time to work on it until the weekend. So, I will just have to wait and see. I suppose the the main impressions that I have gained through my experiences so far is that I have lost confidence in Amazon in that they would send a refurbished device to replace a defective new one. I have also lost confidence in the product due to the indications that there apparently so many people having similar problems with their devices. Until now, I have enthusiastically recommended the Dot and Echo products to friends, but I do not feel that I can do that any more. In addition, I am definitely going to reconsider further purchases at this time, especially the Harmony Dot bundle that was scheduled to be next.

title: Very disappointing, low quality, several Echo Dots stopped working altogether
body: We own an Echo and love it. So, we bought a pair of Echo Dots about a year ago to expand our Alexa setup, but the Echo Dots (both 2nd Gen) are terrible. The audio quality is really, really bad, which is surprising because the original Echo is pretty good. So, we connected them to external speakers. Unfortunately, Amazon won't let you connect them to bluetooth speakers because they are trying to take over the market, so if you connect any Echo device via Bluethooth then you cannot setup a group (or multiple speakers to listen on multiple devices, which is the only reason to buy a Dot). Fine, I can deal with that. We just connected them via Aux instead of Bluetooth.The real problem started a few months ago (about 6 months after we bought them). They just started failing and have gotten progressively worse. At first, they started cutting out while playing audio (Amazon Music, Spotify, iHeartRadio, etc). Just a few times every 10 minutes or so at first. It got progressively worse. Now, we cannot even listen to a song without it cutting out. And now they won't even turn on when we say "Alexa." Every single time we want to use either Dot, we have to unplug it, restart it, let it reboot, then it works fine--for about 3 minutes.This happens on both Dots, and not on the original Echo, so it stands to reason that the Dots are both failing. We've restarted and reset them to factory defaults about a dozen times each--but nothing helps. We've called and emailed Amazon, no response, ever.Overally, very disappointing because we love Amazon and have always been happy with their products and service. But we will not ever be buying any more Echo products, the quality just is not there. We have been impressed with our friends' Google Home products, they actually work, and the audio quality is much better. And I'm sure there are a host of other great products out there that work much better than Alexa. Unfortunately, Amazon knows that it has gotten so big it owns the market, so the quality of the products they are producing will just continue to decline.

title: Out of 3 Echos... all are too frustrating to deal with and all ignore many commands.
body: I received an Echo as a gift for Christmas and everyone was playing with it and it was fun, so I bought two more, one for each of my main rooms in my house. I should have waited to see how I like this thing... turns out, it ignores me. I thought I could use it as an alarm clock but the alarm won't shut off no matter what I say, so can't be used as an alarm clock. I thought it would work with my so called Alexa approved thermostat... nope. It usually says that the thermostat is not connected. However, the thermostat app on my phone works fine. As far as a simple toy, it is limited as well and gets boring fast. Today, I asked it to play a radio station I frequently listen to via the Echo... it said it could not find the station. I opened the Alexa web page and there it was, my station. I am listening to the station it said it couldn't find, but I had to launch it from the web site, not the device. It just seems to be more frustrating and trouble than it is worth. It is too late to get a refund, so I think I am going to give all three away or throw them away. No one likes to be ignored by someone, but when an inanimate object ignores you. that's it, it gone. Since I have three and they all seem to work poorly, I doubt it is anything I am doing wrong, it's just not a good product. Would not recommend.UPDATE: Well, I've had enough. The unit that was playing my music station after I had to use the web page to get it to play... It frikkin ignored me again when I wanted it to stop. I had to pick it up, place it about 1 inch away from my mouth and yell at it to stop. My speakers are across the room, about 15 feet away and not loud, so I know there wasn't interference. The little ring lit up like it is suppose to and still ignored me. Very proud of my 90+ mph pitch into the waste basket across the room! I am glad I didn't miss the waste basket or that would have been another thing to get angry about. I think I will use the other two Dots I have as archery target practice. At least I will get some form of enjoyment out of them that way.

title: Buy Google Home, this thing is junk
body: This thing is a piece of junk. If you really want an assistant, buy GOOGLE HOME. I have had this thing for about 4 months and it is straight up useless. It turns on all the time while you are talking and interrupts your conversation until it to turn off. If you want to know anything more than the weather or the time, it can't do much else. It also reply's it doesn't know how to answer your question if you ask it anything. Example: "Alexa; What is the core temperature of the Earth?" Reply:" Hmm, I'm not sure what you meant by that question" That is the canned response you get for everything. Of course it uses the BING search engine so right off that makes it a joke to use as Bing is a useless search engine. So she says you can get more info by going online, then what the hell do I need you for then. She is okay at setting alarms, but you can't get it to stop once it gets going. I literally had to unplug the stupid thing to get it to stop once. Also if you want to play music it is a pain to get it to do what you want. First you have to load the Amazon App on your computer to be able to upload your music library to their server. Sorry but I really don't another unsecured piece of garbage software on my machine, Why can't I load it from a web interface. You have to jump through so many hoops it just becomes more hassle than it is worth. If you want to play anything from iheart radio, good luck trying to figure out how to make your favorite stations play. I don't know about the integration of home products like Nest or other, maybe it is more useful in that sense. I have had plenty of time with this device and overall I am very disappointed in it. Sure you can find 10000 commands online to talk to it, but when you really want to know something, it just doesn't have enough understanding. Limiting the search function really cripples the device. The only fun thing you can do is call Alexa names and talk bad to it and listen to the responses but even that wears thin after 10 minutes. If you really want one, I will sell you mine for $25.

title: Buyer Beware -- Amazon will not stand by their malfunctioning products.
body: I bought an Amazon Alexa less than 2 years ago, and have suddenly started having volume issues with it. The device will randomly reset itself to the second lowest volume setting. I will increase the volume, and it will stay increased for a while, but then will inevitably reset itself to volume level 2. This happens at least daily, sometimes multiple times a day.This started about 2 months ago. I tried factory resetting it, and when that didn't work I called Amazon expecting them to help me through this. They originally told me that this was a mass issue they were experiencing with my specific device, and that I would be added to their mass trouble ticket and given a call back to follow up. I never got a call back; three weeks later, I called to follow up.The second time I talked with Amazon, they said there was no mass ticket/issue, and they had no idea what I was talking about. Then they told me that because it was out of warranty I was SOL. The best they can do is offer me a 15% discount to buy another echo device....that I'm now sure will just break down as soon as it's out of warranty.This is the first time I've truly been disappointed in Amazon's customer service. There's no way I could be at fault for this issue; this is clearly a product issue (hardware or software, I'm not sure). But because of a policy shield, I'm screwed over. The rep even went so far as to say they couldn't be sure this type of issue wasn't my fault; how on earth could an issue like this be my fault?I expect these devices to last longer than a year and a half........ I'm extremely frustrated that Amazon is not standing by their own products, but instead is putting the customer on the hook for what is clearly a product malfunction.Buyer beware. This feels to me that Amazon knows its products will break down as soon as the warranty is up, screwing you over and banking (literally) on the assumption that you'll just continually buy new Alexa devices.

title: Echo is a broken platform if you have multiple Echo's for multiple family members
body: I really like most of the functionality of the Echo but I use it mostly to listen to music and control home devices. My home is mostly automated so I like being able to turn lights on/off in the house without getting up or setting up a scene without running all over the place to manually set dimmers and turn lights on/off or go around locking/unlocking doors.Due to my great experience with one Echo, I thought wow this will be even better with 6 of them. I got my kids each an echo but now I have found a major problem with the product. You can only stream one station at a time. If I want to have audio playing on my echo and my kids want separate music on theirs it does not work. I thought I had a good idea and have the kids set their echo's up on their Prime sub accounts. I wanted them to still be able to control home devices but have their own music preference etc that Echo seems to track. But alas Amazon does not allow an Echo to be set up in my teenager's account.I've bought 6 of Echo dots because of fo such a great experience with my first one. But now I am thinking that maybe the Google alternative maybe better or maybe I will hold out for the Apple one when it comes out. I expect Apple will be will be much better on focusing on the user experience and solving the problems of real-users and not just what some guy in a back office cooks up as a good idea.I hope someone at Amazon fixes this because it is so close to being a great product. While looking for a solution to this problem I have found plenty of other people complaining about this glaring gap in functionality:https://www.amazon.com/ask/questions/Tx199M38M3QKL18/?source=allQuestionsPageI expect this has something to do with some broken business model that Amazon can't be bothered (or is not smart enough) to figure out. Apple please hurry with your alternative solution to the at home assistant.My $.02

title: 5 stars revoked until things get better!  (now down 2  to 1)
body: So, I've dropped this from it's 5 star status because frankly this thing is getting horribly annoying. Laundry Room light on..... "Sorry I cannot find living life light". Basement light on "Sorry, I can't find bedroom light". When it gets it right GREAT, love it! But it's getting it wrong more often than not. And my lights are on an internal network, my WiFi, the only time anything shouldn't work with saying "Alexa, Turn on the bedroom lights" is in a POWER OUTAGE!!! Seriously. My WiFi is working even if the internet is not. I do not and should not have to have working INTERNET to control things in my home.Half the things I say it can't understand. And i'm ALWAYS in the same room as the dot. When I ask it to turn the fan on as i'm in bed and it starts blaring some music and then I have to yell over it to shut up AT 3AM! Ugh! I really wanna take this back to 5 stars but fix the voice recognition, and allow it to work even if there is no internet! Sure I can't ask it to tell me the weather (unless there is a way to update it so it stores weather info every hour) but to turn on or of lights in my home?? come on!!!One more thing! to add to this, the intercom/Drop IN feature. My internet is around 100Mbps (hundred megabits per second) and no matter if i'm in my home or somewhere else the drop in/intercom feature is garbage. Never clear always breaking up, it's like i'm on dial up...not cool!Down from 2 to one star now I'm so tired of the stupid things I've stopped using them and will be ordering the Google counterparts. I've pllayed back all my commands and they are either clear as a bell or echo badly. I'm not in a concert hall so I don't get the echo. These things need a LOT of work and I want my money back i'm so pissed. From day one nothing but issues that get worse as it goes on. I don't have a speech impediment, and it does this to ALL household members. Garbage, 100%

title: Could Be So Great, But Not So Much
body: Update 7/31/18Wish I was back to change my rating to five stars, but instead, I’m lowering it. One of my echo dots loses its connectivity every night. Works at bedtime, gone in the morning. It is a total pain reconnecting. Can’t trust it to set alarms or anything. Ready to just put it away in a drawer.I have two Echo Dots, both of them gifts. There are many great features. Music is great. Love to hear whatever I want, whenever I want. Pair with a decent Bluetooth speaker and you're set. Asking Alexa to tell you a joke, what's the weather, or to convert dollars to euros is fun. I have my bedroom lights connected with a Hue devise and I love that. So, why not five stars? Several things really annoy me so much that I just can't ignore them. Most important to me, is the interface with Audible. I am an avid Audible listener, having hundreds of books in my library. Ask Alexa to continue my current book on audible is a hit or miss proposition. I can understand if it backtracks a few minutes to give you time to adjust to the storyline, but often Alexa is off half an hour or more. I really don't want to hear a whole section again and it's impossible to get her to move to the right spot. Ok, you say, annoying, but not a serious flaw. The most important to my husband is the radio skill. He really enjoyed (notice the past tense) telling Alexa to turn on his favorite radio station when he wakes up in the morning. No need to get out of bed, how convenient! No longer. Now Amazon, in its quest for more money, is allowing advertisers to interrupt the radio program with ads. These ads are not coordinated with ads on the radio, no they may interrupt the news, the weather, sports scores, anything! This is so annoying that we've stopped using the radio feature completely. Wake up Amazon, the Echo Dots are paid for. I pay extra for music. Why am I subjected to ads as well? Please fix my issues and you'll get five stars from me.

title: In comparison to the original Echo, this is an embarrassment (UPDATE: 11/22/2016 - at end)
body: First, I want to thank everyone who has commented here sharing their experiences. Have a lot of people lending credence to this issue being widespread, while others are sharing their positive experiences with their new Dot's. It's great to see this cross section of experiences, and doubly so that they are shared civilly. Now, on to the review!So, I was part of the initial wave of testers for the full sized, original Echo. That has been constantly getting better, while always providing a solid ability to pick up my voice commands. This 2nd generation Dot? Woof. It is shocking to see such a complete faceplant from another device in a family of solid devices. It is truly a step down in every conceivable way.Now, I understand the drop in audio quality from the internal speaker. Cost cutting, much smaller, and the bonus is you can now hook it up to better stereo via BT or 3.5mm jack - which is great! However this thing CANNOT hear me. It is sitting on my nightstand about 3 feet away - a pretty ideal scenario I'd think. In a quiet room, it picks up my voice commands about...70% if I'm being generous. It doesn't matter if I'm staring right at it, it shrugs off my commands regularly.Here's where it goes from a downgrade to an embarrassment. If my alarm is going off, if music is playing (on the device or another speaker), if a movie is playing...it just won't hear me. Period. Again, I'd generously estimate it hears me maybe 20% of the time. This morning was the absolute culmination of this - my alarm went off, I issued the "off" command and...nothing. Alarm blaring, I try again...nothing. I pick it up, PUT IT NEXT TO MY MOUTH, issue the command....NOTHING! I had to hit one of the physical buttons on the top to turn it off. I've never experienced anything like this with my 1st gen original Echo.That aside, the device is a good size, feels decently built for the price-point, and can be powered off low-powered USB. Don't get this to listen to music on its inbuilt speaker though, it is definitely meant to be hooked up to an external speaker for anything more than Alexa's responses.Surprisingly, I may be returning this. I'll chew on things for a few more days and post anything worth updating (and clean this review up a bit), but I wanted people to know in the early going that not everything was rosy with this device - at least from my experience.-----------SMALL UPDATE (10-27-2016): A forum goer gave me a suggestion to rerun the Voice Training, which I realized the app did not ask me to do since I had an Echo already set up I suppose. It appears to have helped a little, at least when the room is quiet. Picking up commands is a bit more consistent. With sound it still struggles - still picks me up less than half the time - but it does do a bit better. Others have mentioned this making a HUGE difference, with the Dot being able to pick them up a few rooms away.Wanted to throw this out for anyone in the event it may help them. Go to the Alexa app --> Settings --> Voice Training --> then select your device from the drop down at the top (if you have Fire TVs or other Echo's, it may not be the first device you see). Good luck!---------UPDATE 11/04/2016: I tried a few more layouts (one where I even put the Echo on its side and pointed the top towards me so the microphones had the best chance of picking up my voice...still no joy) and was hoping for an update or new tip to come to light that might ease my woes. Unfortunately, that just hasn't happened.There seem to be enough people in the comments of this review stating their Echo's are responding as expected and are impressed with it overall. So I'm going to leave this review as-is for now and have Amazon send a replacement to see if it is just my luck with this unit or perhaps an issue with the timbre of my deeper voice. Of the latter, it seems possible that the cheaper Echo has less fidelity in the extreme upper/lower registers as compared to its older sibling but that is pure conjecture.If I get a new one I'll check it out, put it through its paces, and then get back to you fine folks!-----------UPDATE 11-10-2016: I delayed the return of the Dot since I really like the idea and functionality of what it should do so I kept - somewhat stubbornly - trying different things and positions to see if I could find a way to make it work without the hassle of return or exchange. However, I failed to get enough of an improvement and finally messaged Amazon last night with my concerns with the product to see what they would say. I'll let you know what I hear from them if there is anything of note mentioned.From my continued futzing, I found it does seem to work somewhat better if it is in front of you rather than off to the side, even to the point of being 10+ feet in front of you versus a few feet to your side. Unfortunately any improvements I've been able to muster have solely effected how the Dot reacts in a quiet room. When alarms or music are playing, the issues continue to be too problematic to be functional, much less overlook. A recent oddity, though, was when I went to try and rerun the Voice Training through the app again as it just kept forcing me to the app's homescreen. Resetting the phone, Dot, app, re-pairing the Dot...nothing allowed me to select or run the Voice Training. So that is currently a negative point for me, and possibly even more so for others who may not have had the chance to run it at all yet.I'm hoping Amazon respond with a message discussing an incoming update or with the knowledge there is something deeply wrong with a certain batch of units and that mine possibly came from that early batch. I got mine a week or two before official release, so perhaps some of the earliest production units had issues.----UPDATE (11-22-2016): Quick update, but Amazon responded to my message with a kind of boilerplate response and suggestion, but one I hadn't tried. It was to factory reset the Dot by holding down the "mic mute" and "volume down" buttons until the unit starts flashing the orange light. Then go through the process all over again. I still had issues with getting voice training to work with my Android phone, but I COULD get it to work with an iOS device running the Alexa app. So using that awkward combination, I was able to reset and retrain my Dot.I haven't spent a lot of time with it since then, but in my very, very limited hands on it seems to have improved things a little. Again, I haven't found any sweeping changes, and I've heard a new update was released recently that helped other people having issues with their Dot out. I can't easily separate if the minor improvement came from the update or the reset, but it might be worth trying if you are frustrated with your Dot's performance.I'm considering a bump to 3 stars since I feel like maybe these little improvements are starting to creep the Dot towards an "average" ability to understand my commands, but I have to test the performance more and give more attention to the bigger bugaboo - if performance with ambient noises/alarms/music has improved meaningfully as well.

title: Great Potential. Very affordable. Sadly, it should still be in Beta testing. In depth review below.
body: First, I'll start off with the positive. The Echo Dot (2nd Generation) price is excellent. Whether it is a good value is another matter. That's more of a subjective issue. If you don't mind dealing with the too often very frustrating shortcomings, you might love it. When it is actually functioning properly, it can be a great resource for knowledge, music, games, and access to "skills" (which is a mixed bag, I'll explain below). They're constantly adding features (and hopefully the will have a firmware and update for some of the issues below). As far as the sound quality coming from the speakers, they sound very clear. I was surprised that music sounded as good as it did for the price and size. Finally, it's hard to beat Amazon customer service. They're typically very helpful.Next, I will discuss the flaws the Echo Dot (2nd Generation). The first ones deal with the actual device. I will start off by saying that it's completely reliant on "the Cloud" (which is problematic on it's own from a privacy & security standpoint). Therefore, a constant internet connection is a must or she goes into what I'll call her "coma state". You will basically get the equivalent to the MS, "Uh oh, something went wrong message.", when one loses connection. If this happened rarely it would be understandable. Despite having a state-of-the-art network and very fast internet connection, it often goes comatose (with the exception of the red ring and the "uh oh" message. My network has MANY devices on it. NONE of them have a problem with dropping off of the network.1. Anyway, this sudden dropping from the network and going offline happens to me multiple times a day with the "lovely" red ring of death. Sure, it's easy to fix it in the short term. Simply, unplug it and plug it back in. After doing this for the 5th+ time in one day, this gets really annoying. I've tried all of the troubleshooting steps to resolve this issue in the long term to no avail.2. Alexa's microphone range is overrated, especially when it's playing music (at a moderate volume level) and typical everyday ambient noise. It is inconsistent. Sometimes, it seems to pick up one's voice very well. At other times, not so much. This may be at least partially related to the next issue. Alexa frustratingly can't understand some things no matter how well one enunciates what they're saying. Instead, of asking for a clarifying question such as, "Could you repeat that please?" That would be much more preferable to it deciding to just start playing a song off of Spotify or just no response at all even though it lit up as hearing what one said. I've have to tell Alexa to "stop" countless times. She/It is also very impatient. When one isn't accustomed to talking to AI sometimes and you lose your train of thought or you are slow at articulating what you want to say, it will cut you off. It's also very short on it's answers. Some people may see this as a plus, but, if I want to know the definition of something, I want it to be more in depth than just the first definition. It also seems to lag behind the other AI's like Cortana when it comes to breadth of knowledge overall.3. Then, there is Alexa and it's reliance on the Alexa app. I believe that the Alexa relies on the app too much as a crutch and for making up for a relatively scarcity of knowledge. For example, it will either refer you to the app or look for more information that just a verbal line or two, then the app often refers you to a web search option. This defeats the purpose of having a device such as this. If I want to rely on my app for knowledge, I'll just use OK Google or Siri instead. Anyway, the app is rather unintuitive and sloooooow compared to the other apps I have (I also have a high end phone and tablet so this shouldn't be a problem). To add on top of the slowness, for example if you want to add a "skill", it will take you way back and you have to go down the list again. My other gripe with the apps is that it doesn't have some of the things that would make an app awesome as in more customizeable besides the "skills". Most of these really weren't worthwhile looking and had mixed reviews at best. Even if one finds some skills that they like, instead of Alexa actually acquiring the so-called skill, you have to prompt her to "ask" another app to "open"4. Finally, here are just a few things that would be some desirable changes. 1) allow it to access Office 365/Outlook tasks, Wunderlist, etc. It only has a couple of options like Todoist. More options for shopping lists would be great too. 2) Add portability by having a built in battery 3) improve connectivity through a firmware update and/or an ability to "hop" from multiple wi-fi connections. 4) Make Alexa more conversational or better yet a setting to decide on how "personable" you want her to be. For example, if you want her to actively engage you in conversation then that would make her seem more "alive". 5) Finding a functional balance between the data accumulated on the cloud 6) Have an optional model with a storage capability to help alleviate some of the frustrations of when it goes offline.With all of this being said, I want to say a few caveats. 1. This is a recent purchase (about a week) 2. PA AI's are very much in their infancy. I understand that it takes time to add knowledge and features and understanding human speech. These are remarkable feats on their own. 3. Perhaps I received a defective unit.

title: So therein lies the issue - If the original Echo works fine, my router can't be the culprit here - ...
body: Something very strange is up with these Echo Dots and my router. I don't know what, exactly, but I think there is a bug in their baseband software/firmware. I've had multiple calls with tech support and the issue has supposedly been escalated to development but I have not reached any sort of resolution and have already been through 3 replacement dots (and all of them exhibit the same issue on my network).The array of problems varies from:1) "Sorry your Echo Dot has lost its connection"2) "Sorry I was having trouble understanding that. Please try again later"3) Echo Dot wakes upon "Alexa" but does not process or recognize my queries (this can be verified by going into the Alexa App/Settings/History and seeing "Text not available. Click to play recording.") - the blue ring and cyan directional mic-light stay lit as if Alexa keeps wanting to listen (this is with no background noise).4) Echo Dot takes more than a couple seconds to actually process any command5) Echo Dot will respond but the response will sometimes be cut-off halfway through.This is all very strange because I've tried putting these Dots on my iphone hotspot network as well as using a different router and they seem to not have these issues, for the most part. I've also gone through all the troubleshooting steps (factory reset the Dots, put them on different networks, turn off firewall, open ports, factory reset router, etc etc etc). I would tend to blame it on the router at this point, but the kicker is that I have an original Echo on this very network and it has ZERO issues. So therein lies the issue - If the original Echo works fine, my router can't be the culprit here - obviously there are design/hardware/software differences between the Echo and Echo Dots, and that is why I'm guessing there is a bug with the Echo Dot baseband software/firmware. At some point through communication with tech support, it was even acknowledged that there is some known issue regarding Dots losing their connectivity. I'm just surprised this isn't as widespread, but perhaps it only manifests itself in certain environments where specific routers are used. Either way, I can't sit around and wait for development with zero updates to the problem. It seems like it is just being swept under the rug. BTW: yes, my firmware updates have been applied to both units. Perhaps I will ask if they can roll the firmware back to what it was initially, and block upgrades, to see if there is any difference. I noticed, before the units upgraded, that they were fairly responsive. But then again, the upgrades occurred several minutes after the initial setup, which didn't give much time to tell if they would be 'stable'I am running DDWRT on a Netgear Nighthawk X4 (r7500v2). But I have seen another, on Reddit, reporting the same exact issues as myself and he is running a TP-Link Archer C8. I'd be curious to know what routers, those of you running into this problem, are using in your environments. I think I've reached the end of the road here - I will probably need to send these units back for a refund, sadly.UPDATE 11/3/16: I ended up resetting my router to factory defaults and upgraded to the latest DDWRT image as well. Still no luck with the Dots maintaining a connection. This is a very strange problem all the while my original Echo continues to work flawlessly. This points back to bugs and issues with the Echo Dots, whether it's hardware or software/firmware. I reached out to tech support again and asked for any updates or follow-up to no avail. The tech support rep was at least very helpful and understanding. Seeing that there was no resolution in sight, I asked what they would do and he offered a refund for *both* Dots (even though I got one at Best Buy... in reality the refund was for my order of the Echo Dot + Philips Hue bundle I pre-ordered at $99.xx in prior months). Additionally, he offered me a $30 promo credit and then told me to go ahead and keep the Dots. GREAT customer service! But it still doesn't excuse the fact that something is inherently wrong with these units. I consider these kinds of bugs to be ticking time bombs - if they leave these "minor" issues unresolved, who knows what impact there will be as they release more and more updates. I envision there will, slowly, be an increase in the number of support cases revolving around connectivity issues with these Echo Dots. 1-star added because of the experience with Amazon support, but in reality this review is still about the Echo Dot product.UPDATE 12/20/16: Several weeks ago I saw an updated post on Reddit from another user who was experiencing the same issues I did and clued me in on something - create another network/guest network, use WPA (rather than WPA2) and hide the SSID. Not the optimal solution as far as security is concerned but I gave it a shot and the last two Echos I was having major issues with started working flawlessly and have been working ever since. So there is a very OBVIOUS defect/issue with the way these Echo Dots negotiating and maintaining connecting via the WPA2 protocol. To my knowledge, Amazon has neither confirmed or addressed this issue at large. It's hard to believe a majority of users out there are *not* using WPA2 but I guess I wouldn't be surprised either. Perhaps this still is only limited to specific hardware when WPA2 is in use but I've reproduced the issue on my dad's network which was also secured with WPA2 and an AC router.

title: Comparing home devices shows huge flaws in Alexa
body: I've had my two Echo Dots for one year now. Now, a year later, Google Home has really come forward by leaps and bounds while Alexa has basically stayed exactly the same. For Android users, the easiest way to describe it is that the Alexa has become an iPhone vs. Google's Android. I think I am the norm in that there is almost no "Skill" I have use for with Alexa. Instead I ask things that are already built in features: weather, time, set alarm, add things to shopping list, and of course, control home devices like lights. However, ALL home devices can do those things just as easily and intuitively as Alexa does, so to see if you are getting the best device you need to compare other features:Asking a question - Google wins hands down. Google uses... Google, to get your answer. Alexa uses Bing I think, nuff said. Most of the time the response from my Dot is "I'm sorry, I don't know that" and if it does "know", it is giving an answer to something I'm not truly asking. For example, I asked it what movies were playing on FOX tonight and the response was "I'm still learning about fox. Ask me a fox fact so I can learn more about them." Many times it even just answers to search the internet. Well, if I have to search the internet then I want to use Google to do it and there is no way to set your search engine. Essentially every time it tells me to search the internet, what I hear in my mind is "Use Google" - yeah, I think I just might, Amazon."Skills" apps - all but useless for every day life. Skills I can see some possibility of using are the reading a bedtime story if you have kids, or asking Alexa to play a game. But for adult life and for truly being a life/home assistant to you, none of the Skills compare at all to the platform that Google already has in place from decades of being an internet powerhouse. Example: finding your phone. This is a pretty common issue that I think it's reasonable to assume any of these home assist devices should be able to tackle easily. For Alexa you must download a "Skill" (there are a few to choose from) and register your phone number to that app. The major flaws with Alexa doing this are: 1. you need to first tell Alexa to open and use the skill with precise wording for it to work (i.e. "Alexa find my phone" won't work.
