"Alexa have Tracker find my phone" won't work. "Alexa open the Tracker app. Alexa have Tracker find my phone" won't work. "Alexa open the Tracker app. Alexa ask the Tracker app to find my phone" works). 2. if your ringer is off there is no ability to turn it on for the purpose of finding the phone. Essentially you'd be better off to add "me" to contacts with your own number and just tell Alexa "call me" because that is all you are getting with Alexa's "Skills", it's easier to just do that, and it only has the same flaw of not ringing if ringer is off. In comparison, with Google you download nothing, simply say "Google find my phone", and viola, your phone will ring whether the ringer is on or not. Google also has "Maps" which is huge. "Maps" gives you locations, hours of operation, can list like-businesses, tell you how to get somewhere and how long it will take you, and even tell you which buses to take if you have public transit in your area, etc. Alexa can only utilize Yelp and if a business isn't on Yelp then Alexa has no access to even the very basic info Yelp supplies (hours).There's one 'Pro' to Echo over any other home device, and that is as it relates to other Amazon devices. Like Google, Amazon is also an internet powerhouse in it's own way. Having a Prime membership is the norm rather than the exception in most households, and obviously that means asking Echo to order something or play a Prime movie or Prime music is going to be smooth and easy with the Echo. Also, where Amazon still leads the pack is with it's Fire Stick, and again, obviously, Echo is going to be well integrated with that device, while other Home Assistants aren't given that level of access to integrate as well. So that's why I give it 2 stars - because I can't use my FireStick AS EASILY if I owned something other than an Echo. But I can still use the remote so that ability is def not worth more than two stars, especially when Google has the ability to take away access to things like YouTube (which it did) from Amazon in retaliation for being denied access to things like FireStick integration into it's own device.Bottom line, Echo/Alexa seems great until you see how much better the later competitors to the market have improved on the user experience and are continuing to improve by leaps and bounds with each new update, while Amazon rests on 'we were the first'. Or, in other words, don't fall for yet another same ol' iPhone that is introducing features Android users have had for at least five years when instead you could just buy the Android and have the newest and best features right now.

title: Setup was AWFUL
body: I had an Echo a few years ago but I gave it to a friend. I decided to get an Echo Dot because the price was good, and I have a bluetooth speaker I can hook it up to.It arrived the day after I ordered it, which is great!I opened the box, and took out the Dot, the power cord, the power plug, and the two booklets that fell out.The instructions were just four steps with very clear pictures.Great, I thought.I followed the first two instructions, and then downloaded the Alexa app to my phone. That was...puzzling because why did I need to download it to my phone? My Echo Dot sat there with the yellow-orange ring, and after fooling around with the Alexa app on my phone, I realized that the Alexa app on my phone didn't really do anything to connect my Echo Dot. THIS WAS VERY FRUSTRATING, AMAZON.Then I hunted around until I found the link to the Alexa device "app" on my browser, and after futzing around for about 20 minutes, trying to figure out WHAT THE HECK do I need to do, I found the page to set up the Echo Dot.First thing--the Echo Dot doesn't recognize my network. My wireless router is literally a foot away on a shelf behind my desk. "Can't find the network." I reboot the router several times, reboot the Echo Dot several times, try using a different network.Nope. It won't connect, so I can't proceed.I write my first flaming feedback email to Amazon.I keep trying to connect it, and after about 20 minutes more, I get it to connect. But it won't set up, because the page on the Alexa set-up page can't access the network now. It just shows a "retry later" spinning circle.WHAT THE HECK AMAZON?I reboot the router again, close the browser window, clear the cache, and try again.After another ten minutes of futzing around I get the Echo Dot registered! Hooray!Now it asks if want to connect it to a bluetooth speaker. YES, I DO! I have the Amazon Basics bluetooth speaker which I've connected to my Fire tablets before, so I'm pretty sure it will connect to the Echo Dot. The bluetooth speaker is approximately a foot away from the Echo Dot, so I'm pretty sure the Echo Dot can find it--it's within range, after all.WHAT THE HECK, AMAZON?It won't connect. Won't connect. Can't find it. Can't connect. Over and over. I reboot the speaker over and over. I reboot the Echo Dot over and over. Finally it connects, but the Echo Dot says THROUGH THE BLUETOOTH SPEAKER that it can't find the bluetooth speaker.WHAT THE HECK AMAZON?I'm ready to hurl this Echo Dot through the window.I continue to try to set it up, which deregisters it and re-registers it, and now I'm asked to confirm my Amazon account by getting a code sent to my phone. AMAZON I'M TRYING TO FOLLOW YOUR INSTRUCTIONS. WHY DO YOU THINK I AM SUDDENLY SOMEONE ELSE USING THE SAME NETWORK AND ACCOUNT?I get the Echo Dot connected, finally, but not to the bluetooth speaker. I give up on that, furious that my bluetooth speaker won't connect now. "Try again later."I try to contact Amazon for help. I've been futzing around for more than an hour. It's late, I have to go somewhere for an appointment--but Amazon has removed the email option for contacting them! I can either phone or chat.WHAT THE HECK, AMAZON?I don't have time right now to phone or chat. I want to dash off a note of my experience, not go through the SAME TROUBLESHOOTING STEPS I just went through.GRRRRRRR...I'm ready to turn this back to Amazon after trying AND FAILING for over an hour.This was a CATASTROPHIC FLOP, Amazon. I followed the directions, I have everything in place, but this device DOES NOT WORK OUT-OF-THE-BOX AS DESCRIBED.I'm hoping this is just because the 2nd Generation Echo has connection problems. I'm thinking of returning it and getting the 3rd Generation device.Maybe Amazon has fixed things in the newest version.I gotta say, I had the 1st generation Echo device, and that setup was kinda difficult, but it was nothing like this.

title: Error Code 7:3:4:4:1
body: I am giving 2 stars because I have two of these; one connected to wifi and works fine; the other will not connect to wifi, error code 7:3:4:4:1. I've done all the troubleshooting listed online. No help. I've been on Chat Support four or five times and this is where it gets funny. The second time the representative decided it was a defective device and shipped out another. Received second one and lo and behold, same exact issue, error code 7:3:4:4:1. Twice I've had the representative tell me to unplug my modem. The first time, duh, it disconnected chat and I lost the representative. The second time, I told the rep that I had done the whole unplug/replug bit and it didn't work, AND that doing so would cut off the chat. Rep guaranteed doing so would fix the problem and chat would pick right back up. Well, you see that I'm still writing that the Dot isn't working. Get on with Chat again. At this point it is becoming comical with the "help" I'm getting. Finally get it through the rep's head that the basic troubleshooting is not working and she agrees to send it up the chain to the developer team. Said I would get an email in 48 hours. I received the email and here it is...Hello James,This is Aditya from Echo Chat Support team!This is a follow up email regarding the WiFi connectivity issues on your Echo Dot. We'd require some more information in order to help our technical team resolve the issue as soon as possible.******Please write back to us with the following information******* Your router's make / model / firmware version* Your router's hardware revision number (typically found on the back of the router)* The current configuration of your Wi-Fi network (for example: "I have a modem, router, and an Echo device.")* The approximate distance of your Echo device from your router* The location of your Echo device in relation to your router (for example: "My Echo device is located in my kitchen.")* The color of the LED light located at the bottom of the device* The name of your Wi-Fi network (SSID)* Whether your Wi-Fi network is hidden or if it appears as an available network in the Alexa app* Whether there are firewalls, traffic filtering, or MAC address filtering rules on your router* The Wi-Fi network security type (WPA / WEP / WPA2 / AES / TKIP / Mixed)* Whether you connect your Echo device to the 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz band on your router (can be found using your router's configuration or settings page)* Whether you connect your Echo device to the wireless channel 1, 6, or 11 on your router (can be found using your router's configuration or settings page)* The radio mode of your router (a / b / g / n / ac / Mixed / Auto)* Channel width (20 MHz or 40 MHz)* Whether you're using a Wi-Fi repeater* The character length of your Wi-Fi password and whether it has uppercase letters or special characters (for example: +, -, &, ?)* The current speed of your wireless connection (can be found by contacting your service provider)* The maximum number of connections allowed by your router* Error messages that appear in the Alexa app when you try to set up your Echo deviceIf you have any queries, You can reach Echo Customer Support via phone or chat by clicking one of the links below:To contact us via phone:http://www.amazon.com/clicktocallTo contact us via chat:http://www.amazon.com/clicktochatThanks for using Echo . We hope you can contact us soon so we can help solve this problem quickly.Best regards,Aditya I KThank you.Amazon.comDid I mention that I have a Dot that is hooked up to this very same wifi with no problem... Yep, I've got nothing but time to do the research on a problem with the device I PAID THEM FOR!!! If your system is that sensitive then maybe the issue is you... Think I will simply be returning said Echo Dot and buying Google Home...Error code 7:3:4:4:1

title: Don't see what all the great reviews are for
body: The concept of Echo is cool. A computer assistant that's always listening and connected into the Amazon mothership. I have a Tap and it's great because I can use it as a portable wifi or bluetooth speaker, but have to tap the button to activate Alexa. I thought I was missing out by not having an Echo that listened all the time. I was wrong, I'm not missing much of anything.Before I get to the bad, what can Alexa do that is good?She can give me weather forecasts and current conditions and they're accurate.She can give me good deals exclusive to Alexa.She can tell me where my Amazon stuff is.She has a limited database of jokes, some of which are pretty funny.She can add stuff to my cart for me to finalize and order later, she adds several things to choose from based on what you tell her and it's fairly accurate most of the time.That about wraps up what Alexa can do for me."Woah," you might say, "she can do so much more!" Can she?"Alexa order a dust pan""Amazon doesn't have the last desk fan you ordered, but here is a Lasko [...] for $37 dollars. Is this what you want?""Alexa stop. Alexa order a DUS-T-...-P-AN.""Here is the number one search result for Dust Pan...[...]...would you like me to order this?""Put it in my cart""Would you like me to order this?""Put it in my cart""..." Light shuts off.----I have a Philips Hue system. Hallway is named Hallway, Fireplace named fireplace, etc."Alexa, dim the fireplace light""I've found multiple devices named light, which one do you want?""Fireplace""Ok"Sometimes it works, sometimes it takes 5 minutes."Alexa dim the fireplace""I'm sorry I can't find a device named fireplace""Alexa dim the fireplace light""I've found multiple devices named light, which one do you want?""Fireplace ""I'm sorry the device fireplace is not responding "I open the hue app on the phone and dimming fireplace works like a champ."Alexa dim all the lights" or "Alexa dim the lights""I've found multiple devices named Light, which one do you want?""All the lights""Ok"---"Alexa add [product] to my cart"Half the time this doesn't work despite saying the search string that returns the product as the first hit on Amazon.com, and Alexa hears it correctly.--"Alexa, send this to my Amazon Tap""I'm sorry, I can't do that yet"---"Alexa, play iheartchristmas on iheartradio.""Playing Christmas songs from Amazon"I could never get it to do this. It always pirated what I said and played Amazon's station which is terrible.So, what Alexa can do for me is extremely limited. And what she can do is frankly easier many times to just open the app on the phone and do it myself.I tried to use Alexa to order things on Amazon as I think of them. If I tell her to just add it to my cart from the beginning, it works out pretty well. However if I don't understand her run on sentences as she describes the item in a much quicker dialogue with a very computerized voice with no inflections than she usually talks in - and I want her to simply add it to the cart so I can review it, she can't. I have to cancel it and re tell her to add it to the cart, and by then I could have done it from my phone.So Alexa is a cool concept but it's not there yet. She's definitely not artificially intelligent in any definition of the word, and it's usually faster to do her limited actions on your phone. I'll try again in a year or two unless Google or Apple end up having a better solution.

title: Crippled by poor training
body: I purchased this primarily to control my home automation equipment. I've had it for a day, and while my first impression was positive, it has rapidly gone downhill.First - It cannot be easily used for music streaming. There is no way to command it by voice to disable the 3.5mm aux output and switch to internal speaker - It is designed assuming that the external speaker will be ALWAYS turned on and ALWAYS have the Dot selected as the active input. No home theater system is set up this way, and nearly any speaker system that might be connected to the Dot is setup this way.The most frequent stated workaround for this is to use Bluetooth, since the Dot can be commanded by voice to turn on/off Bluetooth. The problem is - The voice command to turn off Bluetooth doesn't really turn it off. It just disconnects all active connections. If the paired device initiates a reconnect remotely (which nearly ALL Bluetooth audio receivers automatically do!), it will switch audio back to the Bluetooth device with NO indication on the local speaker that it is doing so. The end result is what appears to be a dead Dot (since, typically, someone who tells the Dot to turn Bluetooth off will change inputs on their AV receiver at the same time.)The Dot has an option to do per-user training by reciting 25 phrases. However, the training function has serious defects/limitations:1) It uses a selection of 25 predetermined phrases. If Alexa is having trouble with something not covered by that phrase set, you're SOL2) If you make a mistake during any of the 25 phrases, there is NO way to repeat just that phrase - mess up ONCE and you have to go through and recite EVERY phraseThe Dot will give you a history of SOME of the commands issued to it, but none that were determined to be a home automation function. So if you have issues with an HA command - you're SOL. Alexa frequently hears "away" as "hallway" and even when it seems to properly hear "away, it claims that there are multiple scenes/devices called "away" - but won't give you a list of the ones that it can't decide between for clarification. As a result, I'm unable to trigger my most commonly used home automation scene - "Away". It's not just difficult, so far it's been IMPOSSIBLE. Either Alexa hears it as "hallway" or hears it as something ambiguous (despite having one scene titled "Away" and no devices with that title.) There is no way for me to give Alexa further training to allow her to be able to properly determine "away" vs "hallway".Same with other "less standard" words - I tried to get scores to last night's Cornell hockey game. Not once did Alexa recognize the word Cornell - I can understand an untrained system having trouble with this - but Alexa is at least partially trainable, right? Nope, can't teach her a difficult word she obviously has trouble with. Seriously - if I'm asking for sports scores, I'm going to be asking for Cornell sports scores. There's no way to train Alexa to handle this better. As a result - it's impossible to obtain sports scores for the ONE sports team I actually follow. This at least lets me click on the Alexa website to say that Alexa didn't do what I wanted - but there's no way to specifically state what Alexa got wrong. CORNELL NOT HORMEL NOT SOME RANDOM STRING OF WORDS.Some of these issues may be because I briefly stuttered on 1-2 of the original training phrases - but see above - there's NO way to retry a phrase after you made a mistake during the 25-phrase recital marathon. Make ONE mistake and you have to repeat them ALL.That doesn't even get to the fact that music playback is crippled by an inabiility to enter a fallback mode where you spell an artist's name - I can understand Alexa not recognizing "Klingande" - but let me spell out the name when it fails!

title: Initial impression and usage is mixed.  Now the app wants my contact list... NOPE!
body: UPDATED: 2017-05-09Wow, talk about messing up a good thing. Today the Alexa app on the phone updated. Now has messaging. Just what I DON'T NEED another MESSAGING method. The app which allows you to control your Echo Dot's now wants access to my contacts. NOPE. Why, I don't plan on using Alexa Messaging so why give it access to my contacts? Lost another Star.Note I now have 3 echo dots. They work great for what I use them for, remote control of switches in the house. I don't use them for streaming, music etc.. I've got better ways to do that. Now you can add Messaging which I don't need.UPDATED: 2017-03-13I now have 2 Echo dots. Have had them both for quite some time now. Basically they work just fine. Found out this morning that Alexa dosn't know what the Arizona timezone is. Arizona doesn't do Daylight Saving time. We are in MST 12 months of the year. Yet, when picking a timezone the Arizona timezone is not an option. You have to pick either Montain Stadard which is really now Mountain Daylight, or Pacific Standard which is now really Pacific Daylight. There is no option to say that your timezone does not follow daylight saving.So, unlike everything else we have, we need to change the Timezone on both Alexa devices when Daylight saving goes into effect and when it goes out of effect. So, instead of saying MST, I need to pick PST otherwise the time on my Alexa's are off by an hour.I've only had this for two days. Never had another alexa unit before so this is a new experience.Originally obtained to see how it worked and if it could be interfaced with other data and devices. The developer program that Amazon has is interested and there are a number of other skills out there, but found it initially hard to discover (no search in the skills site, strange).After a day, I ran into a problem, Red Ring where the Echo dot said it couldn't understand me now, try again later. Support seemed to be missing as the app said it could load the help page and finding help on Amazon. Trying to reset the device with the Alexa App is very hit or miss, the Echo Dot will say it is in setup mode and the app will just sit there and spin its icon forever if you let it. The best fix seems to unplug the unit for a while and then plug it back in.ProsSmall, contained speaker it comes with is adequate and voice range seems to be pretty goodThe ring is a good indicator that it is hearing you.Creating new skills looks to be straight forward but find what is available is limited with no searching for apps, poor rating abilities.Setting up to use a skill seems fairly straight forward as well, but I found them via Google not the Echo Dot or the app.ConsLack of access to lots of data... This is where the difference between Googles Assistant and Echo Dot most likely will lie. Huge amounts of info on Google is not available to the Echo dot as it is using inferior Bing and even Yelp for data neither of which are very impressive.Apparently can fail and just stop recognizing me (red ring). Not clear why. Don't think it is the WiFi signal as I have a very strong router and everything works well with it.Poor support - Searching for Alexa doesn't understand me red ring told me to use the app to teach the device, hard to do when it doesn't recognize me and says so right off the bat.Right now, kinda mixed. Was not very expensive and my goal is to interface it with other services (lots of ideas) and local hardware for possible in home automation.Just for kicks, I added an external remote speaker and that worked well with the unit. I suspect that any plugin speaker will work okay.Will update as I get more used to it.

title: Well...it's a good idea in theory...
body: Decided to take the plunge and get one of these now that they're down to $50, figuring I'd use it with my main stereo or with my old computer speakers, which have two inputs (one of which is already being used for my clock radio). It arrived the day before I took a long road trip, so I decided to pack it and take it with me, along with a wifi router. I setup the wireless network with the same SSID and password as I use at home, so I figured when I returned home the Echo would connect automatically without having to change any settings.In a dead quiet room, after a bit of training, the Echo could understand most of the commands I issued. It does occasionally have trouble understanding me, especially when music or the alarm are playing. While it was cute being able to ask for weather forecasts or the flash briefing, they weren't particularly useful and nothing Siri on my iPhone couldn't do. I also found the iPhone app kind of poorly laid out and unnecessarily difficult to use.My big hope with the Echo was that it would be able to playback music from my Amazon Music library, where I have tens of thousands of tracks and dozens of playlists. Unfortunately, this hasn't gone smoothly at all. The Echo will frequently stall, or stop playing a track right in the middle and advance to the next. It also makes bizarre choices, preferring versions of tracks from Prime music to those in my own library. Just earlier today I asked it to play Culture Club's "Do You Really Want To Hurt Me" and it picked a live Amazon Prime version instead of the album version from my own library. I can't even get it to serve up a playlist in my library called simply "Sleepytime" - instead, the Echo keeps playing something called "Sleepytime Pop Classics" from Amazon Prime.The Dot has frozen up at least half a dozen times since I got it. Earlier this evening it seemed to lose its mind while playing music and just hung, with the blue light ring pulsating. Alexa frequently tells me she's having "some kind of problem" and can't respond. I thought maybe my old cable modem / router was dying so I purchased a new one. It's fantastic - my iPhone screams when connected to this thing - but the Echo still hangs.I have been using the Echo's alarm clock as kind of a backup to my old clock radio. Surprisingly, it doesn't let you wakeup to a track or to a playlist, which seems like pretty basic functionality. I was able to connect it to my Harmony unified remote, but Alexa can't do much - just turn on my receiver and TV and switch between inputs via voice command. Hopefully more functionality will come.One thing that does work incredibly well on this device - Bluetooth. I have a couple of Bluetooth speakers - a TDK Trek Max, which is a small portable, as well as their larger, older A73 boom box. The older device in particular has all kinds of trouble connecting to newer Bluetooth gadgets like my iPhone - it tends to skip and drop off the network - but the Dot had no trouble connecting, sounded better than the iPhone, and never dropped the connection (unless it crashed). It would also always reconnect successfully after a restart. It could also communicate with both devices over a much greater distance, connecting to the Trek Max when it was in another room behind a closed door!I just wish everything else worked as well with the Dot as the Bluetooth does.I haven't given up on this thing yet. I'm still hopeful a firmware update will cure a lot of these issues, and that more, better functionality is coming down the road. But I probably wouldn't recommend buying one of these just yet - seems half baked.

title: Don't use it as much as I thought I would...
body: So I bought this during the Prime sale. When I got this I was very excited to see how it worked and to use it. However, I came to realize very quickly that I am not a voice assistant fan to begin with.Some problems with Alexa:Some commands, you have to be utterly specific and that can get annoying after a while when you just want the device to perform a simple action.I notice every now and again that if I'm talking to someone, Alexa will all the sudden become triggered even when it's not asked to do so. So about 99% of the time, I keep her on mute because when she does chime in at unexpected times, it will scare you quite a bit.Because I dont have a visual with this device(such as a screen like another device Amazon has called Echo Show), I feel a little nervous as to whether it's actually doing what I want.It is so much easier just to order something online on my phone or iPad rather than to go through all the nonsense with Alexa. And on top of that, I'm afraid it wont order the right thing. So I keep myself away from that feature.A lot of times when I try to wake the device up by saying "Echo", it does not respond and takes 2 or maybe even 3 times for to trigger. (For those who are not familiar with Alexa, you are able to command it by the name Alexa, Echo, or Computer. I chose Echo in this case and you are able to change it again; the name choice is not permanent). Even when I had the wake word as Alexa, it did the same thing as well.I notice the only things I ask Alexa to do are to tell me the time and weather (if I dont have my phone around me at the moment) and to change/turn on/turn off my bedroom lights (Philip's Hue Color Ambiance lights work OKAY with Alexa however it does require you to be specific by which room you want even if you only have one room using Philip's Hue. It will also not recognize certain "scene" names and sometimes may not even change it to the color you want). Other than that, Alexa is pretty useless to me. If this device cost more money, I would be pretty upset with my purchase but I wasnt too upset due to the fact that it's not CRAZY expensive and I prepared myself for it to not be as awesome even though I was still excited when I got it.Another thing that may bother some people: if you dont like talking to a robot because it makes you feel awkward,then dont waste your time.Don't bother playing music on this thing if you have an actual bluetooth speaker made for music or something of the sort. It will not compare. I have a stereo system with a subwoofer and once you listen to music on that, the Alexa is worthless speaker wise. Some say the bigger version of the Alexa is a good speaker, however I can only speak for this one and it is not good for music. If that is the only reason you are getting it, spend the extra dollar and get an actual speaker.I will be honest, I dont really have too many pros for this item but I think it depends on the type of person you are and what you prefer in a voice assistant.So before purchasing this, think of how many times you've actually said to yourself "man I wish I had a voice assistant for this. Itd make my life so much easier". Because I never said this to myself once and I'm realizing why now. I simply bought this because the idea of it sounded cool. But if you want to experiment, think you absolutely want/need it, or just have money to throw away, then by all means:purchase it. Although I'm sure there are better voice assistants out there rather than the Echo Dot. My advice would be to research.

title: Insecure, under-developed, and a thin disguise for "premium" upsells and datamining.
body: The Echo Dot is sold as a cheap entry-level buy-in to "Smart Home" systems and an easy access point to amazon's rapidly growing library of plugins that let you use voice-activated convenience features to do common household tasks like playing music, taking calls, or adding to your shopping list.The Dot does not meet these lofty expectations. The hardware seems perfectly fine; microphone and audio quality are good. The unit itself is nicely compact and features a pleasant ring light and four unfortunately identical buttons, meaning you can't tell the volume up from the volume down by touch alone. The real problem with the dot is software.The voice-recognition tech just isn't there yet. What it can do is parse very carefully-phrased and clearly-spoken bits of speech that use pre-defined and cataloged words. What it cannot do is process anything you say intelligently. If you listen to any music that isn't top 40, you're out of luck. I like classical and there's simply no way for the Dot to do what I want; either I have to say "echo, play Dmitri Shostakovich Piano Concerto Number One Eugene List and the Berlin Opera Orchestra Four Allegro Con Brio" - using exactly those words and format - or it "can't find" the track. It fails to recognize track titles until you say the track number. It fails to differentiate between two songs with the same title. It fails to play the non-premium version of the song you have in your library, opting instead to try and sell you on another amazon service, because that's what it is actually in your house to do, not play music or turn on lights.This would be fine if the amazon services actually worked or offered anything of value, but they don't. If you're under the impression amazon is supporting this echo ecosystem with their own expanding library of cool apps, you're wrong - they've released the API and have basically thrown their hands up and left it to individual developers to build plugins. What this means in practice is that everything you might find cool has it's own keywords to start up and it's own set of voice commands, fragmenting the experience and offering no guarantee of universal quality or control.Even the most basic interactions with the actual amazon software are painfully awkward. Anyone in bluetooth range can pair to the Echo, meaning that if your neighbor accidentally connects to your Dot while trying to set up his new bluetooth speaker, you'll be suddenly hearing someone else's music from your system. It's rather unnerving to have a device that literally monitors everything you say suddenly out of your control.You can always load up the mobile app and un-pair the neighbor and select your music from there - if the app will load or respond at all - but at that point it's no better than a regular bluetooth speaker. Even trying to load music into amazon's music cloud to play with the dot's voice-recognition was a disaster, since they cut you off after a certain number of uploaded songs to push you into - you guessed it - a premium subscription. If you aren't able to play what I want already, why would the premium service help?Bottom line - the dot is not worth the money or the hassle, and it seems likely amazon knows that. Their play is to use these systems to collect data and try to sell more services, not to provide an exceptionally smooth experience. Until these smart-home speakers have much, much better intelligent decision-making behind the fancy voice recognition piece - stay away.

title: Not ready for prime time.  Slow.  No error correction: you have to start from scratch everytime!!
body: 1) When it works, it's just a little slow. Right now, one of my echos has about an 8 second delay when asked the time. That's ridiculous. 2) When it doesn't, error detection and correction -- well they are totally absent. EG: the machine is supposed to be totally integrated with Phillips Hue; but it frequently loses contact with phillip hue bulbs that are just fine in the Hue App. You can't just 'synchronize with Hue" to fix things. You have to eliminate the hue bulbs from the Alexa app and search for them again. This should just work and it doesn't. 3) Music: don't even ask me about classical music; Echo is patheticly inadequate: someone, please design a skill for this. The Amazon jazz channel (favored by Alexa) isn't bad, but it always plays the same songs in the same order. (Mozart ; the requiem movement 3, every time. 4) If you change your network password, all the Dots drop from your network. You have to set up each on individually, which would be ok if it was easy. It ain't. First, you can't tell which of the dots listed on the Alexa is the one you are looking at. I've tried just forging ahead, which sometimes works. When the dot tells you go to the alexa ap and follow instructions, you end up at the home screen (if you are lucky) or a connection screen from a previous failed attempt. I've tried adding the echo to the system which works sometimes. Sometimes. Argh! You should be able to match a dot to its name by pressing something or asking Alexa. If any of this is possible, it doesn't seem to be documented. 5) If you have more than one Alex, you get more problems. Right now, the closest dot rarely responds, its usually one further away. This would be OK excpet for setting alarms and timers, which you can do only on one dot at a time. The other dots don't know what alarms are set on the other dots. So sometimes I try to set an alarm in the bedroom, but the alarm gets set up in the kitchen. To cancel it, I have to walk into the kitchen. Or I have to yell, Alexa, stop, and have every dot in the house respond that no alarm is set. Of course, it's still beeping in the next room. 6) The dot makes a good kitchen timer, I'll say that. 7) You can't turn on something for a specific amount of time. EG: Alexa, turn on bathroom heater for 15 minutes. Or if you can it's not documented. 8) Biggest mess: trying to use Alexa with Sonos. You can set up the Sonos Ap, but you can't address it; you say the sonos room number. But you have to say the whole command at once : Alexa play Moscow Nights by the Red Army Choir from Spotify in Dining Room" (no, that's not my usual preference), and half the time the dot itself starts playing, or plays its own version not from Spotify. It doesn't help that the Sonos site gives zero documentation on how to use the interface. Forget trying to play your own music library. 8) Generally, trying to find out what a particular skill does and what phrases it will recognize is a matter of trial and error. 9) My advice is to turn off the ordering function altogether, or you'll get all kinds of random stuff you don't want. "Do you want to order that?" Really egregious. 10) Apparently there are different levels of wordiness from the dot, but none are usefull, and not of them ask sequential questions. This would be nice: Alexa: Sponos. "What composer, artist, song , or title," If you choose "Mozart" : what piece of music? [Answer} what artist (reading available artists."

title: Great when it's working; not so happy with the call functionality, warranty or support
body: Initially, I loved the echo dot. However, after owning it for about 3 months, I started having some problems. The main problem was that often when I called another echo owner, the first 30-40 seconds of the conversation would be completely distorted on my side, but they could understand me fine. After that period of time, then the audio was clear. It was frustrating to call someone and say "hold on it's not working, give it 30 seconds." I ended up calling in to Amazon to ask for help, and the customer service person had me do a factory reset and said they'd call me back in a day to see how things were going (which is great follow up). Before I received the call, I started having another issue here and there where I would say "Alexa" and the dot wouldn't even light up or take a command. So, when I got the callback, I explained the situation. The rep asked me to look through some things on the app, but we didn't actually make any modifications to the app or the device itself. So, when they told me they'd call me back the next day to see how things were, I asked why we couldn't do something else now. I was told that there was a process to go through and that even if I were transferred to actual technical support, they wouldn't help me (which seemed strange). Since we didn't actually make any adjustments this time, and it didn't make sense to just wait and see for a day to check whether there were any problems during that time frame, I asked to speak to a supervisor, who basically ended up saying the same things. After we talked for several more minutes and me trying to understand all this "process" stuff, the supervisor finally said he'd make an exception and send me a refurbished replacement even though my product was a few weeks out of warranty. BTW, the warranty for the echo dot is only 3 months. I wish I had paid more attention when I purchased the item, but I guess being a happy Amazon customer for so long, I didn't really think the warranty would be so short. To me a year is customary (as with Google's version). Oh well. When it works, it's great. When it doesn't, it can get frustrating quickly. I don't know if I have too high of expectations for Amazon, but I expected a bit more from the longevity of the product and the customer support department.update 12/21/2017_____________________________After using the replacement I received, I'm still very unhappy with my echo dot because it's not working well for the reason I bought it. The new device has mostly the same issues as the one it replaced. There may be less early call distortion with this one, but that could be because I unplug the dot more often. Primarily, I wanted something I could use to call another person's dot so that we could maintain communication at points throughout the work day. However, the connection is often dropped between a dot to dot call, or only one person is able to hear the other, requiring a manual disconnection and re-call. Frequently, the dot will appear connected (green light swirling around), but in reality the other person has been disconnected or hears you but cannot respond back and you're talking to Alexa only. It becomes very frustrating. Consequently, it's easier just to use a cellphone on speaker. It works well if you just need to ask it things or tell it to do things, but if you plan on using the "call" feature, it may let you down.

title: Manual no good + instructions for connecting to "speaker" jack
body: Typical of what one can expect these days.The device is okay I guess, but the manual jerks.So in the instructions for connecting an external speaker say to use a "3.5mm audio jack".WHAT KIND OF 3.5MM AUDIO JACK?There are three kinds of 3.5mm audio jacks that I can think of. Use the wrong one and it won't work right, might even damage the dot.Typical "THROW THE CUSTOMER UNDER THE BUS"-------------------------------------------------------Few days later now, I experimented to sort out what's up with the speaker jack.You can use either a smartphone plug, such as the plug on smartphone headsets, or a stereo mini-jack. In either case the output is monophonic into only one channel.The writers of this manual did not know what they were doing.The instructions refer to this jack as an "aux" jack in one place, and a "speaker" jack in another place.It is, in fact, an aux jack, aka auxiliary jack.If you plug a speaker directly into this jack it will sound weak and tinny. And it may eventually damage guts of the Dot.When they say "speaker" jack, they may be referring to an AMPLIFIED speaker. Many computer speakers are amplified speakers. This is actually two things in one, an amplifier plus a speaker.So if you are using an amplified speaker, you can plug it directly into this jack.INSTRUCTIONS FOR CONNECTING AN AMPLIFIED SPEAKER:If it is a single channel speaker you can just plug it in and you're in business. Wahoo!If it is a stereo speaker, just plugging it in will give you audio on only one channel. If that's okay with you, then cool. If you want monophonic sound coming from both speakers then you need an adapter. Specifically, a mini jack mono-to-stereo adapter, such as the Philmore 560A. Here's an Amazon link, if it's dead by the time you get to it, please notify me. I hate dead links.https://smile.amazon.com/Philmore-560A-Stereo-stereo-mono-Adapter/dp/B008YOFQMQ/ref=sr_1_18?ie=UTF8&qid=1478719869&sr=8-18&keywords=mini+jack+adapter+mono+to+stereoSo, plug the adapter into the Dot, and your stereo speaker jack into the adapter. Good luck. This is a tricky business.INSTRUCTIONS FOR CONNECTING A SPEAKER WITHOUT INTERNAL AMP:You will need an amplifier, like the Lepai LP-A68. Here's an Amazon link:https://smile.amazon.com/Lepai-LP-A68-Digital-Amplifier-Remote/dp/B00721Z1AE/ref=sr_1_7?ie=UTF8&qid=1478720274&sr=8-7&keywords=lepai+lepyI know, you got the Dot because of the appealing price, and now I'm telling you to fork out another 20+ clams. Such is life.Now there's two ways you can connect this. If you want to use one speaker, cool, the output of the Dot is monophonic anyway. But if you need to fill a room with two speakers, then you need to use the adapter described above.FOR CONNECTING HEADPHONES,Follow the same instructions for "speaker without internal amp".In the case of headphones it's probably more important to have both channels working. Use the adapter.If you don't and you listen for extended periods with only one channel working, you head will involuntarily tilt to one side.

title: UDATE:  Lower Rating and no Longer Can Recommend Echo/Alexa Products
body: Update 11/04/2018. I've lowered my rating to 2 stars and no longer can recommend the Echo products. This morning an alarm went off at 8:20am which I did not set. Amazon can't tell when it was set, but I know I haven't set any alarms. Also, in the past 2 months, it has been activating from the sound of the TV. It did this a little when I first purchased it, but after I trained it for my voice that dropped significantly. In the past 2 months or so there have updates to the Dot firmware, and since then it is constantly being activated by the sound from the TV - even with words that aren't anywhere near "Alexa" so until Amazon can give me a reason why this is now occurring with such frequency I can't recommend any Echo/Alexa devices.Original Review: I would have given it a 5 because it is a phenomenal device (once you get past the setup). However, there are 2 issues that caused me to drop a star. First is the documentation (instructions, FAQ, help, etc.) that is online for the end-user (you) and for the Customer Service Reps. The documentation to support this (and other Echo and Alexa enabled devices) is awful. It is difficult to find answer for questions, to find instructions on how specific functions work, and to even find instructions when Alexa tells you to look it up. It is just as difficult for Customer Service Reps to look it up when you call in for support.The second is that if you would like to use this device to control your Fire TV it only has the capability to control one Fire TV device, regardless of how many Echo devices you have. So if you have multiple TVs with multiple Fire TV devices (box or stick) you can only control one of them. This is a major design flaw. So if you have a Fire TV hooked up to your living room TV and an Echo device in that room, and you have a Fire TV hooked up to your bedroom TV and another Echo device in that room, the Echo device will only control 1 of the Fire TV devices regardless of which Echo device you are speaking too. However, it DOES do a great job of controlling the Fire TV device and is really nice not to have to look for the remote to launch a Fire TV app, or pause/restart what you are watching!There are many, many great features about the Echo devices. For example I love being able to ask what the current weather is, ask for the 7-day forecast, play Jeopardy, have it look up the definition of words and phrases (it pulls a lot from Wikipedia!!) and even ordering things from Amazon. Hungry for Italian? Just ask Alexa! And so much more. New "skills" are being added every day and you can look up on the site and see what they do and enable/disable them. And don't miss out that it can call just about any phone number in the US.With some improvements (such as the ones I named at the start of this review) it is the greatest device!Oh....and don't forget to ask it to "beam me up!"All in all I love the device and will likely get other Echo devices once they resolve the issue with only being able to control one Fire TV device. I've "cut cable" and am disabled, so being able to speak commands is great....it just needs to be able to operate all of my Fire TV devices!

title: Underwhelmed
body: This is a slightly useful device. It's ok for asking web search info items , like weather or scores, and a few other things.I had a lot of problems connecting to it initially, becuase I use 2-factor authentication on my account and by the time I had gone to my authenticator app and got a code, returned to Alexa app and gone through the login process again, the code expired. I had to turn off 2-factor on my account, and even then it made me do a capcha that was complicated by the fact that the app won't scroll, so I was typing in the capcha field blind. Thanks for the frustration, Alexa. I have secure physical possession of both devices, why can't the process be much easier?I have a Harmony hub for AV control, and Alexa can use it. Once again, connection problems abound, as the Alexa app insisted that I didn't have a Harmony hub, even though the Harmony app is on the very same phone that Alexa app is on. Eventually I was successful. Then came the sad discovery that all it can do is turn AV gear on and off. There went my dream of tuning my dvr by voice command. More frustration.So then I linked my Spotify account to have it play thru bluetooth speakers. This works, but every time I return to play music again, I have to manually make spotify use the bluetooth speakers, as it defaults to using the Dot speaker. Haven't found a work-around yet. Thanks for nothing, Alexa.As far as simple voice commands, it works about 80% of the time. Sometimes it ignores me, sometimes it whines about the internet connection. I live in a rural neighborhood that's not too badly wifi congested. My cell phone stays connected anywhere on my property but Alexa struggles from a 30 foot distance to my wifi router. It randomly announces when it connects to the wifi or bluetooth. It reacts to what it hears on TV sometimes(yes I have done the voice training), which leads to baffling announcements. I say 'Alexa, shut up" a lot.In summary, this would be a good deal at $19. Too bad it cost $50. I'll keep it, but it doesn't really do anything my Nexus 6 won't do with the "OK Google" function. Amazon is a major cloud provider, why can't they put a good web app up for connecting and configuring and linking?I can't recommend this device unless you are a 'smart home' hobbyist who wants to spend money linking it to Zwave or Zigbee devices.UPDATE: I decided to keep this and use it as a voice commanded search engine for simple questions, but its results have been poor. I will be returning it as being nearly useless to me.When it failed to answer a question, I repeated the question to Ok Google on my phone, and in all cases it was able to answer. Here are some of those questions:1. When was the piano invented?2. Who was Field Marshal von Rundstedt?3. What is maximum allowed length of a golf club?I'm not interested in using this as am Amazon shopping tool, which it is probably good at. I'm sending it back.

title: This gadget may work less than 6 months...and its only warranteed for 90DAYS!!!
body: Syed Khursheed: Hello, my name is Syed Khursheed. I'm here to help you today.Me: thanks. My Echo Dot does not work anymore. I chatted with a customer rep today and they gave me reset directions. Those failed to make my Echo Dot work. I'd like to send it in for replacement or repair.Syed Khursheed: XXXXX may I know what is the problem with the device?Me: There is no sound coming from the device. The blue lights keep flashing. I could not even reset it. There was no orange light.Syed Khursheed: Alright, a member of our Echo Tech Support will be the perfect person to help you with this. I will be transferring this chat, this will only take a moment.Me: I know how it works. I have been using it fine for many months, and now it does not work.A Customer Service Associate will be with you in a moment.You are now connected to Amazon from Amazon.comAmazon: Hello, my name is Praneetha. Please give me a moment to review the previous correspondence.Me: Ok.Amazon: I’m afraid, it seems that your device has malfunctioned and needs a replacement.As you might be aware, your Echo dot comes with 90-days limited warranty which ended on January 19, 2017, I'm unable to process a free replacement.However, I'm happy to give you options of replacing at a discounted price. Would you like to see the option?Me: Hmm, I thought the warranty was for a year!?Amazon: Unfortunately it is only for 90-daysMe: Could you please look at this Amazon warranty webpage? Echo dot is listed under 1 yr warranty items. https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html?nodeId=201014520Amazon: That is Echo dot 1st generation [my name] and your's is 2nd generation deviceMe: What...so 2nd generation is built worse than the 1st??? Very disappointing. what is the discounted replacement price?Amazon: Please check below link:https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=hp_left_v4_sib?ie=UTF8&nodeId=201854160Amish, it is not that it's worstThe policy is set by the companyYou can purchase the same device at 15% offMe: That's fine. I really didn't want to spend that much more money for something that works less than a year. I will certainly tell everyone about this 90 day warranty. :(Amazon: Most devices never have this issue. The likelihood of this ever happening again to a Echo dot you own is significantly low.Me: Thank you for your kind words.Amazon: Also I understand your concern. However, as per the warranty policy of the Company, I'd be unable to process a free replacement after warranty has passed. I hope you understand my limitations regarding this.Me: Thats fine. Its not your fault. Thanks anyway.Amazon: I am very sorry that we were not able to meet your expectations this time around. I hope that you will allow us another opportunity to serve you in the future.

title: A lot of functionality and potential, but poor implementation
body: I really wanted to like this product, and at $50 it should be a steal (I even bought multiple), but I've been extremely unimpressed.Technically it has a lot of potential functionality, but here are my problems with it:- It doesn't recognize my voice well. I've done the voice training exercise for it multiple times, and I do NOT have an unusual accent or speech pattern/difficulty. But very frequently it won't understand phrases that I know work. It's so frustrating that I feel I need to make an effort to speak loudly and clearly each and every time, but even that doesn't seem to help much.- Sometimes, especially when I haven't used it for a little while, it won't respond to my query at all except for lighting up the blue ring briefly. Just another instance where I have to repeat myself, often multiple times. I don't know if it's because it goes into some kind of sleep/low power mode, but it's annoying.- I didn't expect the speakers to be loud or to be worth playing music out of, but I wish the max volume for Alexa's voice was louder. I can understand it from across the room, but not all that comfortably. I don't get the impression it's meant to be communicated with from more than a handful of feet away. I have excellent hearing according to my ENT, though I'll concede it won't be a problem for everyone as preferred volumes are subjective. But having even slightly better speakers would increase the range of options for those that do want them.- In terms of the basic things it can respond to, Google Now is just lightyears ahead. I get relevant information from Google Now nearly the vast majority of times I use it for new questions/topics, but with the Echo I was shocked at how basic some of the questions were that Alexa hasn't been able to respond to. I find myself trying new queries less and less unless I've already made an effort to look them up in her skills menu and make sure they work.I'm sure Alexa's response will improve with time, but since I'm rating the product as it is, I can't recommend it. It's been less than a week and it's already feeling like more of a chore to use the device than a convenience. For example, it's easier and faster to pull out my phone and open the Nest app to change my thermostat's temperature than speaking the phrase to get Alexa to change it multiple times. Same thing with just about everything else I wanted to use the Echo with.All in all, it doesn't feel like it's been a beneficial addition to my home. I think the cheap price was enough to warrant the second star, but in its current form it would have to hit ~$35 for me to be comfortable rating it any higher. That might sound like a crazy price considering the last gen model was $100, but if I'm giving an honest opinion, it does feel like a $35 device - specifically, a $35 off-brand knockoff of what I actually expected the Echo Dot to be.

title: JUST OK
body: UPDATE TO REVIEW BELOW: I have 3 of these set up in my house. I've already had to exchange one because it quit working. Now 2 of the other 3 all of a sudden can NEVER hear you - you have to yell at it, whereas before they all worked just fine. ALSO, I ask/tell it to do something, like turn off a light, and it gives me an answer that is totally unrelated to anything even close to the words I've said. I'm beginning to think maybe I should try that other one of these things that are out there by another company because these are not holding up. ALSO, out of the blue I am simply sitting on my couch BY MYSELF surfing the net, and all of a sudden she starts talking for no reason whatsoever. I do not have the tv on; I'm not talking to myself; so WTF! I'm becoming less and less enamored with this product, and I've bought about 10 of them - 3 for myself, 2 for my boyfriend and the others I've given out for gifts or plan to (Christmas is almost here). I normally don't rag on something on here BUT I've bought enough of these that they better not all start messing up, like 2 of my 3 are already doing. Who wants to have to yell at something for it to respond - AND OH, just so you know, I'm standing with 6' of it - I'm not 50' away. RIGHT NEXT TO IT and I get NOTHING. Then I yell, and all of a sudden the stupid thing responds. Did I mention I was ANNOYED!!!I received my Echos timely and packaged well. I am in LOVE with this little thing. It works perfectly for me so far. I have had a time or 2 when it simply would spin and not respond, but not enough to cause me to want to return it. I have one in my living room and one in my bedroom. While it does reach out, for me, I don't want to have to yell to it from a distance. Also, it connected to my Samsung bluetooth soundbar instantly (even though my speaker is not on the "approved" list you see here on Amazon). It has come in so handy numerous times, like asking a quick question I have without having to pull out the tablet or phone to look it up; I have intergrated my google calendar so it will tell me what's on the schedule for the day or week. I have also integrated the music from my Samsung S7 and it plays all my music as soon as I ask it to. I have it hooked up to my Hue lights, and it works great from Alexa's standpoint - the Hue lights are a hit or miss as they often keep losing the connection to the bridge - even with the light in the same room as the router. If this doesn't stop, they'll be going back because at $200 for my Hue kit, these things should not keep losing a connection and becoming undiscoverable - BUT, that has nothing to do with the Alexa. If you don't have one of these, I highly recommend it. It is handy as can be and ONLY $49. What a dream gadget. MY ONE SUGGESTION IS THIS THOUGH. IT IS AN AMAZON DEVICE SO WHY DOESN'T IT HAVE A SKILL TO TURN ON OUR AMAZON FIRESTICK!!!

title: Alexa doesn't learn, is behind the game of other similar technologies, and just disappointing
body: Let me start with this example:Q: Alexa, what is a great white shark?A: My favorite number would be 36 foot.WT%!!!!!This is just and example of the stupidity. And, after working with support, it still gives the same answer.Next, I try over and over to get the voice recognition calibrated... It never seems happy. I use US Pacific Coast, Masters Level Educated Caucasian English. Ya, Alexa doesn't understand that. But, it catches every time the word Alexa is said on an ad from the TV. Again... WT%!!!!Lets move on to simple tasks.Q: Alexa, set an alarm for 6am playing my default playlist. (Note: this is my default amazon playlist)A: Ok, I will set an alarm for 6:30am playing best of the 90s country music.WT%!!!! A) I don't listen to country, so where did it get that from. Next 6 vs 630... ugh.It takes multiple attempts on any task to get it to do the right thing. And, it doesn't learn. It makes the same mistakes over and over and over... Despite trying to use the App to correct it, complaining to it, and so on.Next, when I place a complaint... suddenly in the middle of the work day / night I get a message with ... can we contact you right now to go over your issues. NO! I can contact you at my convenience not yours. again... WT%!The development team needs to "Get A Clue", as does the support team. First off... if someone says to their echo, "Alexa, you're stupid." Alexa should ask... should I send the last commands to support so they can see how to help you?Also, when people complain... listen!!!! Don't try to defend the defectiveness of your device.Alexa is like a horrid GF who thinks it will change you, and doesn't need to change itself... as it thinks it is perfect. Ugh... Dump it already...also, when I listen to an audible book at home, I usually don't want to listen to the same book I am listening to when I drive. (aka... I can read/listen to more than 2 books at once... it's called multitasking...) Play the book I ask to play... don't try to guess what I want, or just do the lazy thing.Also, audible, music... "Alexa, Play ____ from the beginning." should play from the beginning. You sooo can't seem to get this right, or even remotely close. So basic.I could go on and on about all the stupid, idiotic ways that this tool works.Google Home, and Apple Siri are years ahead. Samsung Smart Home "Bixby" is at about the same level... but actually does work slightly better... and Amazon just keeps slipping. You are partnered with Amazon Prime, Music, and Audible... why do these apps work better on the competition than on your own device????Anyway... My review.

title: Worked great for about 10 months, now a petulant, pricey paperweight
body: I own a black Echo (great) and a black Echo Dot (great), which have given me no problems worth mentioning. This white Echo Dot, however, has been a bit of a nightmare the past couple of months. It worked fine for about ten months. Then it started behaving strangely:(1) First it became harder and harder of hearing. Shouting at it from a few feet away and speaking loudly and with perfect enunciation a few inches from the microphones were the only ways to communicate with it reliably.(2) Then it began to randomly stop playing music without being asked. I would yell at it to play, and it would dutifully do so, then it would stop again at some random point, perhaps a few seconds, perhaps a few minutes, perhaps an hour and half later. There was no discernible pattern.(3) Then it started playing music on its own, without being asked. I would yell at it to stop, and it would, but then as with (2) above, it would begin playing randomly again at some unpredictable time.(4) Then it started adding items to my shopping list and to do list without being asked. Although I realize this could be due to its misunderstanding some unrelated conversation in the room as a request to do so, it never communicated back "I have added X to your shopping list," and the additions seemed to happen when no one was around it (I could be misremembering that, however).(5) Most recently it took to ignoring commands. I would yell at it to do something, the light ring would do a quick half-second flash, then nothing. Repeated yelling elicited repeated ring flashes and no other action. Then on the sixth or seventh yell, it would perform as expected, other than (2) and (3) above.(6) Finally (I think—there are so many issues I may have forgotten some), it began to randomly drop Bluetooth connectivity. Audio would stop playing through my Bluetooth speaker (in the same small room), then start playing through the Dot after a second or two. When I consulted the app, the Dot showed as still being connected to the speaker. I would forget the connection, reconnect, and all would be well—until the next time it lost the connection, a repeating occurrence.I spent a good deal of time with tech support online and was given steps to follow that did not help. Then they mentioned something about replacing it (I would gladly have accepted that option) but then immediately pivoted to additional steps to follow, involving the Alexa app and enabled skills, that were so convoluted and nonsensical, even after my attempts to clarify what exactly they were asking me to do, that I gave up.So now the white Echo Dot is unplugged, banished to a corner of my office, regarding me sullenly while I try to decide what to do with it.

title: Better off using your smartphone
body: This product would be pretty great if it actually worked consistently enough to be useful. Instead, Alexa rarely gives you what you asked for, eventually forcing you to just go do the same thing on your smartphone -- which you would have been better off doing in the first place. Alexa does little but waste your time.The voice recognition itself is usually spot-on, as you can find from reviewing the logs in the Alexa app. Unfortunately, Alexa is pretty terrible at rendering search results for calculations, definitions, or other simple questions, failing to answer >50% of the time. ("Hmm, I don't know that one" is a far too common - and obnoxiously smug - response.) Worse, it rarely plays the song you actually asked for, even if you very carefully specify title, artist, and album. You can either yell at Alexa several times and get nowhere, or just use Spotify Connect from your phone. I'm not sure why Alexa tends to play the most obscure, incorrect titles imaginable from Spotify, considering that Spotify's top search result is usually correct.An additional problem with music commands - and commands in general - is their hit-or-miss nature. For example, telling Alexa, "restart this album" causes her to restart the song. That the AI is still weak is understandable, but having to learn the exact combinations Alexa can and can't understand significantly reduces the usefulness of a voice-controlled device, and makes it about as fun as using a DOS prompt.The sound quality on the Dot is pretty abysmal (think tinny little smartphone speaker), which isn't surprising, but it can connect to a Bluetooth speaker. This would be fine other than two problems: you have to spend more money in order to make the Dot useful for music, and the Dot routinely decides to randomly disconnect from Bluetooth devices, failing to reconnect upon voice command until you reset one or both devices. Even when caused by the other device, this is still a practical problem that decreases the Dot's value.The Dot doesn't seem very capable of filtering out its own Bluetooth output from the microphone input, meaning that if it's playing music, you often have to shout at the top of your lungs just for, "Alexa, STOP!" Combine this with Alexa's proclivity to play the wrong song, and the Dot becomes downright frustrating. Nor does the self destruct sequence actually work.The two Echo Dots we have in our house have devolved into being voice-controlled egg timers and occasional Spotify Connect outputs (controlled by smartphone). There is really no reason to choose it for anything over saying "Ok Google" to your Android smartphone, which works far better for all purposes.

title: ONLY one device per music unlimited.
body: Can't use music unlimited on more then one device. They force you to upgrade from 3.99 to 9.99 to listen on two separate devices, not even at the same time. I thought they would sync all my account information together so as I move rooms I didn't have to bring it with me. Amazon disappointed me on this one, what a waste of a second unit. Stick with one only.Just wanted to update on the ease of use with Alexa.I ran into a few problems. I ask Alexa simple questions as "What is the review on a certain Movie?" I dont get a proper answer, and that happens with a lot of questions.Also, in order to do certain features, you have to remember the application you used as a skill to ask Alexa, otherwise you wont get a response.For example, if you want local headlines from your newpaper which is probably on the internet (which you can easliy use your phone for but want the convience of Alexa) she cant help you.You want yelp ratings for a favorite restaurant? or a suggestion of restaurants to try? Well, Alexa cant help you with that either.Or thought about having your books from Kindle Unlimited read to you while you are busy around the house. Well, you need to purchase the audible books seperate from the Kindle Unlimited, even if you sign up for audible.com you only get 2 free audible books a month.My expectations were to have music where i wanted, the web read to me when i asked a question, kindle unlimited to read me books, give me reviews and star ratings for local places and movies. Purchase movie tickets, reserve me a table at a restaurant. Turn my applications into simple commands. Turn my bedroom lights on and not (specific skill) turn my bedroom lights on (honestly i cant remember all these skills names)What i would like to see in the future is:Ask a question, get an answer.GIve a command and it responds (of course after the intial setup of course)Give me things that i dont have to use my phone for: local news, breaking news, lotto numbers, reviews on movies and parental reviews on them. Yelp star ratings, local suggestions of places, Trip advisor reviews. Accident reports for your commute. Traffic reports from your location to a destination.Pros: Music is great if you get the upgraded package, you can play pandora and other apps as well.News flash for your major cities is nice.Weather reports are a big plus (no 5 day forcast)Alarm clock and timers (wish there was an LED display for time on it)Sleeping melodies as water, and rain fallAble to connect with a blutooth speaker (only one speaker) Echo is unable to connect to an external speaker.

title: The AI needs a ton of work; and they need to focus less on "selling things".
body: This is a product that I wanted to love, but it just didn’t pan out. I own four “Dots” and two “Echo” devices. I’ve had them in my house for a period of about 8 months.Firstly, the AI needs to be improved – drastically. I feel Amazon went out their way to create a talking “sales” interface versus a true home device. Any time you deviate from an Amazon-related question, “Alexa” seems to really struggle. If it is not a basic question, she stumbles.Secondly Alexa doesn’t seem to know when to listen and speak versus keeping quiet. I have a Midwestern accent – which is akin to saying I have almost no accent at all. I articulate words so clearly that you could put me on a newscast tomorrow. But, at least 15% - 20% of the time, Alexa doesn’t understand that she is being spoken to. Having to speak to the interface over and over ruins the experience. Conversely, you can be sitting there – doing nothing – and Alexa will pipe up. Last night, she started going off about some contest that I did not prompt her for. And she wouldn’t keep quiet no matter how many times I told her to stop. She just kept trying to follow whatever predetermined protocol she was given for the contest. It took about 10 attempts to get her stop. I considered unplugging her.I emailed Amazon “Support” and they recommended muting the device… Are you kidding me?? Doesn’t that TOTALLY defeat the purpose??? And they insulted my intelligence.Thirdly, the Alexa phone app interface is kludgy and impractical. It defaults to open at “Conversations” – which you probably aren’t having. It takes 3 or 4 steps to get to a meaningful portion of the app for any function you may actually want to use. The app is HEAVY with propaganda, which I find annoying. I’ve purchased your products – and your never-ending attempt to re-sell me is accomplishing the exact opposite. I just ordered four Google Home devices because I’m getting fed up with the Alexa interface and the corresponding app.And let’s talk about features: Amazon has an amazing ability to build features that nobody wants and nobody is asking for. I get emails about once a week with features that are beyond useless.Here’s an idea: I have six Amazon devices placed throughout my home. It would be awesome if I could ask for a song on one device and hear it simultaneously on every device in my home. Google Home is miles ahead of you there.Finally, there’s no easy way to leave product feedback. It takes a while to find it in the app. Why can’t I give my feedback directly to Alexa?Save your money until the AI has been drastically improved.

title: Didn't work in a second bedroom 35 feet from router even after being returned and refurbished.
body: I have the Amazon Echo tall and wanted to stream music to a stereo. I have the Dot in a bedroom about 35 ft from the router. The Echo DOT is unusable in that bedroom. If I ask it a question or tell it to turn on a WEMO outlet very often it will stall out and then the ring will go red saying sorry I lost connection. Once I got it to try to stream music it will constantly stall playing music and give up trying. I also tried using the hard wire RCA connection to the sound system from Dot but it had a noticeable baseline hiss that made that option irritating. I did send it back for an exchange but they said they refurbished it and returned it to me. Whatever was fixed didn't help it work any better. I ended up getting a Bose Soundlink https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01K6P08FA/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o02_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1 and it streams music from Amazon prime very well (not using the Amazon Echo or Dot but using a built in app). The sound from the Bose is INCREDIBLE. I can't use the voice command on it like the echo but at least it works where the dot failed badly. The Bose won't connect to the Amazon Echo tall like I had hoped either.The sound quality from the Bose was FAR better than Echo through Bluetooth. I used the RCA connectors from Bose and the optical connector. I could discern a noticeable bit of better headroom and stereo separation via the optical route but the RCA connection was excellent as well since it did not induce any hiss, squeal or noise like all the Bluetooth gadgets I have tried. Yes, the Bose is 149.00 verses the Dot 49.00 but if you want supreme quality sound from Amazon Prime music, PC, NAS on intranet or internet radio It is hard to imagine a better product than Bose. I am a big fan of Bose speakers but I can honestly say that did not influence this review. I was only interested in listening to streaming music and for now Bose is the OINLY solution. I also have a Denon HEOS to compare with the Bose. BOSE WINS hands down on that contest. I call the Denon HEOS the Denon P-OS. The Bose plays my music from my NAS far better than the Denon and its apps. I have the Synology Disk station ds415play. The Bose app found it and was able to play my music better than my sony receivers, sony blueray players, the ROKU media players and my Vizio smart TVs. I can switch to next track super fast through Bose and go back and forth between folders on the NAS faster than even the Synology DS Audio app. If you want to play your music from a NAS Bose does it the best of anything I have found. I offer all of this verbage about the Bose to help anyone else looking for a streaming solution.

title: Buy with caution!
body: When Amazon first announced that they would be coming out with a smaller and cheaper version of their Echo speakers with Alexa, I was very excited. From the reviews, the product seemed great, a smaller version of the already successful Echo, but for around half of the price, $89.99. This was the Echo Dot 1st generation. The only problem was that to purchase this device you would have had to have owned the original full sized Echo. This was a deal killer for me as I didn’t have the original echo, and wasn’t going to buy one to purchase the Echo Dot off of eBay for an increased price. Then comes the Echo Dot 2nd generation in October of this year. I was very excited for this device. Finally, an affordable alternative to the full sized echo, for half the price of the already inexpensive echo dot 1st generation. This Echo Dot 2nd generation cost $49.99. Initially, I was every impressed with the device, it worked, responding to all of my questions, and playing everything i asked it to. (You can check out my Unboxing and First Impressions Here), but the more I started to play with Echo Dot, the more I saw it’s flaws. First, It is slow. Often times when I ask it to turn off my lights, in the time that it takes Echo to respond, I could have walked over and turned off my lights manually. This is a really big deal for me because that kind of defeats the purpose for the whole voice assistant thing anyways. Secondly, often times the Echo Dot just pauses in the middle of talking. Alexa is in the middle of telling me the weather, and right about when she is about to tell me the good part, Alexa goes silent and I hear the dreaded phrase complaining about a connection to the internet. Third, (this one really got me), I have attempted to purchase things many times using voice, and it just doesn’t work. For example, I might say “Alexa, order AA Batteries.”, and Alexa responds, “Amazon’s Choice for AA Batteries is Amazon Basics 48 pack”. Not only did Alexa not answer my question, she didn’t get my sale to amazon. One of the huge draws for Amazon about consumers having this device in their homes is the ability to purchase items off Amazon with voice and if that doesn’t work, we have a problem. Lastly, I have to restart the Echo Dot about once per day because it just stops responding, this is a huge problem for a device that is supposed to be always on and always ready.In conclusion, I do not think that the Amazon Echo Dot would be a good buy for the average consumer. Enthusiasts like me will learn to deal with the hiccups, but the average consumer will learn to hate Alexa.

title: Fun for some, rarely useful, and ultimately just a toy.
body: Having used voice assistants for over a year and Echo products for a few months I can honestly say that, on average, their real world value is very low. These things are just toys for people with a lot of disposable income.Now, I understand that there are people that really like these things (it’s a fun toy). And I recognize that there are situations where a voice assist can be truly valuable to people in specific situations (such as for those with mobility and accessibility issues). But these are edge cases and don’t reflect the reality for most users. Which is that these thing don’t work reliability, consistently, or simply enough to replace existing processes for doing almost anything.Playing music can be awesome. Finding an artist, song or album usually works great. Usually. But when it doesn’t, it’s painful. And try playing it on something other than your Echo? The command string, reliability of recognition, high likelihood you’ll need to repeat yourself multiple times, or that it will work at all, usually mean you could have done it faster yourself on a phone, tablet or computer.The same goes for ordering something. If you didn’t buy the exact same thing from Amazon recently, forget it. What about news, traffic, and weather? Yup, most anything else is faster, and more targeted and reliable. Basically, Echo is great when you have a very simple command and don’t care too much about the outcome. Which is a really, a pretty limited set of circumstances. And beyond the use cases, you need to deal with the Alexa app experience—which is horrible—and the limitations of third party integration. When taken together, Echo requires so much extra effort that it’s basically costing me time and money to use it.Now these thing have dropped in price to the point where most people can afford one or two or ten. And like I said, they’re just a toy, and when they do the simple thing well, people rave about them. But at some point during your ownership experience, you will start to experience the limitations and challenges. And maybe you won’t care. We live in a disposable society and when this thing is no longer useful or interesting, you can dump it and never look back. But if you’re curious and interested in buying one, and wondering if they’re worth it, I can save you a lot of time. If you want a new toy or a speaker for your kitchen. Great, have fun. Want a useful tool? Don’t bother. Stick with your phone/tablet/computer. It does all these things and more, and works a lot better.

title: False and emply promises of home automation
body: I've spent weeks trying to make this do something OTHER than act as an alarm clock and someone to ask the weather. It won't. I've attempted to integrate it with my TV, a sonos, philips hue, wemo, osram lightify, and harmony.......it works inconsistently at best. No matter what device I replace to something that is supposedly "alexa compatible" I still end up spending days trying to make them talk and then after a bout a week, they stop working with eachother and I have to reconfigure them. The idea of home automation is a false promise being sold by tech companies who manage to make this thing work in a vacuum. It won't work in real world conditions, only the ideal "clean room"environment of their lab.Update 1/12/18Someone tried to call me and get more info but I was never able to connect with then so here's this:I have attempted to use my echo to control my stereo, my lights, my TV etc. All I can EVER do with some reliability is get the time and weather.I have found the following:1 - If you have a 2g and 5G network, you will have problems.
