ATT is doing a thing where they want you to have the SSIDs the same so that you can bounce between the networks but if you do that, your home automation will crater. So after all my devices kept going stupid after a few days, I broke the traffic shaping nonsense that ATT was doing and put all my home automation on 2g.2 - My wemo mini plugs would still fall off the network every few days3 - My wemo switch would lose it's config altogether and have to be reconfigured about once a week4 - I tried Lightify, that switch was more reliable but the lights were hit and miss. I had one light that wouldn't change colors in a specfic lamp even though it is the same model as the other lamp that works fine with a lightify bulb.5 - My harmony home hub also falls off the network every few days6 - When my echo can see the sonos, it can really only play a radio station because it usually can't get the harmony to start my stereo even though i bought a new Pioneer 1131 VSX SPECIFICALLY because it has wifi and bluetooth and should work with all this other stuff.All of this means that when I ask Alexa to turn on something, she says "I cannot connect to blah blah" so I don't get to say "Alexa, watch Netflix" or "Alexa, listen to metallica on sonos" or "Alexa, turn off the bedroom lights". But I can say "Alexa, what time is it".

title: Expected to much?
body: I've been reading reviews on the Echo and the Dot since the Echo was released. I have a Fire TV and a Fire Stick and had a Fire tablet at one time, all with Alexa. None of them were very good on the Alexa front. Very solid products otherwise.I guess from all the oohhhing and aahhing, I expected to much. Maybe it doesn't take much to impress the average Amazon shopper.Google Assistant and Google can run circles around this thing. Even Siri looks like a genuis in comparison. Haven't tried any smart home integration as I'm not into that at this point.I didn't buy mine from Amazon, it was part of a package deal with a Soundtouch 10 Bose speaker I purchased directly from Bose.This is getting off track , but, the speaker combined with the Bose app and connected to wi-fi is OUTSTANDING if anyone is interested. The package was only $14 more with the Dot, so I'll play around with it a little more to see what I can get it to do. But my opinion right now is it is no better than what's built in the other products. Definitely not wort buying as a standalone item,voice recognition is terrible( I was sitting three feet from it and it didn't respond until I raised my voice to almost a yell), Not acceptable. 9 out of ten responses are "I don't know the answer" or "I'm afraid I don't understand". If I WANTED to talk to an idiot, I'd call customer service and ask them questions.Edit: Enable "Smack Talker" in skills. It's hilarious and she definitely isn't shy! Not suitable for those with no sense of humour or are prudish.Working with your phrasing and wording helps, but it is miles from a resemblance to natural conversation like Google Assistant. You must speak the wake word on every question or command. If she is always eavesdropping on you anyway, why can't she recognize when you are addressing her? Oh well at least she doesn't call the cops on you for saying the wrong thing, like Google Home will do. lol! Raised rating one star on Smack Talker alone, I love that skill. Like every other skill, you must tell her to open it every single time you want to use it. Example: Alexa open smack talker, when she acknowledges, say, have smack talker speak to John. She will respond by speaking to that name and delivering an insult. Sophomoric I admit, but, can be funny to get a rise out of someone who isn't familiar with Alexa. Think of it as an automatic "Simon Says" trick you can do with the remote.

title: Voice remote faulty after software update.
body: Update: I had been contacted several times from Echo Dot support but problems still persist. It constantly drops its connection with the bluetooth speaker. I have to tell the Dot to "connect my speaker". It usually is successful but still happens sporadically. And its response to the remote continues to have a few issues. None of these problems happen with my first gen Dots. Amazon support communication has failed to provide a permanent fix and they don't contact me anymore. Disappointed.We've had the original Echo and the 1st Generation Dot and voice remotes for both. I recently ordered a six pack of the new 2nd generation dots. I hooked up one of the new ones. It works perfectly fine except for the voice remote. So I tried a second dot. Same result. The problem occurred on both new ones AFTER they downloaded and applied a new software/firmware update. (And I confirmed this with two different bluetooth speakers and two different Echo Voice Remotes on two different new 2nd generation echo dots.) What happens is when you (after the software update) press the voice command button on the remote, it may not respond at all on the first try after it has been idle for several hours. The second attempt it works ok. BUT if I give it another command thru the remote while it is playing a radio station or song, when I press and hold the voice command button it doesn't give us the feedback tones and it plays back broken bits of what's playing on the speaker while I'm trying to give it a command. It then may or may not perform the requested command. This does not occur if giving the dot voice commands directly to it and not via a voice remote. My technical background with computers and electronic devices tells me, thru all of my testing and resetting and plugging and unplugging, that this is a software issue that came thru with the update the dots have received shortly after they are activated for the first time. I have contacted Amazon Dot support via phone. My call was escalated to a higher tier tech support who agreed that this sounds like an issue that needs to be addressed with the Alexa software engineers. So he created a support ticket and suggested that I also write a review on the Alexa app. I give the first generation dots with 5 stars because they've been flawless. I give the second generation dots four stars because of this issue with the voice remote. As soon as this gets corrected, I'll gladly change my review to 5 stars.

title: The Echo just replies with "I don't know" to most stuff that isn't super basic or super specific in nature
body: The Amazon Echo is dumber than Google Home. I have both and constantly find myself waiting for answers from the Echo with the same expectations I do from the Google Home and she often fails to meet those expectations. You can say anything to the Google Home and with even the far out stuff you wouldn't expect a decent answer to, it will come back with top search results that are usually what you were looking for as if you punched your question into a google search engine in a web browser. The Echo just replies with "I don't know" to most stuff that isn't super basic or super specific in nature.I knew that the Echo Dot's speaker was going to be inferior to the Home and to the full sized Echo before buying it, and let me tell you it certainly is. It's "good enough" to listen to Alexa speak to you in a normal room environment, but if there is a lot of noise, you have to turn up the volume to maximum, which is also not a good solution because at max volume the speaker begins to distort and becomes more difficult to understand. Listening to any kind of music is out of the question. When hooked up to my home theater system via the 3.5mm headphone out jack it sounds pretty good though, however, doing so takes away the instant convenience of simply talking to Alexa and getting a result because you have to switch your receiver input to the channel the Echo is hooked up to in order to hear it's response.The Echo Dot has bluetooth, which was one of the reasons I bought it thinking I could connect it via bluetooth to my bluetooth receiver. What ends up happening though is that after connecting, the receiver will send signal to be played through the Echo Dot instead of the Dot sending signal to be played through the receiver which is the opposite that I want to happen.Music selection and play functionality is pretty good with the Echo Dot. It finds and recognized what you are asking it to play quite fast and efficiently. Then after a certain number of plays, you get hit with the hard fact that you must now start paying for your music service, and all the good songs are unavailable to non-subscription users. If you have the music in your Amazon music service though, it's all good.My suggestion is go with the Google Home if you want a more capable conversation companion. The speaker quality difference is granted. They are about the same in capability in all the rest of the ways.

title: BUYER BEWARE  NOT AS SMART AS YOU MIGHT THINK
body: Alexa isn't what I expected even though I now have two. It isn't all that intelligent as you must remember key words to invoke any skill. With 15000 skills and climbing that is impossible.. Alexa should be able to determine by what you are asking what skill would handle that. To arms my alarm in the house I have to go through a miriad of crap when I could either pick up my celllphone and arm it a hundred times faster or walk over to the alarm and push the button. Clearly GOOGLE is still the enemy and so they use BING but Alexa doesn't always give you an answer. If I ask a question I can see it searched on bing by looking at the app but it never returned a verbal answer to me. "I don't that one" or something similar comes up. If I ask my GOOGLE on my phone the exact same question I get an answer each and every time. Also I have had zero luck in my DOT creating a contact list of anyone who has the app or the appliance in their home so I can't call anyone. GOOGLE calls anyone in the United States on their Device. As far as skills go 90% of them are garbage skills and again to invoke them YOU must remember the keywords or forget it. Not going to work. Take example ambient room noise to sleep. There must be ten different skills with ten different phrases to invoke the apps. One would think I should be able to say, "Alexa play ambient sleeping sounds" and it should ask "which type". If I say one that does not exist it could then ask me if I want a nature noise or animal noise etc. followed then by which in that genre it has. Probably the easiest skill is my thermostat. I can have Alexa change it with relative ease. So you get the idea and last the wake word for the device. They should let you add maybe two to three others. I have a habit of calling it Alexis which wakes it up but then it doesn't respond and it takes me forever to realize I have not said Alexa. And if you buy more than one you must change the wake word on each device individually. Right now this is a toy. Needs lots of work on their sever end to make it a truly useful product that can do almost whatever you ask. There is no intelligent processing of your request going on here. My advice is that its become so cheap why not buy it but don't expect much. Apps on your cellphone right now work quicker and you can access much faster and you don't have to remember some keyword to get the app or skill to work.

title: It's great when it works.
body: It's great when it works. But setup was a pain, As if I had never heard Albert Einstein's saying, "doing the same thing over and over again, expecting a different out come is a sign of insanity"... I turned my phone off, unplugged the echo and then started the app, the I did in in reverse order tried setting it up again and again, 30 times or more each with a minute (meaning little, small) variation in protocol. Sometimes I would press the dot button sometimes I would not, sometimes I would wait 5 minutes to see if it need to think about it. Each time I would get stuck after the echo turned amber and I had connected to the Echo via WiFi. Then I would search the web for trouble shooting advice, there was none. So back I would go over and over again until at about the 60th time it finally went to the next step. I can't tell you what I did differently that time. Probably nothing. It was so mind numbing by that time my mind was numb! My son thinks it was because we were setting up with a Motorola X pure. His google phone did it first try, Further evidence of that was that my Daughter also has a Moto X pure and she had the same trouble. She also did not remember what did the trick, thus I was in 2 hours of purgatory, on two separate evenings. Why get one: when I'm not sure yet, it sure makes me more of a couch potato. I can now turn off the outdoor lights with my voice and at those at the dining room table via my Insteon Gateway. My son can change the light color in his room via Phillips Hue, our Nissan Leaf is supposed to work with it, so while planning a trip at the dinner table I guess I can ask how much charge my leaf has. It seems it is good to tell time in the dark, it will work when the TV is on but it doesn't like the in window AC. My Son and daughter demonstrated the little gimmicks like letting it read you a Wikipedia article, or having it tell you a joke, it will set a reminder, make a to do list or read you things from your calendar, but I still fail to see the killer app, that does things so much easier. Guess we all want to play Star Trek....I don't need an external speaker, the one in the dot is just fine for me (I'm 53 and remember transistor radios and analog music over FM or even AM). My Adult Children Both insist it is not good enough and they need higher fidelity from a better speaker. I guess it is a matter of what you think you need.

title: Least Helpful Tech Product Purchased in a Long Time
body: I received this as a gift and due to who gave it to me, I wanted to like it. But there is very little that it helps me do that I can't do elsewhere...PROs: - It makes use of my Amazon Prime Music stations. This is the only reason why I have any interest in keeping it. I really like Amazon Prime Music (six stars) and this is the best stand alone streaming device when I am at home (as long as I keep it on one station).- "Alexa! Tell me a joke." and other easter eggs. It can be a good source for party tricks.- Other PROs? Ummmm, I can't think of one at the moment.CONs: - You have to speak everything. That's the only functional interface.- You have to spend more time thinking about how to phrase your command than it takes to click through menus on an app for other devices.- It doesn't work very much like Hal (another easter egg, by the way). I can recognize your speech pretty well, but it can't interpret the thought in your spoken words very well. I cannot imagine ordering products this way without having visual feedback about what I ordered.- If your spoken command was interpreted incorrectly you waste even more time.- The Android app is virtually useless. You have almost no control over the Dot via the app, other than showing you what you've done. Plus, the app takes FOREVER to load. Maybe I get if I open after restarting my phone and opening the Alexa app the first time. But if I just hop over to another app, send a message or take a call, it seems to back to step one.- It's not wireless. It needs a power cord plugged into the wall to operate. It would be nice to have a battery so I can take Alexa around the house with me.- It looses Bluetooth paring with my speakers almost every day. You can't just go through paring to find the device. You have to forget paring and re-pair (which requires menus on two devices, really three when you consider the Alexa menu is on an app).In short, as long as I can play one of my favorite music stations, it stays, otherwise it will go into the closet which would be a big disappointment to the person who gifted this to me if they ever find out.- Knowing that Alexa is ALWAYS listening is kind of creepy. This causes you to mute the microphone taking away the advantage of calling out "Alexa!" arbitrarily.

title: Do not buy (2nd Gen Dot) unless you like Alexa telling you that she can't connect to the internet.
body: I'll start off saying I have the original Gen 1 Echo as well as an Echo Dot Gen 1 so I have been working with these devices for a long time. I recently ordered the Gen 2 model to add to my collection only to be terribly disappointed. I do like that the internal speaker quality is better than the Gen 1 model but other than that this device is garbage. My biggest complaints are as follows:1. Wireless connectivity. This thing drops its network connection constantly - it is by far the most reliable trait of the new dot. I have tried rebooting it - yes that works but I am not going to do that multiple times per day, that is just stupid on its face. Other recommendations have been to use a 5ghz network (yep already doing that, try again) and to place it centrally (it is about 15 feet from the router with only one thin wall in between whereas every other device in my house is much farther away with multiple impeding walls.. so.. nope - try again).2. Hard wired connection to the speaker. I have this plugged into a small speaker using the 3.5mm jack. When I initially set up the dot the app asked how it would be connected so I selected wired. It worked fine until the battery died in the speaker so I had to unplug it until recharged. Switching back to the internal speaker happened automatically but it failed to push audio through the speaker and the app didn't have a setting to just select the output without going back through setup.. what?! I literally had to reboot the dot several times and turn the speaker on and off until it finally started piping audio out through the 3.5mm jack... during this entire process no sound at all was coming from the dot either from the internal or external speaker.3. More of a preference than a problem but I really liked the Gen 1 rotating bezel for volume, it kept the top of the puck looking more clean and was a very nice method of interacting. To me the volume buttons on top make it look and feel like any other generic piece of hardware with nothing to really differentiate it.Long story short I will likely be returning this junk, it isn't even worth 1/2 of the $30 discounted cyber monday price. Come on Amazon - your company is worth Billions of dollars (literally) - get your act together and invest in quality assurance, there is no reason for you to produce junk.

title: Wait until Amazon makes improvements if you’re considering buying
body: I would like to start by saying I bought the Echo Dot because it got MUCH better reviews than the regular Echo. I did not want to buy the regular Echo because many reviews talked about how slow the software is. That being said...I feel as if this product is lacking in so many ways. For $50, I am only using this to ask what the weather is outside. I have also used the Echo Dot to reorder things from Amazon. I find that because Amazon does a pretty terrible job at regulating pricing on household items and food, it is much easier to open the Amazon app and search for the best prices than use the Echo Dot. However, if you have a very specific product that you order regularly, I can see how the reorder feature may be useful to you. The news briefings are pretty terrible and there aren’t a whole lot of news sites to chose from. I also would appreciate it if they would not play recipes when I ask for news, I feel as though that should be a superset category- maybe “Alexa, could you play the cooks daily briefing?” Again, it is easier for me to go to my phone to look up the news. I don’t like the news stories that are provided on the Echo.When you use the Alexa app to add skills and smart home features, you will notice right away there are almost zero apps that get over three stars. I have found that many of the skills do not work at all. It looks like other people have the same problem.Another problem the Echo dot and Echo have is that you must have it plugged in for it to work. It’s 2018. No one could find a way to make this run on a battery? Apparently the Echo Tap does not need to be plugged in, but you also cannot use the Ask Alexa feature while it is unplugged. Why would Amazon not work that out before releasing a product? It just tells me that not a whole lot of thought was put into this.Also, I would like to note that if I say I to play songs by a certain artist, it plays the wrong artist about a quarter of the time. The speaker quality is below average but it was not like I was buying this for the speaker.Basically, I use this to tell me the temperature every day. I have a thermometer with a LED screen sitting next to it that cost $20. The Echo Dot cost $50. I know the Echo Dot does much moare than the thermometer, but it doesn’t do anything that well.

title: Very irritating experience
body: So I finally decided to jump on the Alexa/Echo train because I kept seeing all the ads and thought some of the items would be convenient. I mainly decided to purchase because I wanted to use the smart home functions and to play sleep sounds. Everything else was just a bonus. Those two things were my deciding factor.After I got the Dot set up, after about a day it wouldn’t find my lights. Per the instructions I found, I had it search again. In the Alexa app, I now have two offices and two bathrooms. So if I asked to turn on the lights in the office, Alexa told me to rename because I had two of the same. It doesn’t allow you to delete one. You can disable, but this didn’t make any difference. I should mention, my Phillips app and my ISO Home still listed just one of each room, no issues. So I renamed the disabled ones and it didn’t make any difference, still said the same thing. So I renamed everything and it appears to be working now but it was frustrating to figure all that out.Second problem and the thing that is really making me post this review. I have problems sleeping. I need something playing, some sort of noise in order to sleep and it can’t stop. If it stops, I wake up in a panic. I thought this would be a good method to use. However, the loop function does NOT WORK. It is intermittent in when it decides to work or not. First night, I played thunderstorm. I was pleased, it worked great. Second night, I played the same thing, it played once then stopped. Alexa said something like it had difficulty playing. So I figured to try brown noise. Made sure the loop was active, played once then stopped. Next night it worked O_o. Next night it didn’t. Tried Desert. Played once then stopped. Tried rain, played once then stopped. At this point, I am getting really pissed off. How hard is it to have a repeat function really? Some of the things I have read have said it might be my internet? Really? Because my apple products in the same room have never had any issue. Neither does my Amazon Fire tv. I stream a lot of videos, no problem. Which takes a lot more than repeating what is essentially a song over and over.I am going to try a reset and see if that helps but if not, I will probably return this.

title: Not kid friendly enough - inappropriate access to adult Kindle books
body: This device is a game changer - Works really well, but not as kid friendly yet as I would like. You have to trust your kids. One feature difficult to disable is the Phone and messaging. I have the Echo version 1 in my den, the kids liked it, so I bought them Dots for Christmas and let them have at it. The phone was enabled on my original Echo, and that carried through to the kids' Dots. In just 3 days, The oldest kid thought it funny to teach the youngest how th tell Alexa to call people on my contact list - and since my cell phone shows up as the caller id, not a house phone, while I'm at work, I have friends / family "returning my calls" that I never made from my mobile. Good the kids told me that day, because it acts like you phone is cloned or hacked. Since calling bypasses you phone, anyone and everyone in your house can make a call from your cell even if your cell phone is locked. This calling feature, once enabled, cannot be enabled/disabled from the Echo or the App. It would be nice if you could place a passcode phrase or complicated pin number string to wake up the Echo or certain commands and skills - otherwise anyone could walk into a house and prank "Alexa, call mom". Also, since the first Echo linked to my mobile phone, my oldest kid or wife's mobile number could never show up on caller id I hate to have to buy the Echo Connect to fix this caller id problem. Another issue with kids - I don't think I can do selective enable/disable explicit lyrics by Dot device on one account. Scheduling issue - Other than "do not disturb" for an adult, cannot schedule a Kid's Dot usage on/off from the App.Another thing, Since your account is linked to your Kindle books, your kids can have access to any Kindle books you may have purchased - kids asking her to read inappropriate material is just a matter of time. Maybe someday as these things mature, Echo could be trained to recognize an Adult vs Kid's voice, and have proper parental lock outs by device. If I had to do it again, I wouldn't have bought these dots for the kids - I'd wait until the technology matures with better parental lock outs, and content default settings by device with a simple question like age of Dot user.

title: Very Frustrating Experience
body: Echo Dot 2nd GenerationYou will not like this review since it is in opposition of some 117682 reviews in which 89% rating the device average or above.I ordered the Echo Dot , 2nd Generation that was sold with the Sengled Element Classic Smart LED Light Bulbs, A19 Dimmable LED Light Soft White 2700K 60W Equivalent, Starter Kit (2 A19 bulbs + hub), Works with Alexa / Google Assistant / IFTTT . The combined order was made because of the potential of voice control of lighting in my entertainment room.The Sengled Elemet Classic Smart LED Light Bulb WAS NOT EVALUATED, but I suspect that it would perform as indicated. As of 9/222/18, 719 had rated this item at 74% average or above and 26% rated it below average. Since I did not get the Echo Dot 2nd Genereation to work, I did not evaluate this item.The sales hype promises Echo Dot can accomplish many tasks and 89% of the ratings as of 9/22/18 seem to agree. My experience was one of frustration. The shipment came as indicated with both items in one shipping box. I left the Sengled Element Classic Smart LED Light Bul(s) of which there were two untouched.After several attempts, over several days, I was unable to get Echo Dot 2nd Generation to recognize my voice. I was able, after some considerable effort to connect with my internet but when I tried to connect with the Echo Dot, it broke the internet connedtion. I tried the Alexa app on both my smart phone and on a laptop. Long story short: Never got the item to work! Frustration was high. This experience has set against devices such as this and I will probably never try to use one again.Some observatins. If you can get a connection thru the AMAZON-XXX WiFi to work, there is no security option available. When the echo dot spoke during set-up it was not very loud so a larger speaker might be needed.The Echo Dot refers you to the Alexa app for details but the Alexa app is not very forthcoming. Also the app provides many skills and activities it can perform but some may require additional cost or equipment to work.All in All, very frustration. I hope you have better luck than I did!

title: Alexa sits right next to me and still gets the commands I give her wrong a lot of the time.. save your money
body: I have been using my Echo Dot in my office for the past six months or so now. It sits right on my desk to the right of my keyboard. My biggest complaint is that Alexa has a hard time hearing me even though its less than a foot away from me. I have a loud voice and speak very clear English but somehow she cannot understand the commands I give her. This happens about 25% of the time. Lately, Alexa also seems to take a long time to process my request to turn on my lights/fan which is sent through my Smartthings hub. Now, this delay could be a Smartthings problem, but given the fact that Alexa is not understanding my requests, im leaning more towards Alexa. The first month or two after I got my Echo Dot, it was quick to command my smart devices.If the Echo Dot is supposed to work from across the room, why does it fail to hear or understand me when I am less than a foot away? Im a big techie and thought this would be a cool thing to experiment with and to automate my home. The one thing I can say about all these smart devices, they are not very smart. Im really not interested in beta testing products when I am paying for something that is being advertised as ready for prime time. With so many hubs and switches to control all the devices in our lives, there are many incompatibilities and quirks that keep me from expanding the use of items like the Echo. Voice recognition has come a long was over the past 10yrs but its nowhere near where it should be. I hate repeating myself, and repeating myself for a stupid device that sits <12inches from my mouth is unacceptable.Amazon, maybe instead of sending me emails every other day telling me "Whats new with Alexa", you should just have it be able to hear basic voice commands that ive been using for months now. Maybe in another 5-10yrs a universal standard for smart device protocols will be implemented (and enforced) so the consumer doesnt have to buy redundant devices from other manufacturers just so ALL the devices in their home can communicate with each other. I have completely stopped trying to automate my home any further and I plan to sit this one out until someone gets this right.

title: they’re pretty prevalent and Amazon still doesn’t have fixes for it
body: I’ve had Alexa synced to my hue lights, Sonos, Lutron, Kasa, and Ama home. The pros: they link, the cons: they don’t work all the time and customer service will only try to blame the other applications and tell you to call those respective companies for help.I finally swapped to google home as Amazon kept telling me to reset all of my applications (disconnect them all and reconnect them individually, losing all of your groups and settings in the process). My two biggest things that didn’t work that I wanted:1. full hue control (google home does this marvelously and faster and 100% of the time while Alexa for me would miss 5-10% of the bulbs [i have about 50], requiring you to issue the command of turn off the lights 3-4 times). I still have an outstanding ticket on this, but it’s evident they think this is a Philips Hue issue when a competitor Google Home can control these no problem.2. Intercomm. When trying to set this up they need to send you a code. I’ve tried this on 3 phones and the code doesn’t send. Amazon has tried multiple things, but again blames Verizon (whom I’ve called to humor Amazon, and they say they don’t have any holds or blocks). I set this up in Google home mini with no problems.Other cons for Alexa: she’s hyper sensitive and will pick up on words that have a consonant with a k for Alexa. You have to add a bunch of “brains” to her for her to be interesting, as compared to google home where it already has it built in (news, any questions you may have, measurements for when you’re in the kitchen). Also she’s very slow now that I compare her to Google, I’d estimate probably 2-3x slower based on the same action triggers I’ve asked of Google.Anyway if you look up outside of reviews for these issues on reddit, they’re pretty prevalent and Amazon still doesn’t have fixes for it. So, if you’re like me and have 7 of these bad boys around the house, I’m sorry to tell you that google home is far superior. For those of you who haven’t committed to this yet, I guarantee you google home is better and their customer service hasn’t tried to blame others, rather will look into the root cause.

title: It works great as a music player when paired with a speaker
body: I wanted to give it some time before writing a review on this so I could be fair.Pros:1. It works great as a music player when paired with a speaker. It sounds just okay on its own.2. Setting reminders works well as long as you are in the room when it goes off. Think kitchen timer.3. There are additional skills that you can enable from the Alexa App Store If you are on the fence, check these before buying.4. Questions about news or current events are answered well though mostly political but that is based on the media.5. With appropriate switches, outlets and devices you can control devices in your home.Cons:1. Being an Amazon device, you would expect to be able to review wish lists or you cart. Her response "I'm not quite sure how to help you with that."2. You can add items to your cart, but not remove.3. When you add an item, you will need to go online and select the item you want because Alexa only puts a header that asks you to select the item. This only helps if you want a reminder to search for the item yourself.4. Conversations go like this. Alexa, how do you poach an egg? The All Recipes Skill can help with that, would you like to enable it. Yes. I've found hundreds of results with egg. My recommendation is the egg noodles recipe. It takes 33 minutes with 4.5 stars. Would you like to hear it? No. I found an egg with 4.5 stars it takes 5 minutes would you like to hear the details? No. - This goes on until you say Stop. She will also ask you for any additional ingredients to narrow the search.This was just an example but short of requesting music, most questions result in frustration with the AI behind Alexa. Lots of "Hmm, I don't know that one.", "Hmm, I'm not sure.", and "Sorry, I'm not sure about that." You would think the AI would be paired with an Internet search engine but it does not appear to be. Some things are answered but most are not. Bottom line, if you want a music player or want to control devices in your home, the echo is great other wise not worth having until the AI is drastically improved.

title: I have a perfectly good BT speaker for $20 all I wanted was to ...
body: I bought the Dot to replace a Bluetooth speaker I have been using to listen to audiobooks. Unfortunately, it does not work well for this. The Dot’s speaker is just too quiet. If my furnace comes on I cannot hear the book, at the highest volume setting. My house is small (25’x25’), so I was never very far away from it.I contacted CS to see if it was a fault with the device and if I could get a replacement. I was told this was the normal volume and if I wanted more volume I would need to purchase a larger (and much more expensive) echo device.It was also suggested, I pair my Bluetooth speaker with the Dot. The problem is, I purchased this device so I could listen to audiobooks while working around the house (including washing dishes) and be able to answer the phone (set cooking timers/pause when vacuuming) without having to touch it. If I pair the speaker and am downstairs away from the Dot, I would not be able to answer a call. I purchased a battery with the Dot to make it portable.I have a perfectly good BT speaker for $20 all I wanted was to be able to have the same sound, but with hands-off convenience. I paid about $80 for the Dot and the battery. I think for $60 more than the BT speaker, hands off would be a reasonable expectation. Even just trying to hear the Dot tell me a daily news update, in a quiet room with the furnace running, is a challenge to hear. I am surprised, disappointed and frustrated Amazon put such a weak speaker in a potentially great device. I understand this device was not meant for playing loud music, but I think my expectation of being able to hear an audiobook while doing housework is not unreasonable. A larger Echo device makes no sense for my budget or for being able to easily carry it around. Also, purchasing multiple devices, so I can hear it in every room does not work for my budget. I can take my BT speaker outside when gardening too.I will say the Amazon CS rep was very kind and helpful. I was able to return the Dot. Most of the time, I am very happy with my Amazon experience. I hope in the near future Amazon will consider making a device that fits my needs.

title: I don't get the purpose of it
body: I got one of these for Christmas, and don't get the point of it. I don't think it solves any problem in my life. I don't have a stereo, or any smart home things for it to control, and have no plans to get any, just to have a use for this thing. I don't find it terribly inconvenient to manually turn lights on and off, and already have touch lights where useful. Beyond controlling things I don't own, and getting a few extra deals on Amazon for things I don't need, I don't know what the echo dot does that my smartphone doesn't already do better, since I can take my phone with me wherever I go, and I don't have to use voice commands to interact with my phone apps. Even when alone, talking to echo, siri, cortana, android, or my new cable box makes me feel like an a-hole. Aside from when I'm driving, I don't find using voice commands to be significantly easier than a keyboard or touch screen, and in fact, much prefer to read information than to hear it dictated by a computer voice (which I haven't yet found a way to change to a male voice on through echo dot, which might at least be a little less annoying, and which all my other devices allow).While true that all of these devices have come a long way in being able to understand humans, in my opinion, there's still a long way to go before they're truly useful in the lives of sighted, able bodied people (though I sincerely appreciate the existence of these options for the differently abled, and imagine that these things are life changing for those that need them).5 stars for the existence of such a device, as there are many people who benefit greatly from being able to use voice control. 1 star from me personally, as I have no idea what to use it for (I'm giving an extra star just because I'm glad it exists, and it does seem to be good at the things it does, even though I don't need those things)It is currently sitting in my room, muted, with the microphone off (it kept responding to a tv show I was watching), and it has been like that since the day after Christmas. It'll probably stay like that until I need that plug again, when it'll wind up in a junk drawer.

title: Not terribly bright or helpful an awful lot of the time, but moderately good play value.
body: *** Update: downgraded rating because "skills" don't work unless language setting matches country of your Amazon account - what is this? putting programmer convenience ahead of customer needs? This problem has been reported by multiple people in multiple forums for over a year *** I have two echo dots, one was a gift, one was purchased from Amazon. One is plugged in, always on, always there. The other is in a [[ASIN:B0749K9C1T MOKCAO POWER+ Cordless Speaker with Battery Base for Amazon Echo Dot 2nd Generation]], so it isn't always on, and is often somewhere else. Mostly, I like them. Not as much as I like my Google Home Mini or other Google Assistant devices, but they are an interesting variation to go back and forth between female Alexa and set-to-male-voice Google Assistant. I haven't found anything yet that Alexa does better, other than accept voice requests for Amazon music, but it's fun to explore the variety of skills. Neither of them does very well at recognizing foreign names (Google does a little better, and when it fails, its bad guesses don't seem to be as consistently awful), so they tend not to recognize music artist or titles out of the more recent or best known few. Very frustrating, especially since most of my dozens of playlists have names that Alexa doesn't understand, so will have to be renamed. But that's an artifact of the way they were designed. Another big frustration is that neither can set to give the time in 24-hour format, though Amazon will give the time in 24-hour format if you can remember how to ask, and Google (far more usefully) will accept 24-hour time in voice inputs. That is simple insensitivity to the needs of others, there is no good excuse, though I suspect that Siri is the same way, and we must all simply follow in Apple's footsteps, no matter how wrong-headed. I thought I was going to buy an Echo Spot next, so I could see what I was listening to in a shuffled playlist, instead of yelling at Alexa to tell me, then listening to the butchered pronunciations, but that will wait at least until it displays 24-hour time.

title: I can no longer abide the Dot's shortcomings
body: We've had an Echo for almost 2 years, and the Dot since May. Frankly, there's a lot to like about the system. But, two major flaws has me switching to Google Home.1) The audio cuts out when using external speakers: We've gone round and round, troubleshooting on our own and with Amazon tech support, trying to get this sorted. I'm convinced it's a software bug that Amazon won't admit to. From reading the various forums and Reddit out there, I'm clearly not the only user experiencing this. What happens is that while playing back music or Alexa herself is speaking, the audio intermittently just stops. It's like the Dot just forgets that it's supposed to be outputting audio. Very consistently however, it only does it with the external speakers, not the internal ones (but the internal ones are rather meh). We've tried: bluetooth and Aux audio outputs, 2.4 & 5 Ghz wifi, resetting the device, restarting the router, replacing the Dot with a new one, changing the audio source (Tune In, Pandora, Amazon Prime Music, etc), deleting and re-adding our audio accounts (Pandora), etc. No matter what we change, the results are the same: Internal speakers = no audio drops, External speakers = audio drops up to every 10 seconds. We've effectively ruled out that it's not the WiFi, the audio content, the hardware (unless it's a design flaw, not a manufacturing issue, or we got 2 duds), and pretty much all that's left is it's a software bug. A voice assistant with an iffy audio output, is pretty useless.2) The companion App for Android is HORRIBLE. I have yet to open and not loathe the experience. Why do you always take me to Conversations when I open the app. I don't use that feature and don't want it. I have a cell phone for calling people. Why is the app so damn slow and laggy. Why can't you just open the app backup to where I left off? Why do I need to cross off and delete everything from the shopping list? Seriously Amazon, hire yourself an undergraduate UI Intern and you'll get a better product than your current app.

title: It's an expensive toy that you get tired of quickly
body: It pretty much does not do anything worthwhile. One-third of the questions I asked I get silence. The other third I get "Don't know that one," and the other third are just answers to silly question, like "What the weather" , "What time is it," and other things you normally find listening to your radio or on you watch or phone.Echo will not tune into a local radio station, like "WABC." You have to subscribe to one of the companies list to listen to a radio station, and theyrequires either monthly payments or you giving up some of your privacy by signing up with them and agreeing to their terms, which brings me to playing music, none of which I am willing to do.If you don't have Amazon Prime, which I don't, you can't play music, and you can't pair your Bluetooth mp3 player or computer to play you private collection.If you imported you music into their system, Amazon allows only 250 sound, after that, there is a fee, also, and I quote, "Some record companies require Amazon to insert identifiers in the metadata that accompanies music when you download it from the Digital Music Store or My Music."Phone calls; you can call anyone who owns an Echo, but that's the extend of that, but my phone can call everyone I know, and I can see them as I am talking. I tried to pair Echo with my phone, did not work you need to download an App for that, and I hate downloading appz because they contain spywares and ads.Turning on and off lights, you have to buy expense extras for that, and for each room.I asked how to spell words and get wrong answers; math, the same. I'm guessing a lot has to do with voice recognition not working to well.Everything about Echo is gear to selling or paying for some kind of service, mostly Amazon Prime and buying Amazon products , I did not know that then, I do now.As it stands, right now, it's an expensive paperweight, because that's the only real use I found for it.

title: Speaker stopped working within a year. UPDATE: have to connect to Bose Wave or a Bluetooth speaker
body: We actually have two of these 2nd Gen Echo Dot...….....one I bought us for Christmas 2017 and another one we received as a gift last year (2018). The unit I purchased for Christmas 2017 works fine, but the other unit we received as a gift now has no sound coming out of the speaker. Sound can only be heard if a headset is plugged in, which I did just to troubleshoot if any sound at all was coming out of the Echo (I DON'T normally use a headset with the Echo Dot).This unit was purchased brand new from Amazon by my husband's sister and is used in a residence with adults only...it has never been mishandled. It just sits on the counter.Very disappointing, to say the least.UPDATE:I upgraded my "1-star" review to "2-stars" because I'm at least able to connect the Echo Dot to an external Bluetooth speaker (It can also be hardwired to an external speaker with a 3.5mm audio cable). Both devices are plugged into power separately.After I submitted my initial review yesterday, I remembered I had a brand new, unopened Boxanne Origaudio Bluetooth speaker that I received as a gift from my workplace about two years ago, so I decided to try it with this Echo Dot. It paired immediately and the sound coming out of the Boxanne is quite impressive, I'm actually very surprised at the bold sound coming out of such a small speaker!Note:I initially tested a different speaker with the Echo using the 3.5mm audio cable that did work, that connection didn't work with the Boxanne, even though that is one of the connection options. No worries, since the Bluetooth works fine and I didn't need to spend any additonal $$ on a new Echo Dot. Link to the Origaudio Boxanne: https://smile.amazon.com/dp/B07GJKRK57/ref=nav_timeline_asin?_encoding=UTF8&psc=1The Echo Dot will also connect to our Bose Wave system. To the main Bose unit using a 3.5mm to 3.5mm cord if the 3-CD changer isn't attached. If it is attached, I have to use a 3.5mm to RCA white/red audio cord.

title: Alexa Logic needs a lot of work to catch up with Siri
body: If I had a nickel for every time Alexa said "Sorry, I don't know the answer to that", I could have bought 2-3 more of this stylish looking machine that can only tell me the weather and traffic. "What's the weather" and "how's my commute" are the only commands I can get to work on a consistent basis. Voice home control is a joke. To be fair, Apple doesn't even attempt voice home control Siri...but Amazon's rush to market with Alexa is sorely lack. The crux of the problem is the I need to learn Alexa's syntax to give in commands, rather than Alexa understanding how people speak. For example, I have an August Smart lock, which Alexa claims to support. I should be able to ask "Alexa, is my front door locked?", and have it understand that I've enabled the August skill and paired it to my lock called "front door". Instead I have to ask "Alexa, ask August to check the status of my lock named Front Door"...or something like that (I've never actually gotten it to work). Even basic stuff, where I just want Alexa to answer a simple query ("how many teaspoons in a tablespoon" or "When is the Braves game?", the failure of Alexa rate to understand my question is probably 70-80%, and in side by side comparisons against Siri on my phone, Siri generally always understands the question (although may not find the answer I want...it's far from perfect). And the always listening mode is SCARY. Not from a conspiracy theory standpoint - at least 3 times Alexa has said "Ok, I'll place an order for..." randomly, while I just had the tv on and was not speaking. And, no, someone on TV didn't say "Alexa", but Alexa hears "essence" or "legs" or "September" and that triggered the blue light somehow. Look, I'm a big fan of Amazon - love my kindle, love prime, and hit 75-80 orders per year - but Alexa is too half baked to justify an echo/dot. Personally, I'm surprised Amazon would go to market with this many flaws.

title: Not so original.  Quirky.  Nice for placing calls.  Terrible security oversights abound.  Too many blatantly awkward commercials
body: Product makes bizarre references at times where the things it talks about in answers can't be the subject of further questions. She drops all knowledge of knowing about some of the things she responds with, which is just entirely unfriendly in conversational terms. Too much time teaching her to sing odd songs has been spent and not enough time spent on improving the flow of conversations to be more natural in progression and terms of backing up subjects brought up with knowledge of the subject mentioned every time so clarifying questions don't result in frustration. Appreciate the weird random answers she gives for some topics I stumbled upon like "you're fired", "what is the meaning of life," and "can you whisper?" but many 'cute' answers are direct or nearly direct copies of things Siri currently says or used to say which I guess might be flattery were it not annoyingly uncreative to plagiarize your competitor's product in what should be a field of innovation. Alexas "abilities" often seem more like "disabilities" because configuring so-called "SMART" bulbs or plugs always requires too many steps and too many hassles of always having to run for your smart phone, do an "setup" treatment to the product that is difficult like turning on and off the switch to it not rapidly 5 times within 10 seconds or more but not less than 5 and then having to set your phone to the products own Wi-Fi (IF you were even able to put it into that mode that is) and then download and run a special vendor-specific "smart"-device configuration program which always requires you to create an online account somewhere, set a password, configure it to your email, blah blah blah and then log into that through the custom (read: JUNKWARE) apps that are very temperamental in reliability to get the thing detected and delivered effectively setup to the Alexa app. That can only happen once you install the "ability" for Alexa to talk to that device and if everything is not setup perfectly on the smart-device specific app, for sure Alexa will never be able to locate the smart device. This is all wayyyyyy too complicated for the average person to want to even deal with it. It's also too easy for these devices to become mysteriously un-programmed. Alexxa's answers to random questions just aren't as creative nor are they usually original when compared to Apple's Siri AI interface. Also, it's not as good at interpreting what was said with minor amounts of competing sound in the room it seems. My iPhone does a better job on voice comprehension by leaps. Still, there are things to appreciate that are cool like the color ring on the box which points directly at the person talking to Alexa no matter which side of 360º they may stand in relationship to the position of the little hockey puck.There are some bad security oversights such as the device giving away the phone numbers of anyone in your contacts list to anyone who isn't even you (and it knows if its your voice or someone else if you set it up correctly so why the big open door?). simply by asking Alexa to call (insert random first name of person you want to obtaint contact info on with or without the permission or consultation of the owner of the Alexa device or the one who set it up with their email account)... . Appallingly poor attention to security considerations on that when anyone in the room can say "call Matt" and she will read off the last four digits of every Matt's phone number in your contacts list. It's not hard to come up with the other three when they are people in your local calling area, trust me). Alexa should be asking for a pin code or something before just giving up pieces of someone's contacts like that. Shame on you Amazon for being so lax like that.Also not appreciated is how many answers to questions are irritatingly clearly guided towards trying to sell you movies on any subject when you thought initially that you were just going to have an enlightening or pleasant non-commercially motivated conversation perhaps. Makes the experience cheaper. Needs to have more well defined control of lighting parameters for smart bulbs such as "strobe", "set to __ % brightness" being different from "make ___ % brighter (or dimmer)". Also, should pay attention to singing still playing at a low level in the background from Alexa even after telling her to "shut up" or "stop singing".... for a random length of time it can still be heard barely perceptively in the background behind Alexa who continues to babble too long sometimes. Alexa comes off as fun or white at times and creepy at others (like the whisper thing). Also, smart control of lighting is just awkwardly unintelligent for something that should be "smart". It should automatically try to ascertain which smart devices might be in same room as you by network triangulation from the Echo Dot and the Wi-Fi base station signal levels. No attempt at anything intelligent like that is made, and frequently Alexa mixes up lighting grounds or light names like "Kitchen Light" somehow being able to turn off the completely unrelated light named "Bedroom Light", scaring me or annoying me at all the wrong times when someone else is trying to control something in the kitchen two rooms away.Much more polish, automation, and less commercial pushiness is going to be needed for me to raise the star rating on this one. Sorry Alexa, but even though I worked for Amazon, and even though there aren't enough competing products in this market space, this one is still not totally ready for prime-time when it comes to home automation and ease of use. I shouldn't have to always be running back to the Alexa app on my smarter phone in order to set things up on the not as smart Alexa because Amazon's technical staff is too lazy to make everything work by voice command on the actual device. Why can't I "undo last command", "erase the last shopping order", "cancel that last order", or "setup user voice profile" from voice commands? There really isn't a good answer for this other than they just haven't invested the time to make these seemingly simple tasks available to be handled away from the smart-phone app.

title: Alexa is not all that smart... A Comparison of Amazon and Google vocabulary
body: I like my Echo Dot. But I like my Google Home better and it all comes down to vocabulary.My primary uses of the Echo are:1. playback streaming audio (via a HyperGear speaker)2. smart home control3. Play the occasional game (e.g. Jeopardy)4. Set reminders or timersEcho is pretty good at recognizing my commands, except that its vocabulary is not as natural as that of Google:ECHO vs GOOGLE HOME MINI : Smart Home VocabularyMy voice queries are in single quotes, the answer from the device are in double quotes. To be fair, I actually used the Google assistant on my phone rather than the Mini, because the mini is in another room at the moment, however the vocabulary for the device and phone app is the same. 'OK, Google, which of my lights are on?' and it told me"The Lamp is on, the kitchen, living room, office and the Christmas Lights Outdoors are off"Alexa just has no clue how to answer that or any similar questions here are several attempts:'Alexa, which of my lights are on?' (badump - failure tone),'Alexa, what lights are turned on?' -> "A few things share that name which one did you want?"; 'Office' -> "Office doesn't support that" or 'All of them' -> "Sorry I didn't find All";'Alexa: is my office light on?' -> Office doesn't support that". 'Alexa: Is lamp on now?' -> OK (turns lamp on)NOTE: The Kitchen, Living Room and Office lights are LIFX lights, the Lamp is a Eufy bulb, and the Christmas Lights Outdoor is a Martin Jerry smart plug.REMINDER VocabularyMost other advertised uses for Alexa I find cumbersome. For example to use the reminder feature of Alexa/Echo, if you set more than one reminder 'Alexa, cancel my reminders' responds with "You have two active reminders, visit the Alexa App to cancel one of them.". You CAN do something like this:'Alexa, what are my reminders?` (it answers with the lists and times), then 'Alexa, cancel my 12:45 reminder'. Canceling the reminder that way confirms it has canceled the reminder, but it is still listed in the Alexa App as a card with no indication that it was canceled.The problem with the Alexa App, is that almost everything you say shows up as a card. I may have told or asked Alexa to play songs a dozen times, and I have to scroll through a lot of cards... or fumble through the "Home -> Hamburger icon -> Reminders and Alarms -> Reminders" after the app finishes loading all the recent activity. And then your option is to "Mark completed" not "Delete"Google isn't much better here. In fact, it's worse. It also runs you through the app to accomplish the task.GAMESAlexa/Echo wins here. Much more to play interactively, though to be honest most of it gets old quickly.SELECTING and PLAYING Music'Alexa, play Tran Siberian Orchestra Christmas Music' sort of worked because I usually listen to the I Heart Radio and it created the channel. But it wasn't Christmas Music that it selected. 'Alexa, play prime music Tran Siberian Orchestra Christmas Music' "... isn't included in Amazon Prime..."GENERIC QuestionsI was astonished the first time I asked Google 'What times does southwest flight 1385 arrive in San Francisco' and it rattled off the full details including when it left, that it was ahead of time and the gate and time of arrival. If you ask the question properly, Alexa does quite a decent job as well, including providing an important bit of information that Google does not: which TERMINAL the flight arrives at (Google names the gate). The main difference between the two platforms, however, is you can ask Google the same question in your car as you can at home while the Alexa App doesn't seem to have any kind of voice interface. To be fair, I'm using and Android phone which Google has very strong ties to.Try this question:"Alexa, what times does southwest flight 1385 arrive" or "Alexa, what is the status of southwest flight 1385"You can even mix things around a little bit but not this much: "Alexa, what is the status of flight 1385 southwest". And this is a bad fail: "Alexa, what is the status of flight 1385 of southwest airlines" 'I don't have any information for Southwest Airlines flight 1385'Asking about a flight that has multiple segments (e.g. SWA 1147 from Houston -> Denver -> LAS -> SFO) may tell you the flight has already landed even when you give it a strong hint that you only want know about one segment:|"Alexa, what time does southwest flight 1147 arrive in San Francisco?" '... Landed in Houston'"OK, Google, what time does southwest flight 1147 arrive in San Francisco?" '... from Los Angeles departs in 6 hours, 12 minutes.' That is, it's only telling me about the last segment, but doesn't tell me the SCHEDULED arrival time.PRIME MEMBERSHIPThere are definitely some advantages - you get more questions in Jeopardy, a healthy selection of music (though not what I tried above), and of course movies.CONCLUSIONS:Google wins on using natural vocabulary - in most cases. Amazon has more features at the moment... so it's a toss up. Although I notice you don't seem to be able to order Google Home Mini on Amazon...

title: Why these things don't talk with each other is beyond me.
body: I'm somewhat at a loss how to rate this device as there is plenty to love and plenty to hate about it. At $50, I think very few people will be disappointed in what the Dot can do for them. I find Alexa's voice recognition ability to be remarkably accurate, and am routinely delighted with what I'm able to ask her for. The Echo is still in its infancy, however, with lots of room for improvement. The two I'm about to list each cost it a star:1. Each Echo device is its own island. In other words, if I tell the Dot in my kitchen to set a 5 minute timer, and then go fold some laundry in the master bedroom, when the timer goes off, only the Echo device used to set the timer (the Dot in the kitchen) knows about it. Really? C'mon Amazon. Surely you must have realized people would get multiples of these (you sell them in a 6-pack) for their home, and might want them to talk with each other.2. There is no way to pair this with a phone to be able to make hands-free calls. The hardware is certainly capable, but Amazon has elected not to provide the software support. What's so frustrating about this one is the ability to make a hands-free call would open up a lot of possibilities for people who are living on their own and who might find themselves in a situation where they need to call for help but can't reach a phone. Even the briefest of internet searches will yield myriad requests for how to get Alexa to act as a safety net for grandma. At the very least, providing an easy way to have Alexa send some canned SMS messages to pre-determined phone numbers would be a huge feature.Honestly, at the $179 for the Echo, I would have told people to pass. At $50 however, it's almost worth it just to have a hands-free, infinite number of timers in the kitchen. All the rest it can do is gravy.EDIT, 21-DEC-2016.Wow. If I had known in advance how much commentary my review would drive, I would have spent a bit more time on it.Yes, I'm sympathetic to those who take issue with me dinging a product not for doing what it claims to do poorly, but rather for not doing things I think it ought to do. But, here's the thing. What is an Echo Dot? Amazon doesn't really say. They go on and on about all the things you can use your Dot for, but since this class of device (hands-free digital assistant? Voice-command Bing interface? Screen-free Amazon shopping tool?) doesn't really have a standard yet, I argue that pointing out where it lacks features one might reasonably expect it to have (let me repeat that so as to dissuade the inevitable hyperbolic responses... "features one might REASONABLY expect it to have") is fair game. The bottom line is Amazon goes on and on about how great these things are, and how much you can do with them, and Skills!, and more features getting added every day! and... and yet I believe most will find, as I have, that it's just not that great.I own 2 Dots. One lives in my kitchen. After the novelty wore off, it now gets used hardly at all. "Alexa, play NPR radio.", "Alexa, set a 5 minute timer." That's it. And you'll notice that it doesn't get an "Alexa, set a 2 hour timer" because unless I happen to be in the kitchen when it goes off, I won't hear it.My other Dot has moved around the house, and after getting zero use in other rooms, is currently in my bedroom. "Alexa, play NPR radio." is about all it does. Everything else is nicely handled by the smartphone sitting on my nightstand.If I'm being completely honest with myself, Alexa is closer to a 2-star novelty for me now as opposed to my initial 3-stars, but that's not fair. It's not that I "don't like" the Dot. I just think it's "okay".The simple fact of the matter is for every thing I've kinda liked about this product, there's been an implementation detail that then annoys me. "Alexa, read To Kill A Mockingbird." Then you stop her and go to a different room. "Alexa, continue reading." Nope. "Alexa, wake me up at 6am." Okay, and good thing you remembered to say this to your bedroom Dot and not the one in the kitchen. "Alexa, remind me to pick up Jessica at 3pm." Okay, I'll make a note in this web-accessed tool and you can manually look up, or maybe someday I'll give you an audible alert assuming someone writes a skill for that. What?! Alexa, use your "Audio/Video Remote Control Profile (AVRCP) for voice control of connected mobile devices" to connect to some devices via bluetooth, but don't connect to the one "mobile device" that freak'n everyone has so as to make this feature useful.I think the Dot is "okay", which in Amazon review parlance is 3-stars. The one in the kitchen makes for a great tea timer. The one in the bedroom has become a little mono radio it turns out I kinda like having there. I think, at $40 each, anyone buying one will have a 50/50 chance of thinking their money has been well-spent. I also think several years and a few product generations from now, the Dot will be a 5-star item. Just not today.

title: Pick one Echo product instead of purchasing all 3
body: I've been an avid Amazon customer since they started. I have 2 kindles and was relatively satisfied. I read all the rave reviews for the Echo, Echo Dot and Tap. They seemed legitimate; however, I guess those reviewers either had low expectations or were incentivized to express those favorable opinions. After going on an Echo Dot, Tap and Echo purchasing frenzy, I now have a total of 3 Dots,1 Tap and 1 Echo and am suffering buyers remorse.If you need or want to know the local weather or get flash news updates on command, the Echo or Echo Dot are your devices. I, on the other hand, use them for music streaming. I find that the "sleep function" has flaws, voice recognition/comprehension needs great improvement and everyone should know that it is really unnecessary to buy more of these devices unless you have multiple amazon music accounts such that you can stream on those separate accounts from different locations/rooms. Otherwise, users should know that the devices cannot be linked together for simultaneous streaming. They also cannot be used by different parties to stream different music from each different device. Apparently, Amazon Prime Music only allows streaming to one device at a time.The Tap cannot be used for streaming unless a wireless connection is available. I wouldn't recommend connecting via an unsecured connection so unless you have a secure, mobile wireless connection, the Echo Tap is nothing more than an expensive, portable bluetooth speaker which you can purchase for much less than the cost of an Echo Tap.One might notice that the Echo, Echo Dot and Tap are often paired with supplemental bluetooth speakers with 2-3 item purchase combination discounts. This is a very misleading promotion as it leads one to believe they can be used for simultaneous streaming. That is not the case at all. Unfortunately, the E-Dot only has a one-way BT connection. Either you play music from the E-Dot or the BT speaker; but, you cannot play music from both.There are a lot of Applications for Alexa, the voice operated system used to operate the E-Dot and Echo. Unfortunately, a lot of them have complicated wake phrases/statements. If you don't phrase the statement exactly right, the app won't work and Alexa will not understand the command. You can honestly spend more time trying to phrase things correctly to trigger Alexa then it might have taken for you to manually execute what you wanted to do like creating a shopping list, or recording water consumption.Another shortcoming with Alexa is the slow re-fresh rate of the application. Sometimes, you have to completely exit out of it, stop it from running in the background on your phone and then reload it in order to get it to reflect your most recent commands or current "now playing". In addition, there are other limitations specific to music streaming. Let's say you want to stream a specific artist all day long to soothe a pet with separation anxiety. If 1) you set the sleep timer for longer than you'll be away (ex. for 12 hours) and 2) Alexa confirms she will stop playing in 12 hours, that isn't exactly true. Alexa will actually stop playing when she reaches the end of the artist's play queue which may only be 4 hours of music. Theoretically, you can manually or verbally set Alexa to repeat the queue; however, I haven't tested this successfully yet. It may only repeat the queue once rather than indefinitely until stopped by the sleep timer.My final Alexa app gripe relates to the repeat function. Unlike the iTunes or Amazon Music apps, Alexa cannot repeat one song in a queue. You'd think that toggling the repeat button 2 times would trigger this function; however, all it seems to do is toggle the repeat option to the "off" position.All in all, individually each product has its benefits so my rating is a low 3. There is just no reason, other than needing a device in a location outside of the primary product's voice range, to purchase more than one device. My thought is that the Echo-Dot is the most versatile of all 3 products; however, the Echo has better sound quality. I have noticed the improved sound quality on the Echo; but, the improvement really doesn't make up for the price difference. If I could do it all again, I'd invest in the Echo Dot for home and an inexpensive Bluetooth speaker for work that I can connect to the iTunes or Pandora applications on my phone. That way, I will still be able to stream Amazon Prime music through the Dot at home for the stressed pet while playing comforting music in my office.

title: Promising, but unreliable
body: Promising, but not ready for primetime. I bought 2 of these things on sale. Both act the same way, so it's not as if I received a bad sample. The first problem was that although they would recognize what I said, Alexa's response would repeatedly cut in and out in mid-sentence, even though both units had strong Wi-Fi signals. I finally got past this issue by connecting them to my 5 Ghz Wi-Fi network. They are just plain unreliable on 2.4 Ghz Wi-Fi networks (I tried them on two different 2.4 Ghz networks - same unreliable performance). I note that other people have reported this same problem on multiple internet sites, so again, it's not as if mine had a unique problem.The second problem is that Alexa will completely misunderstand some obvious requests and come back with a totally unrelated response. For example, when I asked about the local sales tax rate (in two different ways), Alexa responded by reading a Wikipedia article about Puerto Rico. Seriously? "Sales Tax" is interpreted as "Puerto Rico"? If that's an example of the current state of Artificial Intelligence, I'm not worried about losing my job to a machine anytime soon.The third problem is one that was recently in the news, when Alexa recorded a family's private conversation and sent it to one of their contacts. The problem is that Alexa will sometimes wake up and start responding without the keyword (in this case, "Alexa") being spoken. One time I was talking on the phone, and Alexa responded to a phrase in the conversation ("do the holidays"). Another time it happened in response to a phrase on a TV program ("give them the benefit"). Neither of these incidents included the word "Alexa" or anything close to that, at least not by human standards. To try to avoid future incidents like this, I've changed the wake word to "Echo" to see if that helps. The big problem here is that you're limited to using one of only four pre-defined wake words, and three of them (echo, computer, and Amazon) are very common in normal conversations, making Alexa very likely to trigger on them when not intended. I originally selected "Alexa" as the wake word because I thought it would be less likely to cause unintended responses than the other three, but that's obviously not the case. Amazon would be wise to allow you to create your own wake word so that you could pick something which would virtually never occur in normal conversation (either in real life or on TV).In summary, if the above problems ever get resolved, this would be a great product. Until then, though, it's a hit-or-miss proposition. I'm keeping mine for now because they allow me to remotely adjust my thermostat without running up and down the stairs every time, but if it weren't for that, I would probably return them and wait for something more reliable.Update 7/12/18:Now that I've had these things for a while, I've discovered a bit more. When using the 5 Ghz Wi-Fi band, they would occasionally lose contact and go offline. After some research, I found that these units can only use a portion of the 5 Ghz band: namely, channels 36-48. Occasionally my router would switch channels to avoid other 5 Ghz networks, and my Echo Dots lost contact when the router chose a channel outside that range. I finally solved the problem by setting my router to stay on a single channel that the Dots could use. Since then, the connection has been solid and I've had no more connection issues.I continue to see a problem with Alexa responding to background sounds (TV programs, radio programs, and audiobooks), regardless of which wake word I select. On top of this, sometimes Alexa doesn't recognize the wake word when I use it. So the reliability in this area definitely needs some improvement.I've also found that Alexa gets confused by some very simple questions and produces unrelated answers, which can be very frustrating. Also, some requests need to be worded in a very specific way in order for Alexa to understand them. Minor wording variations which mean exactly the same thing to humans aren't understood by Alexa.I'm leaning more toward 3.5 stars at this point (as opposed to my original 3 stars), since the connectivity issues have been resolved and Alexa is good when it works correctly (which is most of the time). However, the times when it doesn't work can be very annoying. If those ever get fixed, I could easily give it 5 stars.

title: Amazing potential, but not worthy of hype it receives
body: There is certainly a lot of hype around the Echo Dot, and it currently does receive higher ratings than competitors who haven't had as much of a head start (although I somehow feel that Google may eventually win the race), but so far I've found that it just sits there unused about 99% of the time (I'm sorry, they both do, since I bought two units for better coverage). Don't get me wrong, the built-in capabilities are very cool (i.e. asking it to play music, provide traffic info for you commute, read or play weather reports and news briefings, look up basic information like the phone number for a local restaurant, set your thermostat and control lights), and the voice recognition out of the box is very good.To be fair, I'm still learning, but that's part of the problem - there is surprisingly little in the way of guidance regarding what you can ask or how (and although it is somewhat flexible, there are certainly ways to ask things that make sense to you but will confuse Alexa), so you find yourself guessing exactly how to phrase things to get the desired response. Also, if the unit itself is acting as speaker for music (not great) or news reports, you do have to speak a little louder than normal to get it to hear your request to stop playing or do something else.It's also a mixed bag with respect to built-in capabilities, as they are clearly banking on 3rd party developers to create useful functionality. For example, if you use Google G-mail, the system can easily connect to your calendar and tell you about your upcoming schedule and allow you to schedule calendar events with ease. However, if you want to create a "to-do list" or "shopping list" list, those lists are only maintained within the Alexa app/system unless you use a 3rd party solution; out of the box, there are only 2 supported so far. I've downloaded a demo of Todoist, which seems fine at first since they have apps for most all devices, but it appears that if you want to get time-based reminders you have to purchase the pro version, which has a yearly subscription fee of about $30 - not something I want to pay for just to keep my grocery list on yet another server with yet another service under yet another account. Basically what I want from Alexa is Siri-like capabilities that include the ability to set reminders triggered by time/date and/or location, I want to continue using Siri on my iPhone to do this, and I want the lists and items created by either of those applications to synchronize. There are various ways of doing this, but so far none of them have worked very well.As for the thousands of "skills" that you can add, all I have to say is that the vast majority of them are either completely useless, seem to duplicate functionality, or provide little more than a novelty. You literally have to search through this entire list and click on each one (they all have silly names that often don't provide a clue as to the functionality) to see what it does, and even then the description leaves you going "what?". Also, each skill seems to be very incremental - it will do just one simple thing - and you have to ask for it by name (again, recall the silly names), and you can't simply ask Alexa to list the currently-installed skills. So if you are considering buying an Echo or Echo Dot to provide convenience or save time, don't be fooled, because it seems, like so many gadgets we have these days, that you will waste a lot of time searching for, creating, and maintaining a useful list of half-baked "skills".Ok the unit is only $50 which is an amazing price and worth that alone if you just want to try a cool new gadget. My main concern is that this is the 2nd generation unit, and it lacks hardware capabilities that many reviewers have mentioned. I have 2 units, but could use 3 or 4 to cover my entire house, but my guess is that we will be seeing the 3rd generation before long, and just like cell phones, the older units will be ignored and end up in the trash. While you can place Amazon orders using the Echo Dot, I wonder if "Alexa" will become self-aware and prevent you from ordering a 3rd generation unit when the time comes? ;)

title: 5 different echo devices increasingly have problems, alexa app lost link with Kasa plugs
body: Five Amazon devices increasingly have problemsI am a 77 year old senior citizen with some disabilities that make it important that I be able to control plugs by voice with my Amazon units.Three echo devices and two dots have become more and more unreliable. I believe the basic problem is that Amazon has sold so many units that its computers can no longer properly interface with the devices. One of the echo devices is a basic echo, two of the echoes are echo plus units purchased in December 2017, the two echo dots were purchased November 2017, and the basic Amazon echo was purchased July 2017. I have the Alexa app on two android phones. One of the phones is several months old, with the app having been updated relatively recently. The other phone is two days old, and the app was installed on it within the last 48 hours.Examples of problems1. Devices are no longer compatible with TP-link Kasa plugs. Yesterday morning the plugs were working fine. Then as the day went on the Alexa devices became unable to control first one of the plugs, then two of the plugs, etc. until it could control none of them.Here is an example of what happens when I try to turn on a light in my spare bedroom with a voice command.
