All five of the Alexa devices give the following message:“Sorry something is wrong. To control the spare bedroom light, try disabling the skill and then re-enabling it from your Alexa app.” Disabling the skill and re-enabling it does not fix the problem. I have the Alexa app on two different android phones.On both android phones the Alexa app for the nonworking plugs shows a message “Skill is unresponsive.” If I attempt to manually turn a plug on or off in the Alexa app, the plug does not go on or off. All that happens on either phone is it I get a message stating that the skill is unresponsive. However, if I go into the Kasa app, I can manually turn the plugs on and off–showing that they are connected to the Wi-Fi network. I tried deleting this skill for one of the plugs and then attempted to re-create the skill. The Amazon app was unable to find the Kasa plug.I spent a long time today on the phone with an Amazon representative who was unable to fix the problem and is escalating it. He said that someone will call me sometime.There is no doubt in my mind that the problem lies with Amazon. The Alexa app on two different phones states that skills for the Kasa plugs are unresponsive. I spoke with the technician at Kasa who said the problem is with Amazon.2. Several months ago all of my echo devices began to occasionally respond to a voice command with a statement such as “I’m having trouble understanding right now, please try again later.” If I repeatedly gave the command, eventually the echo device would understand. I sort of felt like I was trying to make a phone call to a very busy line in which the only way to get through was to repeatedly retry.3. Accuracy of response by all units has declined. Despite my having trained the units to recognize my voice, they increasingly do not properly understand. Sometimes they reply that “I don’t know that one.” Sometimes they state, “I’m not sure” sometimes they carry out an action that I did not ask for–in other words they respond to a command with an incorrect action.The basic fact is that five different devices purchased at different times are becoming increasingly unreliable and unusable. Another basic fact is that I was unable to get through to a higher level technician and was told that someone would call me sometime. Amazon should stop selling these devices and work on fixing their computers.I tried a chat with Amazon and sent this entire review along with a strong request to speak to a supervisor. I was connected finally with a person who helped me restore all of the skills for the tp-link Kasa plugs. There is still the less pressing problem of all of the devices having increasing problems with recognizing commands correctly. The various echo devices are a great idea, but there are still problems I think with the Amazon computers and their interface with such devices.

title: Disappointing but wondering what 3rd Gen will bring.
body: I'm not used to writing reviews so I'm not sure how this will read to other people. My apologies if it is boring or uninformative.I have had my Dot for a few months now and I have to say I am a little disappointed with this.The voice recognition is not all it's cracked up to be. I have mine performing simple tasks like turning the room lamp on and off and playing Pandora. More often than not, when asking it to turning off (or on) the light, it will tell me there is no active pause. Huh? I know there isn't. I have just turned the tv off. Or haven't turned it on yet.Getting it to play a show is puzzling. It always wants to start at episode 1, season 1. Why? Maybe if I started watching a show from season 1 episode 1, and used the Dot to watch the show every time I watched the show (as opposed to using the buttons on the remote) it would keep track and take me to the next episode. However, I got mine partway through watching a show and it just always wants to take me to the very first episode. I don't know why there is no way to get it to play the episode I want it to play. If there is a way, I obviously haven't figured it out yet.Getting it to pretend to be a superhero (the little stories are cute but there only seems to be two different ones. Why??), generates the response "I'm not sure how to help you with that." Why aren't you? It is supposed to be one of your skills! I have to ask it four, five or six times before it will do it.Last night, trying to get it to tell me the Manchester United result in the Champions League, I asked so many times I about gave up. I don't know if it is the way I was asking, maybe I wasn't using the correct verbal request, I don't know, but I shouldn't have to ask 10 or more times trying out various formats of request before it finally gives me what I want. One time I asked, it gave me results for all Champions League results for yesterday. Who cares? I was asking for one particular result. I finally stumbled upon the correct way to ask but holy moly, it was hard work! My Google Home Mini got it right first time. Why can't this thing?Playing Pandora it understands. Fortunately. Unfortunately, however, the sound from the built-in speaker is pretty rubbish, so I connected some small computer speakers that I don't really use, to it. It improved the sound well enough, but if I play the music too loud, the Dot loses the ability to pick up my voice - despite the claims of far field voice recognition.Requests for the 3rd Gen Echo Dot: make it easier to communicate with it. Verbal structure is pretty strict at the moment. Make it more relaxed and everyday-like. Make the speaker better (my other voice controlled device sounds so much better than this). Make it understand English accents. It can speak with one, so why can it not understand one? (My other voice controlled device isn't perfect either, but it understands me so much better and I've had that only a week.)There also needs to be some way to have delivery notifications show ONLY on the Dot(s) and app associated with the account that did he ordering. My wife and I have a shared (or household or whatever it's called) Prime account. My Dot is linked to my Amazon account using my email address, as is the Alexa app on my phone. My wife's Dot is linked to her Amazon account using her email address, as is the Alexa app on her phone. If I order something for her, the notifications show up on BOTH Dots and BOTH apps, despite the different email addresses used to log into these things. Why?? Who thought to do that? They might be on the same network but they are linked to DIFFERENT ACCOUNTS! Goodnight! How stupid is that?I don't think I'll be paying out for another one any time soon but if they sort these problems out and reviews from whatever they bring out for the 3rd Gen are good enough, maybe I'll take a chance on one. In the meantime, I wish I could return this one but I have had it several months now so it's probably way past the return date.There's definitely room for improvement. We'll have to see what happens.

title: Surprisingly little for $50
body: For something hyped so much, this little device does surprisingly little. If you don't want bad jokes or incomplete shopping tips, it does less than you'd imagine such a device would be able to do. As a kind of administrative assistant, which is why I got it, it's practically useless.For example: I got it primarily because I wanted it to read out my daily calendar in the morning so I'd be sure not to forget anything. I'd read that it could sync with Google calendar, which I was using. But getting it to recognize your calendar is a nightmare since Google calendar requires combining several calendars if you want color coding and Alexa only sees one of them, not the master one where everything is combined. So Alexa would repeatedly answer "There is nothing in your calendar" when several things were sitting right there in my master calendar. Long conversations with the Help line were fruitless. Revamping my calendar to suit its pea brain wasn't a job I wanted to spend time doing, so there went the main reason I got it.Even the simplest things proved impossible, as though the developers have no familiarity with modern American life. For example, in the morning I like to spend a little time listening to the news before getting up. You can say "Alexa, set alarm for X p.m." and it will set the alarm. But you can't say, "Alexa, make my alarm radio station WXXX." I have to wake up to its standard alarm and then start my phone radio manually in the usual way. So Alexa can't even do what a clock radio can do! How obvious can you get?Another strange gap: You can ask it "Alexa, what is in my cart?" and it will tell you what's in your Amazon cart. But if you want to buy what's in the cart, there's no command for this. You have to go on line and hit the purchase button as usual. Why leave out such an obvious command as "Alexa, buy what's in my cart"?Update: Alexa can "find my phone" by making it ring, an invaluable service after you've turned the house upside down. But the set-up can erase itself, so when you need it you have to re-register. It asks you for your number, then - get this - sends a code number by text that you have to say - TO YOUR PHONE. Bloody idiocy!And it's perennially odd about what it understands. For example, I once decided to use it for all that knowledge the ads claim it has by asking "Alexa, when was the Jurassic era?" It said, "I'm sorry, I don't know that." Then I asked a question (can't remember the exact words) about the dinosaurs and got a whole little encyclopedia read-out on the Jurassic, with dates, etc. Googling is so much faster than figuring out what Alexa understands that I just don't bother.I think it might work better as entertainment for kids. (Reading articles about teaching kids to be nice to Alexa suggests that it's popular with kids.) Although the programmers did give it some bits of adult humor. The other day I saw its yellow light flashing and didn't know what it meant, so I risked the question, "Alexa, what is your status?" It answered, "I prefer to be single. But it's hard to find someone who can handle the cloud commute."So Alexa can set alarms, tell you the time when you're rushing around getting ready for work, and tell you the weather. That's convenient, 3 stars for that. But that's not much for $50. Plus it has a down side. It can spring to life whenever it hears something like "Alexa" and start talking ("What do you want to order?" or "I don't know the answer to that") in the middle of your conversation on the phone or whatever. And now we're getting word (see the New York Times) that Alexa can bungle what you say to the point of recording what you say and sending the recording to people without telling you. So it's also a bit creepy.

title: Novelty vs Necessity?
body: I purchased 1 Echo and 2 Echo Dot over the holidays with the interest this is a worthwhile investment, and to see if it provides a benefit to my lifestyle. Simple installation and up and running in no time. I have some experience with Tech stuff, Networks, Windows and Android product. This is my first venture into Echo. And I was excited to try it since I’m a Prime and Amazon Unlimited Music userOverall Internet searched asking questions on facts easily found the responses I get about a 50% hit on finding what I’ve asked for, 50% of the time “I Don’t Understand the question. Ask the same question on Android and I get better responses rates. Google/Android allows for follow on questions on the same subject matter. Amazon this need to be swiftly Improved to stay in the game, or at least stay in my house.Music Search, the Echo has a great sound quality for a small device. With Amazon, Unlimited it provides a wider song selection. My wife and I can spend a couple hours selecting sounds in an evening vs some mindless TV shows. It does seem to have weakness. Many times, it has trouble figuring out the song and artist match up, plays some knock off artist of the song version or plays the live version vs the original album version. It can be frustrating to get to the right song each time. And at times to critical on the Artist name…Example Echo can’t seem to figure out“Bob Seger” You must say “Bob Seger and the Silver Bullet Band” And that is a mouth full where as many times Alexis drops before you get the song name and artist out. There needs to be more format in the song request process, ask Song Name, Artist and Album if known. Alexis drops you and you must start over … More Intelligence is needed here.Shopping …I did not buy this device to shop on Amazon. Only Benefit is you can add items to your shopping card for later reviewHome Automation. I use Wemo switches and Bulbs. Creating Group names helps immensely. Over all works well …. But if you have two homes it tends to comingle the devices on one. There’s several weakness, there is no smarts to use the Dimmer on the light bulbs, you can’t ask the status of a switch, are you on or off. Nor is there an ability to tell Alexis to turn on a light for a specific amount of time.Nest Thermostat is a hit or miss. It fails most the time. Many times, Alexis reports cannot find the device or tells you it is in a mode it cannot be adjusted, which is not true. I never us away. You cannot have two homes with Nest named as Hallway. It is not smart enough to use the second label such. Nest or Amazon needs to have the option to ask status and report current temperature and set temperature. Otherwise how do you validate the number for degrees to change to turn on the AC or Heat. This function is of no value as it works today.Other Weaknesses. You cannot ask the Echo down stairs to set and Alarm on another Echo in the bedroom. You cannot set a timer and ask it to report on multiple devices, you cannot cancel a function or Alarm on another device. Example the I awoke this morning early and was down stairs when the upstairs echo Dot alarm set went off. I had to go upstart to cancel. There is no value in the Reminders and To-Do … It seems not to play them back you must go to the App.This is not a review of Google vs Amazon. Both have some issues. But I would have expected Amazon to be further along than this. This issues above lend to trying other vendors down the road. Odds I will have sent up my second home with Google since there is no clean way to configure two locations and limit to only local devices So my Subject of Novelty vs Necessity? I’m heavily on the novelty side now.

title: It's OK
body: UPDATE 9/29/2017 --I have since replaced my Echo Dots with Google Home devices and man am I glad I did. You won't get an idea of how pathetic the Amazon Echo series is until you try Google Home. Since the release of Echo line, there hasn't been one meaningful update whatsoever, as they promised. It's still the same bloodboiling, piece of crap it was when it came out. Instead of actually updating the device, they just released more pieces of crap for you to buy. From what I've seen so far, the NEW Alexa devices are almost no different in operation. Almost never answers your questions and still requires "skills" to be downloaded to it and a extremely specific voice activation code phrase to activate those skills. The Google home devices utilize hidden skills, meaning it uses skills very much the same way but you'll never notice. You can ask extremely complex questions and almost always get an answer. I could go on and on but the point is Amazon failed with me.As a long-time Amazon customer, I wholeheartedly recommend avoiding Alexa enabled devices and recommend the Google Home.Original -- Still needs A LOT of work but here's my opinion so far;PROS:• Great music player. I would argue it's the best feature, the Echo is excellent at pulling up music, sports, and radio stations.• News briefing. You can build your own custom news briefing from just about every news source, even stack news sources for multiple briefings.• Audio out port. The dot has an audio out port so you can hook up an external speaker or a set of bluetooth speakers through bluetooth.• Basic Q&A. Alexa is good a VERY, VERY basic Q&A. Alexa's data comes from Bing.com, so when you ask a question that Bing.com doesn't answer, and Bing hardly answers anything more complicated than 'Who was the 40th president?'. The biggest failure of this product, to me, was to put so much faith behind Bing.CONS:• The 'awesome' far field mics aren't that awesome. You have to speak like a robot for it to perfectly understand you. Otherwise, you could be getting the weather in London when you don't care.• Advanced Q&A terrible. Getting ready for heavy doses of 'Sorry. I don't know the answer." Like I said, Bing isn't Google and needs a major overhaul.• SKILLS, or lack thereof. This part of the Echo family I hate the most. If you've ever owned a Pebble smartwatch, you already have plenty of experience with this silly concept. Basically, you can download 'skills' which are apps that can be triggered by a VERY SPECIFIC phrase to utilize different companies' Echo app. For example, the commercial of people ordering a Pizza on Alexa he says 'tell Dominos' to trigger the Dominos app or skill. Most apps, or skills, that are available don't work well and require an often hard to remember or grammatically incorrect phrase to trigger the apps, which are either severly limited or weren't worth the download. Music is the only skill they got right.In summary, this has become a voice activated music library. For that purpose, this thing is awesome and totally worth the $50. However, if I were to grade the music feature with the rest of the Echo's so called features, I wouldn't give it more than 3 stars. It was a giant disappointment for me, as I expected at least Google level answers to my questions and get far from it. The skills are borderline worthless (unless you like meditating and facts about dogs, cats, and celebs). The voice recognition isn't bad but isn't great either.I'll leave it here for now. If updates drastically change the functionality, I'll leave an update.

title: Okay as a "personal assistant," but less so as a Smart Home controller
body: As a stand-alone device and channeler of Amazon's "intelligent personal assistant" Alexa, who lives somewhere in the cloud constantly listening to your every utterance, the Echo Dot can respond to voice commands to play music, make to-do lists, set alarms, stream podcasts, play audiobooks, and provide weather, traffic, and other real-time information. Whether these abilities alone are worth having a potential electronic eavesdropper in your house is debatable, but what tipped the balance for me was the prospect of connecting the Echo Dot and introducing Alexa to my existing Logitech Harmony Elite and Insteon home-automation (a.k.a. smart home) system. Wouldn't it be neat, I thought, to control my home theater equipment as well as all my lights and appliances without so much as lifting a finger to tap the remote, just by barking orders to Alexa? Well, in my case communicating with Alexa was like trying to make myself understood to a willing but slightly dimwitted individual "without much English" or perhaps a poorly trained pet.Setting up the Echo Dot was easy enough. It readily detected and got itself situated in my WiFi network, with only a little help needed from me via the Amazon Alexa app I'd previously installed on my iPhone. And I was able to teach it (i.e., to download and install) the particular "skill" it needed to communicate with my Logitech Harmony Elite hub (sold separately of course), which in turn had been previously configured to control my A/V equipment and Insteon home-automation devices. Ideally I should now have been able to turn on (or off) any of the "activities" programmed in the Harmony hub by invoking Alexa to, for example, "turn on (off) the TV," or "turn on (off) the living room lights." Unfortunately Alexa's comprehension of and ability to execute my commands has been decidedly hit and miss. Sometimes it (she?) responds with a cheery "Okay!" and does exactly what I told it to do. But sometimes it says "Okay" and does something else entirely -- like turning on the lights in the bedroom when I wanted to watch Netflix in the living room. And sometimes it fails to understand a command altogether, giving me some excuse equivalent to "command not found," even though it had no trouble with the same command an hour ago. So I frequently find myself in the ridiculous position of shouting at the thing for five minutes, with unwanted music blaring and lights flashing, trying to make mayself understood when I could have accomplished my objective in a second or two in the first place by simply tapping the Harmony remote. It's not clear to me whether the problems are with the Echo Dot, its resident spirit Alexa, or the mediating software (which I think is a Logitech product) between Echo/Alexa and the Harmony hub.Also, after the initial setup is complete, making changes to or troubleshooting the configuration of the whole system is quite confusing. You're dealing with the websites, apps, and (often conflicting) instructions from at least three different vendors -- Amazon, Logitech, and Insteon. The integration of the various products and software is far from seamless or intuitive -- it often seems like a miracle that anything works at all.So in conclusion, while the Echo Dot may be an amusing toy for grownups offering some utility as a "personal assistant," its effectiveness as the centerpiece of a home-automation system is quite limited in my opinion, despite the effusive claims of Amazon and the vendors of the various system components (compatible with Alexa!!). I expect the technology will improve in time, but it's not there yet.

title: Echo Needs to be smarter and Voice Calling/Text MessagingNot Ready for primetime
body: This review is unbiased as i own 4 native echo devices, and two third party devices with alexa built in, and i own a google home.The echo devices are great for turning on and off devices and they are compatible with more smart devices than google home at the moment. However, the skill feature i dont like compared to google because you have to remember specific commands while google home doesnt require a specific command. Google home is more natural when speaking to it. An example of this with echo would be, ask the stock app what is microsofts stock price, where as google doesnt need a skill for stock price and i could just say google what is the price of microsoft stock).Echo is not good with followup questions. For example, if i say echo who is tom cruise and then say echo what is his age, it will not recognize im referring to tom cruise from the first question. i have to state his name again for each question, whereas google i can say hey google who is tom cruise and on the second question i can say what is his age without repeating his name. Google home is smarter in this sense.With google home i can choose a tv show of netflix but i cant choose a specific episode. Echo does NOT even have the ability yet to even choose a tv show on netflix. To echos defense however, it can be used for voice commands on plex.Huge downfall to me: Why cant i control my firestick with my echo dot yet? this feature has been requested by many people for a year. Intead i have to use the voice remote and press a button to speak to alexa. That is just dumb when people want to use their echo dot to control by voice without having to press a button. DUHHHHVoice calling feature/text message. Amazon has this feature but google doesn't have it yet(yes it just came out today on 5/9/17 and i have already tested it for 3 hours)The Good: You can call anyone who has downloaded the amazon alexa app regardless of whether they own an echo deviceThe Bad: unfortunately If the person does not have the alexa app downloaded on their phone, you cannot call them with the echo dot and you have to just use your phone like normalYou cannot answer a call that is not calling thru the alexa app on your echo, you have to answer by phone like normalIf you own multiple echos that are registered to the same account or have a family/friend linked who has their own account but they are linked to the same prime account, and someone calls you through the alexa app, it will call all your echos (BAD BAD BAD). I was told by a rep that this is so you hear it ringing in all rooms, and that would be cool if you could mute an individual echo but you cant. IT rings all echos. This is a bad thing if this is at nighttime and you dont want someone in another room with an echo linked to your prime account disturbed. But what about the fact of why cant i call an individual echo without calling them all. Its because Amazon has NOT developed a profile system yet like google. This defeats the whole purpose of the call feature in my opinion.Also, why can i not choose a personalized wake word like "Danny or Jenna turn on the lights. instead you are stuck with four choices (alexa, echo, computer or amazon). When you have more than four echo devices like me, this becomes a real pain. why isnt their even wake word phrases like "living room echo, or guest room echo, instead of having to say alexaI want to root for amazon but they have a lot to fix. I really hope they read my review and fix some of the issues

title: Underwhelming at best.
body: I am one of the Prime members who bought the Echo when it was first released and I loved it. I only had the one and it was getting to be a hassle transferring Alexa from my kitchen windowsill to my daughter's bedroom to play her music at night, so I decided to get a Dot. The first gen was unavailable when I tried to buy one but soon after that the 2nd Gen was released, so that's the one this review is for.I'm incredibly underwhelmed. I didn't expect the same level of performance as I got with the Echo, but this little guy is next to useless.The most disappointing thing about him is the lack of voice recognition he provides. I have the Dot sitting on my daughter's dresser, which is about 5 feet from her closet where I store her cloth diapers. I typically try to tell the Dot to turn her music on from the closet while I am getting her in her night time diaper and it never works. Somehow I keep forgetting and I keep trying and I just continue to be frustrated every night when he doesn't hear me (I come from a family of loud people and I do not mumble...it shouldn't be that difficult to pick up what I'm saying). Her room is otherwise silent so there's no noise interference, either. I have to walk over to the dresser and put my face about 6" from the Dot for him to recognize that I'm speaking to him. I don't really see the point in having this device if the voice recognition feature is useless...The sound is barely audible if there is *any* other noise in the Dot's general vicinity. My 6 year old was horsing around in my kitchen about 2 weeks ago and he knocked my beautiful Echo out of the window and she shattered all over my kitchen tile. The horror of that occasion is a story for another day, however, as I only mentioned it because yesterday I was cleaning and wanted to listen to some festive music at the same time. I lay my head on the Dot where I must put it in order for the da** thing to understand me and asked it to play me a holiday station on Pandora. I started to walk away and realized by the time I got to my daughter's doorway I couldn't hear the music at all in my otherwise quiet home. I walked back and attempted to ask him to turn the volume up, but alas...with the quiet tones of holiday music emanating from his body he couldn't hear me even from half a foot away. I pushed the + button repeatedly until the Dot was at its full sound capacity and walked away again. I went into my bedroom, which is directly around the corner from my daughter's bedroom door, and the music was completely inaudible again. Back to my daughter's doorway I went. I realized that I'd have to stand in her room to hear the music at all. If I had the Dot in my room and wanted to jam to music, even at full volume, I would *definitely* need an external speaker. I don't need my home reverberating from the bass of my Dot the way it does with an Echo at full volume, but I did expect it to be at least USABLE without dishing out the dough for a Bose audio system.All in all, I am honestly disappointed with this purchase. If I wanted something I have to plug into an external speaker to use at all, and be within kissing distance of to activate it, I'd plug my phone into my sound bar and use that (hint: I don't). With the Google Home out now and the potential it has to be much more than the Echo has to offer (not to mention that it's about $40 less), I think I'll forego getting another Echo and try out a different device. I'll always think fondly of Alexa, but I think it's time to move on from the Echo products.

title: Getting there, but perhaps not there yet
body: I grabbed this on impulse when I happened to see it listed the day before it was released. It's a pricepoint I can "play with" for my first gizmo of this sort. I doubt we'll ever pay less than $50 for a wi-fi doodad. So, it's a nifty toy. It has limitations, though. The speaker is better than I expected. Maybe equivalent to an IPhone 7 or an ipad. That's the plus. Now for the longer list of minuses.1) Surely, they're working on a skill for the echo to control the Fire TV products...right?2) The speaker setup is a limitation: I think they really want you to buy the Tap or Echo when it comes to streaming. Plugging in a cable to the 3.5mm jack mutes the onboard audio entirely, and it actually takes several seconds to come back after unplugging a cable. So, one has to have a speaker plugged in and always on, whether that's bluetooth or hardwired. Any speaker that draws so little power that I'd leave it on 24/7 isn't going to offer much improvement over the tablet-like speaker built in. Why isn't there a skill like "Alexa, play through speakers" so I can talk to/listen to Alexa with my stereo (or whatever) off, then choose to listen to music more immersively without having to shuffle cables? Bluetooth seems to be the way to go to combine "casual conversation" with Alexa with listening to radio stations, etc. There should be an option to always play Alexa herself through the built-in speaker.3) On bluetooth, I presume there's no support for APTX, since they'd mention it if it had it, so no CD-quality. Also no pairing with a speaker that requires a pin. On wi-fi, there's no support for 802.11ac.4) I'm deducting a star because the Alexa app doesn't work well with Voiceover on an iphone. App development on Apple products makes accessibility for blind people the default, largely, but Amazon has managed to create a really clunky app in this regard. Taps just stop having an effect, as if the screen contents Voiceover is reporting aren't what's on the screen, perhaps. Kind of a nightmare to manage the device using this app, and it's required. I often have to close the app and restart to regain the ability to navigate the menu.5) "Skills" are buggy. Once you're using the app, you get to see how poorly rated many skills like home automation skills are. Currently, IDevices just doesn't work. I'm sure that's more a matter of IDevices than Amazon, but it's probably shared responsibility.6) I have to speak very loudly for the microphones to hear me over anything at all playing from the builtin speaker, such as news. Plan to hit the Action button to talk to her while things are playing if you don't want to holler.I bought it with the idea of controlling my IDevices switch and socket, plus playing Pandora and radio. Between buggy home automation and the limitations of playing through speakers, I won't be doing either, at least not yet. So, what I have is a talking clock at the moment. Other folks will find it more useful, obviously, and I'm sure the automation will get worked out eventually. Personally, I'm betting that the 5th gen Apple TV will sport a "Hey Siri" microphone array, and then there's Google Home. But at the price, it's hard to say the doodad (do-Dot) won't be worth it. Or, maybe it'll join the ranks of doodads in my drawer that all seemed like good ideas at the time, but which ended up just over-complicating things when they promised to simplify them. We shall see.

title: Not quite what I was hoping for.... but I can see it getting there.
body: So I recently decided to see what I could do with Google Home and Echo Dot. Overall, I like my Dot much better than Home but still was left wanting... And something important to remember is that the service that backs it is a large part of whether these are successful or not, not just the device.My original hope was to have an electronic assistant that I could bark orders to from across the room to setup appointments on my calendar (if you have gmail or outlook/office 365, apple, you're probably fine), potentially integrate with trello (the todo list manager I use - again if you use Todoist, Anydo maybe you're okay).So unfortunately echo fell a little short on what I was hoping. I get that maybe supporting general activesync calendar's (private exchange server) which would allow my calendar to work may not be a mass-market feature and might be something the team gets to in the (near hopefully) future, but as of right now its not there from what I can tell - at least natively with alexa. Just for comparison, Siri on my laptop can do that so in my opinion so should amazon/google home, or at least it shouldn't be far off.As for Trello I guess that's ultimately probably more on the Trello team. Its popular enough that I'd had hoped somebody created a skill for it. As or right now, I don't see one (maybe I'll write one). I was able to get things working though IFTTT where anything that goes on my todo list gets picked up and written to a particular board+list (that I call Todo) in trello. So its a pretty good solution but took me a short bit to figure out.I also like that I can play Amazon Music my current/only streaming service, though I don't often and quality is not great unless you plug it into another speaker I suspect. I like the idea of adding one to the garage/shop though to do this potentially though. And as you know or would guess, the competition's music services don't work from here (Google/Apple Music) - wish we could all "just get along". But Spotify, Pandora, iHeart radio don't seem to be considered the enemies on either platform. Also, I suppose it'd be fun if I had all sorts of automation around the house to dim lights, etc, but alas I don't do that either.Haven't done much with ordering via the Dot but I think one of my main concerns I just discovered today so I'll probably do more with that. I'm not a fan of "just ordering" over the dot unless it something I absolutely know I want to get from amazon, etc. But I just found today that I can tell Alexa to put stuff in my cart which allows me to go in and review after the fact. So very cool and guess you learn something everyday.So overall, cool concept but still needs to grow a bit - at least for my needs. I do think its far more useful than Home. I got Home first and it was back in the box collecting dust even before the Dot arrived. So overall I think Dot is a better platform, but if you're totally invested in all things Google, maybe Home is a better choice. Neither could/would connect to my calendar (I think Alexa had more flexibility in this area IIRC) and w/ Trello I could only get it working with IFTTT on Alexa. For Google Home I had to get Zapier into the mix and overall that solution was just clunkier and more cost than I was looking for - point Alexa.... and as they remind me regularly. Alexa is getting smarter every day, so maybe in time everything I'm looking for will be there.

title: Multi User Functionality is Abysmal
body: I have 2 Echo Dots and a Google Home Mini. I won't do a full blown comparison in this review, but will make some comparisons to justify my rating.On the positive side, the Echo system is fairly versatile, due to the use of hundreds of 3rd party "skills". But that versatility has a significant downside, with most skills requiring an unintuitive and awkward launch command like "trigger X". Google has to build all 3rd party integration into the system manually, rather than having a "store" of skills that you can enable or disable at will. But that means that Google's integrations are much smoother and closer.IFTTT integration is critical for me, and Google's IFTTT integration is undeniably, hands down better. Both allow a custom voice action to trigger any recipe, but Echo only allows one specific command to be given, requires the word "launch" before the command to trigger the recipe, and does not give the option to specify a custom response that the device will give you. Google allows for 3 different variations of your command to be specified, does not require any extra trigger phrase before you're custom command, and allows you to specify a custom response that the device will speak to let you know the command was recognized and run.But the real issue for me is multiuser support. Oh, the Echo devices support multiple users. At least, that's how they advertise. But the implementation is poor, unintuitive, and entirely inconsistent. Let me explain. The Alexa app allows you to set up voice recognition using a long series of training phrases. It allows you to specify other household members and give them full access to the Echo devices, as well as do their own voice recognition training. And it allows each user to access their own Audible library, music library, voice calls, and IFTTT recipes. Sounds great, right? Pretty much the same as Google Home? Just wait.In order to access your own music, you have to make sure there Echo devices you're talking to is "set" to your account. There's no way to know who's account it's set to without asking, so you have to say "who am I". It then identifies who you are based on your voice, and lets you know which account it is currently active. If it's not yours, you have to say "switch accounts". Yes, that's right. It recognizes your voice but is completely incapable of switching to your Amazon account until you tell it to specifically. Once you switch accounts, you can ask it to read your book or play your music. I can't imagine trying to do this with more than 2 household members.To make things more complicated, communications related commands can actually temporarily switch to your account based on voice recognition. If it's set to your spouse's account, and you ask to place a call, it will make the call show up from you, not your wife. So we know switching on the fly based on a recognized voice is absolutely possible. It's just not enabled most of the time. This makes for a janky, inefficient, and inconsistent experience when multiple users are trying to use the device. It wouldn't be so bad for music if the music libraries could be shared between household members, but they cannot. But for audiobooks, it's critical to have more streamlined multiuser support, as I may be listening to the same book as my wife, and our progress needs to be tracked separately.

title: Alexa, order me a Google Home.
body: THE GOOD:Lots of "Skills" (which you can think of as apps) that enable the Echo to do various other things. You can even build your own (if you're willing to write some code) and give the Echo a whole range of new features. There are some great Skills already out there. For instance, if you use OurGroceries on your phones to track your grocery list, you can enable a Skill that links that to the Echo, so you can say "Alexa, tell OurGroceries to add eggs to the Food List." And Alexa will do just that.Additionally, there are built-in features that are pretty good and up to date. I kept asking Alexa what the score was during the Super Bowl, and it knew almost right away when the score had changed.THE BAD:The Echo's ability to understand is very limited. It can comprehend some range of questions with certain key words, but it cannot understand context one bit. You can't ask "What movie won Best Picture at the Oscars?" and then follow up with "Who directed it?" You'll get the answer to the first one, but the second will result with the "Sorry, I don't understand" response that Echo owners know OH SO WELL.Specifically with the Dot, the speaker is not great. I can understand, since it's so cheap ($50), but it's really not great. I keep a Dot in the kitchen (because it's useful there with timers and such), and I can barely hear it about 20 feet away in the living room when watching TV, even at full volume.THE UGLY:"Alexa, stop." "Alexa, stop!" "ALEXA, STOP!!!"If you're an Echo owner, I'm sure you've run into this. When the timer alarm goes off, or the Echo inexplicably begins doing something because it thought it heard its trigger, or it misunderstands what you wanted and begins playing a sample of some music you did not ask for... and you can do nothing to shut it off, even without background noise! It will just keep going until you find yourself with your face 6 inches from a tiny hockey puck-shaped device yelling at the top of your lungs, pleading with it to end the suffering already and STOP.No, "Okay, Google" is not as mellifluous as "Alexa". But I'll tell you what it's also not: COMMON. "Makes", "treks", "plex", "fix a", even the word "breakfast", they can all sound like "Alexa". We've gotten so used to Alexa providing her own commentary at random times throughout the day that we've accepted it as our newfound peanut gallery. Sometimes it's actually funny, but it is never helpful. That super annoying 2 Broke Girls commercial (I hate that show) where she walks in and thinks she's burned 500 calories but then she realizes it's actually the time on her watch (I mean come on, writers)? Yep, Alexa responds like clockwork with the current time. The ironic part is that Alexa can apparently hear the TV perfectly fine, but I can barely hear Alexa from the room where the TV is in. I can't imagine anybody having this thing near a television and NOT changing the trigger word from "Alexa" to something else.And if you try to say what's in the title of this review ("Alexa, order me a Google Home"), Alexa will tell you she can't. I'd consider it a funny joke from the folks at Amazon, but considering what I've seen so far, it's more likely just incapable.

title: Review of the form factor quality, abilities, and capability
body: I am focusing my review on the form factor quality, abilities, and its capability as a voice and speaker conduit for the Alexa service. Alexa changes all the time and, hopefully, gets better. So that is really a different "device" out there in the cloud.The build quality is good but, in the image, the grey top looks lighter then it actually is. It is actually the same black top as the black Dot. So if you are buying it and will cover it with a Dot case, there is no difference for the top from black or white. (The images here are a bit misleading.)The Dot single speaker is the same size as the tweeter on the Echo 2 (which also has a bigger woofer). So the Dot audio is akin to a 1960s transistor radio, but a bit or, a lot better. The down firing speaker is probably much of its problem. The Google Home points up and sounds a bit better. But don't buy either of these based on sound! Well, for background music in a pinch while at your home office desk, it is fine, even very good, for mono.If you already own a better sounder or bigger powered speaker or clock radio that can take a 3.5 mm aux plug or has Bluetooth, you should use that for the audio or, buy the Echo 2 (which contrary to some reviews, has pretty good sound, again, not stereo but, pretty good—if over bass amplified).But, keep in mind, the Dot needs to be 3 feet away from any external speaker you have it playing to. Make sure your mini jack line is 5 or more feet long (but not too long, as signal loss may occur). With Bluetooth, also keep it some distance, which is easier since its range is fine for a good number of feet. Ideally, you could have the dot in the center or near center of a room (or just away from a wall, not against it) and have the speaker on a table against the wall in another part of the room. In an apartment, you could have the Dot on the breakfast bar height counter and have the Bluetooth speaker/receiver 5 or more feet from it in the living room. Yep, you could also hook up a little Bluetooth receiver to your AV receiver into its RCA audio inputs via a mini jack to RCA splinter and do it that way too.I plugged my dot into an 8 year old Sony stereo digital clock radio (the big beefy one with HD Radio that also used to take an iPod on top, it has a good amp in it). I told it to play various stations already in the Sony to compare audio. I don't listen to many HD station so, it was mostly just FM compared to its TuneIn equivalent. At first, the sound was really low, even with the clock radio volume up pretty high. So I told Alexa to increase the volume to 10. I think 10 is the highest. That helped a lot. But it is still pretty weak compared to the same station direct OTA.When I first set up the Dot, it asked if I would use Bluetooth, a wire, or it speaker (default) for audio. I skipped that, thinking I could change it later. Since then, I have looked and looked in the Alexa App and it is not there. I suspect this setting needs to be adjusted for the wire option to work correctly. There is no reason to increase volume to 10 just to hear decent sound. Amazon: Where is there speaker choice in the app?!I will add more to the review as I learn more about the hardware.Alexa itself is a more complex thing and, needs its own rating and review.

title: A fantastic device, except for a few flaws.
body: The Echo Dot is a great device, but as an original Echo owner I have to knock it down a star. I love that Dot can practically be dropped anywhere in a room due to its size (and for the price it's affordable to do so), and that it doesn't have a power light anywhere on it so it's perfect for a bedroom (the original has a white power light on the back of it; practically unnoticeable but in a dark room...). Both devices use the same Alexa voice service, so they both excel at the same areas and have the same limitations and quirks. However, I pre-ordered a six pack of Dots and the two I've opened so far have the same issues - even after running voice training four times on each device.First, the original Echo seems to wake and listen much more effectively than Dot. I moved my original Echo from the bedroom to the kitchen and put a Dot near my bed. Dot will often misunderstand me, even with statements that original was astoundingly good at. For example, "Alexa, turn off the tall living room lamp," with original would be correct every time, while Dot will sometimes misinterpret "off" for "on". A lot of times it won't wake at all where the original Echo would.Second, the audio quality and volume just doesn't seem to be there. I understand this thing is smaller than an alarm clock so, really, what should I expect, but at 75% volume it's equivalent to my phone at 50%.Third, there seems to be some incompatibility with certain skills. My favorite is the Jeopardy game and I play it daily. With the original Echo, each time was like being on a TV commercial for the device. With Dot, however, it usually asks the first question and immediately tells me I'm wrong. I've found if I answer very quickly she will sometimes accept my answer, but even then the performance is oddly slow. Original Echo was so fast it would be nearly conversational, even when interpreting words like "croissant" during the game, but with Dot the light ring will disco for several excruciating seconds before either telling me I'm wrong (and then reading out the answer I had just stated) or that she's having trouble connecting to Jeopardy.Lastly, I like to have the "start" and "end" sounds turned on so that I can just start talking to Alexa and not have to look at the light ring to ensure she's listening. Basically this means that the device will make a small tone when it's actively listening, and another when it's stopped listening. On the original Echo this works perfectly well, but the Dot, after some research, I've discovered will typically hear its own "start" tone while it's trying to listen to me. Since she can't hear me over the tone for the first bit of the recording, she can't figure out what I'm talking about and cause whatever command I've issued to fail. Compared to original Echo, this might be because the speakers on Dot are so much closer to the microphone, or because it uses a higher-pitched and longer tone for "start".I wouldn't say that people should avoid buying this as I'm sure Amazon will correct for these flaws over time with software updates, but for the moment, if you want the most solid Echo experience, go with the original.

title: Good start but the mic and listening interpretation should be improved
body: Very good in general. Really nice if you put in the effort to install home automation devices for lighting and hvac.But here are a few caveats:-Playing TV nearby will trigger Alexa to start listening occasionally.-The mic is probably not good enough to accurately hear you across a room unless it is absolutely quite. I would suggest placing this in a central location in the room.-You'll need to tell Alexa to stop if it's playing music before issuing new commands, as the music lyrics will be picked up by Alexa.-Basically any sound after you say "Alexa" can be interpreted as a command that means background noise, family/guest conversations, and tv/radio can give you some miss fires.-Training is available but doesn't seem to help with above problems. Speech recognition gets better, but it's not distinguishing who is speaking.Tips:-Once you understand these caveats, you can better position the device. I'd suggest placing it away from a tv or speaker set and as close to the center of a room as possible, but not near a dinner table or sectional couch (avoid loud conversation spaces).-Plug in your speaker by an aux cord (I'm using a bose mini sound link). Don't pair with bluetooth, unless you don't mind getting up to turn on the speaker and then asking alexa to pair every time. As with most bluetooth speakers, the mini sound link will power off after some idle time. If I have to get up, I might as well just get my smartphone which is then much more convenient than trying to say the right thing to Alexa. But keep in mind the audio from the speaker can trigger Alexa commands so position it wisely.-Keep one dot in the bathroom, it's really great for music, radio, sports, traffic, and weather while you shower and get ready.-Get TPLink smart plug, smart light switches, and dimmable/color bulbs and set up several categories and themes in Kasa app and Alexa app. IMO that is really the most killer Alexa feature. You can adjust all your lighting, dimming, colors etc with a single command.-Has spotify integration, which works alright. I like iHeartRadio as well. But other than that you'll have to get to know and love Amazon's music offering if you want to use Alexa as your personal DJ. I personally have never used Amazon's music service before this and I can't speak to it's catalog or how soon it gets new releases. Important to look at these services beforehand if that is important to you.-Nest integration is pretty cool, but if you have a nest you'll rarely need to adjust the temp manually. That's the whole point of having a nest. So this is more of a novelty. But it is nice to tell it when you're leaving to put nest in an eco mode.-IMO most of the other 'skills' are novelty and have very little practical use.-Enable purchase control! Those alexa deals are tempting but I had an accidental purchase caused by tv noise. I had to cancel later on that one.-Smartplug+Electric Projector Screen+Echo Dot!It would be great if it worked with specific people's voices and reduced some of these false triggers. Let's see if they hear us and implement that in the 3rd gen or with software changes.

title: This thing is as dumb as rock
body: Not really ready for Prime Time.CONS: Although it can understand your voice commands (about 60% of the time). This thing is as dumb as rock. What I mean by this is it has very poor AI, it doesn't understand context, doesn't ask follow up questions or if it senses a low probability that it has the correct answer say something like "I think you are asking for .... ... is this correct?" - come on that last one is really basic!It also does not learn when you do the same task over again. By that I mean I have installed a bible App and it cannot understand "Alexa use Bible App to Read John Chapter 3" or "Read Bible John Chapter 3" - it replies with "Chinese version of Bi Belee shapper not available in your Kindle Library" or "I have a skill for that - do you want to add a new Bible skill?" or similar response. It's totally frustrating every time you want it to do something it cannot figure out something as simple as a bible app has already been installed - maybe ask to use that rather than suggesting to install a totally different bible skill. Any half decent AI would respond with "Are you trying to read the bible?" Y/N ... "In future would you like to use the installed bible app to do this?" Y/N and then respond - "OK in future say 'Alexa read BibleApp Book, Chapter, Verse' and I will read that part of the bible".Also Home Control is weak - I think smart home manufacturers are just adding the absolute minimum Alexa integration just so they can claim it is compatible with the Amazon Echo/Dot. For example I have the Logitech Harmony home entertainment system and literally all you can do with the Alexa integration is either switch the TV system 'on' or 'off'. Not possible to say watch NBC or go to channel 7.1 or show my recorded TV shows etc all of which I can do with my phone or the Harmony remote. So to do anything you have to pick up the Harmony remote - so I might as well power on the TV with the remote as well - rather than have Alexa say "I did not understand that?" - Arghhh!!! Similarly the Nest and Ecobee smart home Thermostats have bare minimum controls through Alexa compared with the respective phone apps.PROS: For basic tasks it works great - news briefing, telling the weather etc. Anything that is a simple common task where Amazon have obviously programmed in numerous ways of saying the same thing like "what is the weather", "give me the weather forecast", "tell me today's weather" etc... A device like this would be extremely useful for someone who is blind or who is working with their hands a lot and cannot pick up a phone easily.CONCLUSION: Even at the discount price of $40 this is not worth the aggravation. Maybe in a few years these types of devices will be much improved - it reminds me of smart phones before Apple bought out the iPhone and showed everyone how to make a smartphone that was 'truly' useful and user friendly. Don't believe the hype of the slick TV commercials for this product - I bet they were on 'take 97' before they finally got Alexa to understand they wanted to order a Dominoes pizza!

title: Still needs a lot more advanced programming but kinda cool.
body: Well, like Siri, Alexa can, at times be annoying. So far I have not really found anything worthwhile for Alexa to do except turn off lights, play music (which can go horribly wrong!), occasionally set an alarm (that I can do on my iPhone,) tell me the weather (that I already have on my iPhone) and notifications from the Washington Post (that I've already received on my iPhone.) I don't really see that much usefulness for it. Yet.If you ask Alexa a question, most of the time she does not know the answer but hopefully programmers will fix this soon. She has many "skills" but if you don't remember the exact name of the skill, you have to look it up in the Alexa app on your phone which kind of defeats the point of having a voice activated AI device...Now the music! OMG! If you ask for, let's say music from the 80's, it will play a bunch of songs you have never heard of in your life and I was around in the 80's and know almost all popular songs. Tip: If you have Amazon Prime, create playlists within the app of your personal favorites in whatever genre and ask Alexa to play or shuffle your playlist. Otherwise you're just going to get crap. Also if you request to hear a certain album, she will only play a sample unless you specify play FULL album. If there's a way to get her to play from your Apple Music playlist, I haven't found it yet. If you have Amazon Music Unlimited, things are probably better musically, but I don't.The whole Alexa system really tries to keep you in their Amazon ecosystem, so if you have Prime, Unlimited Music, and Audible, it's probably much more worthwhile. I would not trust shopping through Alexa, so I haven't tried it yet.I find myself screaming at Alexa a lot, like a bad dog "Alexa stop!" If I pause for one second while requesting a song or playlist, she decides on her own what to play which has nothing to do with what I wanted. When I requested upbeat Christmas songs, I got slow depressing songs I never heard of about Jesus, when I just wanted to hear Rudolph the Red Nose Reindeer or something so, again, best to create your own playlists in Prime Music...As for the Dot itself, I have one in the bedroom inserted into a Bluetooth speaker which is great, and another in the bathroom to listen to music while I'm getting ready. It would be great if there was a way to hang it on a wall as a sort of bathroom speaker, I might try attaching Velcro. Also, it would be great if it didn't need to be plugged in, if it had a rechargeable battery, but that's probably in the works.All in all, I have not found Alexa that helpful and it often seems silly to request her to do things I could do just as quickly myself. Hopefully programmers will continue to educate her and she will be able to answer more questions and be more helpful. I think what I really need is a full AI robot, something that could clean my apartment or carry up groceries, not a Dot.But if the Amazon programmers continue to develop her AI, maybe eventually, like in the movie "Her," she will be smarter than all of us and move on eventually to a brighter spot in cyberspace...

title: For a smart device, It's not very smart.
body: I mean, it's great for asking about weather (temperature), sports, news, playing some music, setting timers and alarms, some calculations and conversions, and some of the skills are great, but it's just not very intuitive:1. When I ask about weather, it'll tell me the temperature and if it's sunny / rainy / whatever. If I say "Weather in Celsius" (I use Fahrenheit) or "Humidity," it treats what I say like I just asked about the weather.2. "Wake me up in 15 minutes" makes it set a 15 minute timer instead of setting an alarm 15 minutes after I asked for an alarm to be set. Timers aren't as loud as alarms, so it's not a good thing to wake up with. It's a little nitpicky, but my phone can do it, so I've just been using my phone instead.3. Sometimes it's a little hard of hearing. I'll say "Alexa, weather" and it'll light up when I call her, but will just ignore whatever I say after. This has happened when I was a little farther away from it (around 3 feet, but with nothing else making noise in my room), and it has happened when I was less than a foot away from it (it's on my desk, and it sometimes can't hear me when I'm actually sitting on my desk).There's also sometimes a disconnect with regards to how she thinks I talk, which is weird since I don't really have any distinct accent, so I sometimes have to try out different pronunciations to see what she understands, and those don't work all the time either. It's a frustration of mine if I want her to play something from Spotify, but names in general are weird so I'll let that slide4. Sometimes I'll ask something, then I'll ask a follow-up question right after it and she just doesn't connect the dots:"Who played Belle in the Beauty and the Beast Movie?""In Beauty and the Beast, Belle was played by Emma Watson""What are her other movies""Hmm, I can't find an answer to the question I've heard""What are Emma Watson's other movies""Here are a few movies playing near you today..."??????Also:"Play Art Angels on Spotify""Playing Art Angels by Grimes on Spotify""Play more songs by Grimes / Play similar songs""Artangels by Grimes""Play a different album""Sorry, I didn't get that" (I've said it different ways and she still gives me this answer."Play a different album by this artist""Here's an album you might like: The Black Parade by My Chemical Romance"??????It's a little frustrating, but at least I know what I can and can't ask her. I'd probably still buy this regardless since I extensively use some of her features and she's still pretty convenient to use when I'm too lazy to fumble through my phone and look at the weather report for the day or the week / set a timer (for tea / ramen) / get scores for sports so I can basically sympathize / celebrate with the people in my life who watch sports / make to-do lists as i think about it / play music / listen to audiobooks.

title: Google Home mini is much better !
body: I bought the Google home mini and the Echo Dot at the same time. The google home mini is much better for most of my use cases.Here is a comparison between the two.Sound Quality :Google home mini is much better in sound quality. the sound clarity and bass is really good on the google mini. the echo dot is quite tinny.External speakers or soundbars:the echo can connect to external speakers via bluetooth or audio jack. The google mini needs the speakers to have chromecast device connected.Having bluetooth is really useful as i can easily play songs on my Oontz angle 3 ultra, but the speaker/soundbar has to be turned ON manually and the input set to Bluetooth before the music can be streamed.I have chromecast connected to my TV and with the google home , i can ask it to play songs on my TV. It will automatically turn on my TV (HDMI-CEC) and the music will be played on my soundbar from the TV. this is truly hands-free !!In addition, you can ask it to play shows/movies from Netflix or stream videos from Youtube on the TV and when done, you can ask it to turn off the TV, all with just your voice and no remote. You can even cast photos from your Google photos to TV with voice.Music Streaming services :the Echo uses Amazon prime music by default to stream music. the songs are very limited here and you would need subscription to either Amazon music unlimited or Spotify Premium. It does not support free version of spotify at all. it does have Pandora, iheartradio, tunein, sirius XM.Google home supports free versions of Spotify, Google play music and Pandora. you can play your playlists or radio stations on the free version of spotify.Smart home automation:I have the Philips Hue bulbs and it is very frustrating to use it with Alexa. It keeps rediscovering the lights and each light shows up twice under devices. so when i ask alexa to turn on/off a light, it says there are multiple devices. every time i have to delete the extra device or make it "forget" the device from web app. In addition everything on alexa needs a skill and has to be enabled before using.The google home setup and usability is much better for connected devices.Calls:Setting up the echo to use my phone number to make calls was frustrating, since me and my wife share amazon prime account.For google home, you need to export all contacts to google contacts before you can make calls. It can call local businesses also via google search.Routines/Shortcuts:Alexa has good support for routines, where you can get multiple tasks done with single command. Google home now supports 2 tasks with one command with shortcutsThe search functionality and contextual results are better on Google home, but the echo works just fine for news,weather,traffic.. i would recommend the Google home mini over echo dot, especially if you have chromecast connected to TV/speakers.for just bluetooth speakers, echo will be better, but the music services are very limited.

title: We're Working On Our Relationship
body: Echo Dot (Gen2) is the home of Alexa, an intelligent (as opposed to unintelligent) personal assistant (IPA) developed by Amazon Lab 126, where they reportedly also look for signs of intelligent life on Earth. Having co-habitated with Alexa for about a month, I’d have to say our relationship is off and on, but more off than on.Several times each day I panic that Alexa has died. Fortunately, a reboot generally addresses that fear. Then there’s that nagging issue of Alexa repeatedly informing me that my Echo Dot has lost its connection with the internet—even though every other connected device in my home is alive and well.As with most nascent relationships, Alexa and I seem to have a problem communicating. I say one thing and she hears something entirely different. For example, I asked her to play ‘La califfa’ by Ennio Morricone. After searching her vast musical library (one of the things that first attracted me), she announced she couldn’t find a song called, ‘The Cauliflower.” OK, I get it, Italian isn’t her strong suit; however, she had to make it personal by asking (she says ‘suggesting’) that I speak more clearly—or something to that effect. And I swear she impudently corrected my pronunciation on more than one occasion.Then there was the time when I simply asked if she thought it was going to rain. She informed me that she couldn’t find any brain candy. I’ll admit to slurring a word or two on occasion, but I find it hard to believe that anyone would interpret ‘Is it going to rain today?” into a request for brain candy.Eventually, all of this started happening with such regularity that I began questioning whether our relationship would last. Then I remembered—she’s only 3—give the girl a break. So, for now, we’re taking it day by day and I’m making an effort to speak more clearly.UPDATE: 1/9/18 I'm sorry to report that the relationship is in a steady decline. When it comes to music, Alexa is very narrow-minded. Unless you request a song or CD precisely the way it's listed on the Amazon website, she's going to tell you she can't find that. For example, if you want to listen to Wicked, you have to research Amazon to determine that only version available for streaming is Wicked by various artists. Unless you ask for it in precisely those terms (and don't leave out the 'by') Alexa will tell you she can't find it. Ask for something more complicated, like Madam Butterfly by Renata Tibaldi & The Santa Cecillia Orchestra, you might as well go read a good book instead. Ask her to play Barbra Streisand and you'll get nothing but duets, and all too often, songs will be repeated two and even three times before you hear something new. If you want an entire album and not just the title song, be sure to start with: play the cd (insert title). Complicating this annoying pedantic behavior, Alexa is often "unable to connect to the network." I guess this is when she's out shopping with my first wife.

title: Integration of Echo Dot into Home Automation and whole home audio solved
body: It's getting there. Perhaps in a couple years and a few generations it will really work well.Cons: BlueTooth is NOT a good protocol for music, Low bandwidth and muddy audio is very noticeable on HiFi systems. Only other audio output is the 3.5mm line. AirPlay or DNLA should be an option. With AirPlay you get quality audio to any AirPlay device or all (Whole House Audio).Home Automation: Doesn't work with Vera Mios HA controller. Vera is the most versatile for under $150 and supports the most devices unlike Wink, SmartThings or Insteon. If you want to use Echo with Vera you need the HA-Bridge server. You can run this server on a RaspberryPi. At least then you can control your lights. lock doors, control scenes etc...Thermostats: Adjusts temp on EcoBee and Nest. Can't read home temp or humidity. Alexa, what is Ecobee's room temp? Doesn't work. you can only adjust settings.Security systems: Basically non existent integration for quality systems like DSC. I have a DSC with internet access, remote control, and fully integrated into Vera HA. Only thing I can get Echo to do is Arm it through the HA-Bridge. Can't read any sensor status.Home Theater: It can control the FireTV some. That's a start. It can control JRiver media center. But only the music. Not XBMC or Plex. There are developers working on XBMC and Plex though. XBMC / Plex and AirPlay are my top two wish list items.Pros: It's entertaining. It can look up info for you. Play audio from various streaming services. Add to a shopping list, order things, It does work as described. Voice control is really good. We like it and for $50 it's a steal. Buy a 6 pack for Christmas gifts.Update: 12/7/2016 The best option for audio quality is using the stereo jack. I'm working on a way to take the line out into a USB sound card with ASIO drivers to allow connection to DNLA or Airplay speakers. I guess the only other option is to jack in the audio out to speakers in each room and link multiple Dots. that might be a better a cheaper option for Whole House audio.Update: 12/10/2016 You can't link all the Echo's to play the same thing at the same time. So, I came up with a solution for whole house quality audio over Wifi. Get a behringer UCA222 USB line-in adapter. Use the ASIO drivers. Connect the Echo Dot to the line. Download and install a utility called TuneBlade for Windows OS. TuneBlade can take the USB audio input and play it over Wifi to any and all AirPlay receivers with a low latency of only 500ms. It's also CD quality. You can then control TuneBlade from your IOS device for where to play and how loud. I'm sure you can do this with a Mac and you won't need TuneBlade since AirPlay is built in. Then subscribe to Amazon Music Unlimited and you got voice controlled CD quality whole house over Wifi.

title: Love the device, Amateur hour at Amazon
body: The Echo Plus is an awesome piece of equipment, I absolutely love it!However, Amazon is PURPOSELY vague/deceptive on the capability of their equipment/software. For example, the site specifically states the hub is a ZigBee hub, yet it DOES NOT specifically state the hub is NOT AT ALL compatible with Z-Wave. This is fully deceptive marketing, just state what your product CAN NOT DO!!!!!!!!!!!Also, Z-WAVE device advertisements on Amazon flat out state they are fully compatible with Alexa, this is FLAT OUT FALSE, and I consider this to be and industry wide marketing LIE!!!!!!! You CAN NOT connect Z-WAVE devices to this hub, no matter what you read, Amazon will not tell you this unless you buy the hub, connect it, trouble shoot, give up and call them. Then they say "oh... looks like the hub is NOT compatible with you Z-WAVE devices, we can allow you to return all the Z-Wave equipment so you can buy more expensive ZigBee products (which probably contain Chinese hack Software). Rediclous, just be HONEST and tell your customers what your device can an can not do, otherwise you are a bunch of big fat ADVERTISING LIARS.With that said, to connect Z-WAVE to the Alexa hub, you have to buy a separate Z-WAVE hub, something their tech support will not tell you. Oh, also, don't trust the FAQ's/help page on the App, they suggest it is simple and you just find you device and the hub (which everyone thinks they just bought a hub because they have the echo plus) but it is %100 WRONG. You will not know the GE Z-Wave instructions are flat out FALSE until you talk to customer service and they act like you should have known you need a different hub.One other thing that is irritating is that you have to wipe Amazon devices back to factory just to update your WiFi password. If you follow the instructions, it will say this is a last resort. WRONG WRONG WRONG!!!!! Not only is this wrong, but I had to teach Amazon that you have to reset their equipment not once, but twice to get it to work. Then I had to do the full setup again. To be fair, this applies to their devices which have screens.The last comment I have is the Alexa App, it has to be the least professional App, least intuitive App, most difficult App on the planet. It is a TURD. Why do you not have the same capability on the App within no screen devices for devices which have screens? Basically you can not control/setup screen devices using the app. THIS IS JUST STUPID!!!!!Advise to Amazon:.Why ruin such an amazing product by supplying a crappy user interface and false/deceptive advertising? Especially the crappy home automation directions, and hub connections? When you read these instructions, they are FULLY deceptive.

title: Please, Amazon, Make It Smarter
body: The Echo Dot has been out for over a year and I am underwhelmed at how it performs. I enjoy listening to my music, but I shouldn't have to access my Amazon Music account through my phone. I should be able to tell Alexa to play a certain playlist and have it played. When I ask it for a fact, it references Wikipedia. That is the worst reference point on the internet. That is user created content. There is no fact backing the relevance of the topics. It is basically a book report by various students on various topics. Webster's, Britannica, Encyclopedia.com are all better reference sites. I have to keep saying "Alexa" for every follow up question. The apps available seem to be for fun and not purpose. Finding keys/phone. keeping a list, alarm/timers and news are the only useful apps I've seen. Most are jokes, trivia or games. It still gets the city wrong for the weather despite me trying to fix it four times. I use my account and it picks a city 70 miles from me. I have to mention my city every time I need to hear the weather. I had a cold and it kept misunderstanding my requests.Amazon, this is your product. Put up some apps for this device. I want to be able to hear an entire recipe without needing to see it on my phone. If I wanted to see stuff displayed on my phone I would look it up on Google. I know this sounds picky, but all the problems I mentioned are available on a Google Home. I got the Dot as a Christmas present. I can mention a part of the song on Home and it plays it. I can mispronounce a word and it understands me. I can ask it for a recipe and it reads it all. You are dropping Youtube from the Fire Stick because Google is dropping Amazon Video from the Chromecast. Right now, they have the edge. I could review their device, but you no longer sell it here. Right now, I mainly use the Echo Dot for an alarm clock and a memo pad. You are getting by on your name and that only lasts so long before people realize that there is something better, cheaper and more reliable. I was hoping for much more. I don't want to buy more stuff just to get the simple device to work better. I know all about the features of the Show and they are nice, but I just want to hear it read to me without asking if I want to hear more. When I want it to stop I'll say "Alexa, stop!"I am keeping the Dot. I'm not giving it up. I have confidence that Amazon will come through. They make a great tablet, media device and have the best people working here. They have always been understanding when I had an issue. But I feel it necessary to show the flaws so you don't get passed up when Apple comes out with their device this year.

title: Can't stream Amazon Music on more than one unit at a time & moving features to paid subscriptions...
body: I have been using these units for about 3 years. I've had Amazon Prime longer. I am becoming increasingly frustrated with Amazon with their policies and features. Granted - they do add more features all the time - as far as skills, but in home automation and being able to take advantage of device properties to develope sophisticated routines - they are poor there and seem to be making little to no progress there. But here today I discovered something new. I stream music at home for my dogs when I and my wife leave for work to help them feel less lonely. Then I have another dot at my office where I tend to listen to talk radio. I have frequently noticed when I get home, that the music playing for the dogs has stopped. Today, however, I decided to listen to some music at work myself. So I asked Alexa to play relaxing music. It tells me that it is going to play some station and then immediately states that Amazon Music is streaming to another device and wants to know if I want to listen to Amazon Music on this device instead!?! So, maybe with the talk radio station it never asked and just switched them without saying anything - thus why the music is not playing when we get home most days? Here is a link to this Amazon policy: https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html?nodeId=GVQSB3QARNU9AQ78This is absolutely ridiculous! I have a 3rd of these units as well. We have been working on home renovations (MAJOR) and I was planning to put the other one upstairs and maybe even another in our daughter's room so we could use them for alarms, listen to music, etc... WELL - I guess not - apparently, if my wife or I want to be in the upstairs bedroom and the other is downstairs - we can't both be listening to different music at the same time - nor can my daughter. Not to mention the fact that songs we used to be able to play no longer will without adding a subscription to Amazon Music Unlimited, and that they have raised their Prime Membership fees by $30 per year (while reducing my services), and have raised the cost of one-day shipping compared to what it was initially, and so on. I'm getting more and more aggravated by the lack of new innovation with the Alexa units, higher subscription costs, cutting of services and moving them into additional subscriptions that must be added to get the same functionality back... I'm beginning to seriously consider moving my smart devices to a LAN based management system, and firing Amazon/Alexa. I even called customer service to complain and got someone that seemed to not have any idea what I was talking about and that tried to transfer me to Amazon Music Unlimited support and hung up on me by mistake. :( Not happy....

title: I want to love it, but it has issues
body: I really want to like this unit, but so far the jury is still out. Overall it's a cool device to have around, but there seem to be bugs in the system that keep it from being something that I use on a regular basis.I am impressed on how responsive the unit is and how well it understands commands from anywhere in the room. It will even understand my 3 year old who hasn't quite understood that Alexa isn't a real person trapped in a tiny plastic puck and needs to be spoken to a little slower than normal.Response and accuracy are very good.Bluetooth connection to my soundbar is fast and reliable.Network connection is mostly reliable....had a few unexplained dropouts that required a restart, but I can live with that.Setup through the app is pretty fast and pain free.Audio quality is as good as you can expect for streamed bluetooth sound.My biggest beef is the constant audio dropouts when playing music. The worst offender that I've found is when playing from Prime Music, which would be my go-to source if not for this.Every time it starts playback, it plays for a couple of seconds and the drops. *Usually* it starts back up within a few more seconds and will sometimes play a song or two before dropping again and starting back.
